# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
*State and explain the three axioms that a function $\langle \cdot \vert \cdot \rangle$ must satisfy to qualify as an inner product on a complex vector space.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three axioms an inner product must satisfy on a complex vector space $V$ are:

**(1) Conjugate Symmetry:** For all $|u\rangle, |v\rangle \in V$, $\langle v \vert u \rangle = \langle u \vert v \rangle^{*}$. The order reversal introduces a complex conjugate. (1 mark)

**(2) Linearity in the First Argument:** For all $|u\rangle, |v\rangle, |w\rangle \in V$ and all scalars $a, b \in \mathbb{C}$, $\langle a u + b w \vert v \rangle = a \langle u \vert v \rangle + b \langle w \vert v \rangle$. Note that linearity in the *first* argument implies *conjugate-linearity* in the second. (1 mark)

**(3) Positive Definiteness:** For all $|v\rangle \in V$, $\langle v \vert v \rangle \geq 0$ (a real non-negative number), and $\langle v \vert v \rangle = 0$ if and only if $|v\rangle = \mathbf{0}$ (the zero vector). (1 mark)

Any function satisfying all three is a valid inner product; the pair $(V, \langle \cdot \vert \cdot \rangle)$ is called an **inner product space**.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model Question]**
*Define the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality for an inner product space. Discuss when equality holds.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** For any two vectors $|u\rangle, |v\rangle$ belonging to an inner product space, the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** states that:
$$\vert \langle u \vert v \rangle \vert \leq \lVert u \rVert \, \lVert v \rVert$$
where $\lVert u \rVert = \sqrt{\langle u \vert u \rangle}$ and $\lVert v \rVert = \sqrt{\langle v \vert v \rangle}$ are the norms induced by the inner product. (2 marks)

**Equality condition:** Equality $\vert \langle u \vert v \rangle \vert = \lVert u \rVert \, \lVert v \rVert$ holds **if and only if** the two vectors are **linearly dependent**, i.e., there exists a scalar $\lambda \in \mathbb{C}$ such that $|u\rangle = \lambda |v\rangle$ (or one of the vectors is the zero vector). (1 mark)

**Significance:** In quantum computing, this inequality is the mathematical guarantee that the **Born rule probability** $\vert \langle \phi \vert \psi \rangle \vert^{2} \leq 1$, ensuring measurement probabilities remain physically valid.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** Define an inner product space and verify that the operation $\langle u \vert v \rangle = 2 u_1^{*} v_1 + 3 u_2^{*} v_2$ is a valid inner product on $\mathbb{C}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the vectors $|u\rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2i \end{pmatrix}$ and $|v\rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1+i \end{pmatrix}$, compute:
  (i) $\langle u \vert v \rangle$ and $\langle v \vert u \rangle$
  (ii) The norms $\lVert u \rVert$ and $\lVert v \rVert$
  (iii) The Cauchy–Schwarz bound and verify it holds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Definition and Verification (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** An *inner product space* is a complex vector space $V$ equipped with a function $\langle \cdot \vert \cdot \rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{C}$ that satisfies:
1. Conjugate symmetry: $\langle v \vert u \rangle = \langle u \vert v \rangle^{*}$
2. Linearity in the first argument: $\langle a u + b w \vert v \rangle = a \langle u \vert v \rangle + b \langle w \vert v \rangle$
3. Positive definiteness: $\langle v \vert v \rangle \geq 0$, with equality iff $|v\rangle = \mathbf{0}$.

**Verification of Axiom 1 — Conjugate Symmetry (2 marks):**

$$\langle u \vert v \rangle = 2 u_1^{*} v_1 + 3 u_2^{*} v_2$$

$$\langle v \vert u \rangle = 2 v_1^{*} u_1 + 3 v_2^{*} u_2$$

$$\langle u \vert v \rangle^{*} = (2 u_1^{*} v_1)^{*} + (3 u_2^{*} v_2)^{*} = 2 (u_1^{*})^{*} (v_1)^{*} + 3 (u_2^{*})^{*} (v_2)^{*} = 2 v_1^{*} u_1 + 3 v_2^{*} u_2 = \langle v \vert u \rangle \checkmark$$

**Verification of Axiom 2 — Linearity (1.5 marks):**

$$\langle a u + b w \vert v \rangle = 2 (a u_1 + b w_1)^{*} v_1 + 3 (a u_2 + b w_2)^{*} v_2$$

$$= 2 (a^{*} u_1^{*} + b^{*} w_1^{*}) v_1 + 3 (a^{*} u_2^{*} + b^{*} w_2^{*}) v_2$$

$$= a(2 u_1^{*} v_1) + b(2 w_1^{*} v_1) + a(3 u_2^{*} v_2) + b(3 w_2^{*} v_2)$$

$$= a(2 u_1^{*} v_1 + 3 u_2^{*} v_2) + b(2 w_1^{*} v_1 + 3 w_2^{*} v_2) = a \langle u \vert v \rangle + b \langle w \vert v \rangle \checkmark$$

**Verification of Axiom 3 — Positive Definiteness (1.5 marks):**

$$\langle u \vert u \rangle = 2 \vert u_1 \vert^{2} + 3 \vert u_2 \vert^{2}$$

Since $2 > 0$ and $3 > 0$ and $\vert u_i \vert^{2} \geq 0$, the sum is non-negative. Equality $\langle u \vert u \rangle = 0$ requires $\vert u_1 \vert^{2} = 0$ and $\vert u_2 \vert^{2} = 0$, i.e., $u_1 = u_2 = 0$, so $|u\rangle = \mathbf{0}$. ✓

**Conclusion:** The operation is a valid inner product.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 marks):**

**(i) Inner Products (2 marks):**

Form the bras: $\langle u \vert = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -2i \end{pmatrix}$ and $\langle v \vert = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1-i \end{pmatrix}$

$$\langle u \vert v \rangle = (1)(3) + (-2i)(1+i) = 3 - 2i - 2i^{2} = 3 - 2i + 2 = 5 - 2i$$

$$\langle v \vert u \rangle = (3)(1) + (1-i)(2i) = 3 + 2i - 2i^{2} = 3 + 2i + 2 = 5 + 2i$$

**Check Axiom 1:** $\langle u \vert v \rangle^{*} = (5 - 2i)^{*} = 5 + 2i = \langle v \vert u \rangle$ ✓

**(ii) Norms (2 marks):**

$$\lVert u \rVert = \sqrt{\langle u \vert u \rangle} = \sqrt{(1)(1) + (-2i)(2i)} = \sqrt{1 - 4i^{2}} = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5}$$

$$\lVert v \rVert = \sqrt{\langle v \vert v \rangle} = \sqrt{(3)(3) + (1-i)(1+i)} = \sqrt{9 + 1 - i^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt{11}$$

**(iii) Cauchy–Schwarz Verification (3 marks):**

$$\text{LHS} = \vert \langle u \vert v \rangle \vert = \vert 5 - 2i \vert = \sqrt{25 + 4} = \sqrt{29}$$

$$\text{RHS} = \lVert u \rVert \lVert v \rVert = \sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{11} = \sqrt{55}$$

**Comparison:** $\sqrt{29}$ vs $\sqrt{55}$. Squaring: $29 \leq 55$ ✓

$$\boxed{\sqrt{29} \leq \sqrt{55} \quad \checkmark}$$

Equality does not hold because $|u\rangle = \lambda |v\rangle$ would require $1 = 3\lambda$ and $2i = (1+i)\lambda$ simultaneously, which has no solution.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model Question]**
**(a)** State and prove the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality for an inner product space. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the function space $L^2([0, \pi/2])$ with inner product $\langle f \vert g \rangle = \int_0^{\pi/2} f(x) g(x) \, dx$. Compute $\langle \sin x \vert \cos x \rangle$ and $\lVert \sin x \rVert$. Are $\sin x$ and $\cos x$ orthogonal? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Cauchy–Schwarz Proof (7 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** For all $|u\rangle, |v\rangle$ in an inner product space, $\vert \langle u \vert v \rangle \vert \leq \lVert u \rVert \, \lVert v \rVert$, with equality iff $|u\rangle = \lambda |v\rangle$ for some $\lambda \in \mathbb{C}$.

**Proof (6 marks):**

*Case 1:* If $|v\rangle = \mathbf{0}$, then $\langle u \vert v \rangle = 0$ and $\lVert v \rVert = 0$, giving $0 \leq 0$ — holds trivially. (1 mark)

*Case 2:* Assume $|v\rangle \neq \mathbf{0}$. For any $t \in \mathbb{R}$, by positive definiteness: (1 mark)

$$f(t) = \langle u - t v \vert u - t v \rangle \geq 0$$

Expanding using linearity and conjugate symmetry: (1 mark)

$$f(t) = \langle u \vert u \rangle - t \langle v \vert u \rangle - t \langle u \vert v \rangle + t^{2} \langle v \vert v \rangle$$

$$= \lVert u \rVert^{2} - 2t \, \text{Re}(\langle u \vert v \rangle) + t^{2} \lVert v \rVert^{2}$$

This is a quadratic in $t$ that is non-negative for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Hence its **discriminant must be non-positive**: (2 marks)

$$\Delta = 4 [\text{Re}(\langle u \vert v \rangle)]^{2} - 4 \lVert u \rVert^{2} \lVert v \rVert^{2} \leq 0$$

Therefore $[\text{Re}(\langle u \vert v \rangle)]^{2} \leq \lVert u \rVert^{2} \lVert v \rVert^{2}$. Since $\vert z \vert \geq \text{Re}(z)$ for any complex $z$:

$$\vert \langle u \vert v \rangle \vert^{2} \leq \lVert u \rVert^{2} \lVert v \rVert^{2} \implies \vert \langle u \vert v \rangle \vert \leq \lVert u \rVert \, \lVert v \rVert \quad \blacksquare$$ (1 mark)

Equality holds when $\Delta = 0$, i.e., when $f(t) = 0$ for some $t$, meaning $|u - t v\rangle = \mathbf{0}$, i.e., the vectors are linearly dependent.

---

**Part (b) — Function Space Computations (7 marks):**

**(i) Compute $\langle \sin x \vert \cos x \rangle$ (3 marks):**

$$\langle \sin x \vert \cos x \rangle = \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin x \cos x \, dx$$

Using the identity $\sin x \cos x = \frac{1}{2} \sin(2x)$:

$$= \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin(2x) \, dx = \frac{1}{2} \left[ -\frac{1}{2} \cos(2x) \right]_0^{\pi/2}$$

$$= -\frac{1}{4} \left[ \cos(\pi) - \cos(0) \right] = -\frac{1}{4} \left[ -1 - 1 \right] = -\frac{1}{4} \cdot (-2) = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\langle \sin x \vert \cos x \rangle = \frac{1}{2}}$$

**(ii) Compute $\lVert \sin x \rVert$ (2 marks):**

$$\lVert \sin x \rVert^{2} = \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^{2} x \, dx = \int_0^{\pi/2} \frac{1 - \cos(2x)}{2} \, dx$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\pi/2} dx - \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos(2x) \, dx = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left[ \frac{\sin(2x)}{2} \right]_0^{\pi/2}$$

$$= \frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{1}{4} (\sin \pi - \sin 0) = \frac{\pi}{4} - 0 = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\lVert \sin x \rVert = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}}$$

**(iii) Orthogonality Check (2 marks):**

Two functions are orthogonal iff their inner product is zero. Here:

$$\langle \sin x \vert \cos x \rangle = \frac{1}{2} \neq 0$$

Therefore, $\sin x$ and $\cos x$ are **NOT orthogonal** on the interval $[0, \pi/2]$. They would be orthogonal on $[0, \pi]$ (or any symmetric interval around $\pi/2$), but not on the half-interval considered.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Conjugate Symmetry Trap:** Many students write $\langle v \vert u \rangle = \langle u \vert v \rangle$ instead of $\langle v \vert u \rangle = \langle u \vert v \rangle^{*}$. The complex conjugate **must** appear in complex inner product spaces. (-1 to -2 marks)
>
> 2. **Linearity Convention:** Linear in the *first* argument and conjugate-linear in the *second*. Reversing this loses full marks. If a problem says "linear in the second argument," it is referring to a *sesquilinear form* with the opposite convention — always read the question carefully. (-1 mark)
>
> 3. **Cauchy–Schwarz Modulus:** The inequality is $\vert \langle u \vert v \rangle \vert \leq \lVert u \rVert \lVert v \rVert$. Students often write $\langle u \vert v \rangle \leq \ldots$ without the modulus — **this is wrong** for complex inner products. (-1 mark)
>
> 4. **Born Rule Misuse:** The probability is $\vert \langle \phi \vert \psi \rangle \vert^{2}$, not $\langle \phi \vert \psi \rangle^{2}$. The modulus *must* be applied before squaring. (-1 mark)
>
> 5. **Dagger Confusion:** The bra is the *conjugate transpose* of the ket: $\langle \psi \vert = (\vert \psi \rangle)^{\dagger}$. Forgetting the complex conjugate is a frequent error. (-1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Inner Products**
>
> - **Inner product** is a function $\langle \cdot \vert \cdot \rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{C}$ that satisfies **three axioms**: conjugate symmetry, linearity in the first argument, and positive definiteness.
>
> - A **Hilbert space** $\mathcal{H}$ is a *complete* inner product space — the natural home of quantum states.
>
> - **Dirac notation:** $\langle \phi \vert \psi \rangle$ is a *complex scalar* (bra-ket). $\vert \psi \rangle$ is a column vector (ket); $\langle \psi \vert$ is a row vector (bra).
>
> - **Bra formation rule:** $\langle \psi \vert = (\vert \psi \rangle)^{\dagger}$ = conjugate transpose. Forgetting the conjugate is a common KTU mistake.
>
> - **Standard inner product on $\mathbb{C}^{n}$:** $\langle u \vert v \rangle = \sum_{i} u_i^{*} v_i$.
>
> - **Inner product on $L^2$:** $\langle f \vert g \rangle = \int f(x)^{*} g(x) \, dx$.
>
> - **Norm:** $\lVert v \rVert = \sqrt{\langle v \vert v \rangle}$. Always a real, non-negative number.
>
> - **Orthogonality:** $\langle u \vert v \rangle = 0$ ⟺ $\vert u \rangle \perp \vert v \rangle$.
>
> - **Orthonormality:** $\langle e_i \vert e_j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$ (Kronecker delta).
>
> - **Cauchy–Schwarz:** $\vert \langle u \vert v \rangle \vert \leq \lVert u \rVert \lVert v \rVert$, equality iff linearly dependent.
>
> - **Born Rule:** $P(\phi) = \vert \langle \phi \vert \psi \rangle \vert^{2}$ is the probability of measuring state $\vert \phi \rangle$ from $\vert \psi \rangle$. The Cauchy–Schwarz inequality guarantees $0 \leq P \leq 1$.
>
> - **Triangle inequality:** $\lVert u + v \rVert \leq \lVert u \rVert + \lVert v \rVert$ (consequence of Cauchy–Schwarz).
>
> - **Unitary preservation:** $\langle Uu \vert Uv \rangle = \langle u \vert v \rangle$ — this is why quantum gates (unitary operators) preserve inner products and hence probabilities.
>
> - **Three memory hooks for the axioms:** "*Conjugate when swapping*" (Axiom 1), "*Linear in the left slot*" (Axiom 2), "*Self-overlap is always positive*" (Axiom 3).
>
> - **Quick verification trick:** If a problem says "verify that ... is an inner product," check the three axioms in the order: symmetry → linearity → positivity. KTU examiners award partial marks for each.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2: Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eigenvalue Equation (Canonical Form):**
> For a square matrix $A \in \mathbb{C}^{n \times n}$, a non-zero vector $\vert v \rangle \in \mathbb{C}^{n}$ is called an **eigenvector** of $A$ and the scalar $\lambda \in \mathbb{C}$ is called its corresponding **eigenvalue** if and only if the following relation holds:
> $$A \vert v \rangle = \lambda \vert v \rangle$$

The set of all eigenvalues of $A$ is called the **spectrum** of $A$, denoted $\sigma(A)$. The **characteristic polynomial** $p(\lambda)$ is defined as:

$$p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$$

where $I$ is the $n \times n$ identity matrix. The roots of $p(\lambda) = 0$ give the eigenvalues.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Stretching" Machine:**
> Imagine a strange machine (the matrix $A$) that transforms any vector you feed into it. Most vectors get **rotated, twisted, and scrambled** into completely new directions. However, a few **special vectors** (the eigenvectors) have a magical property: the machine only **stretches or shrinks** them along their own direction without rotating them. The **factor** by which they are stretched or shrunk is the **eigenvalue** $\lambda$.
> - If $\lambda > 1$ → the vector is **stretched** (amplified).
> - If $0 < \lambda < 1$ → the vector is **shrunk** (damped).
> - If $\lambda < 0$ → the vector is **flipped** in direction.
> - If $\lambda = 0$ → the vector is **collapsed to zero** (singular).
> - If $\lambda$ is complex (unit magnitude $\vert \lambda \vert = 1$) → the vector is **rotated** in complex space (this is exactly what happens in **quantum gates**!).

In **quantum computing**, every observable (like energy or spin) is represented by a **Hermitian operator** $H = H^{\dagger}$, and its eigenvalues are the **measurable values** you can read on a quantum detector. The eigenvectors represent the **basis states** in which the system is "definite."

### 1.3 Key Terminology Summary

| Term | Mathematical Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Eigenvector | $\vert v \rangle \neq 0$ | Special state unchanged in direction |
| Eigenvalue | $\lambda$ | Scaling factor / Measurement outcome |
| Spectrum | $\sigma(A)$ | Set of all eigenvalues |
| Characteristic Polynomial | $p(\lambda)$ | $\det(A - \lambda I)$ |
| Multiplicity | $m(\lambda_i)$ | Number of times $\lambda_i$ appears |
| Degenerate | $m(\lambda_i) > 1$ | Multiple eigenvectors share one eigenvalue |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Eigen-Problem

The eigenvalue problem can be broken into **three sequential steps**:

- **Step 1 — Set up the characteristic equation:**
  Move $\lambda \vert v \rangle$ to the LHS:
  $$A \vert v \rangle - \lambda \vert v \rangle = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (A - \lambda I) \vert v \rangle = 0$$
  The "Why": A non-trivial (non-zero) solution $\vert v \rangle$ exists **only if** the matrix $A - \lambda I$ is **singular** (non-invertible).

- **Step 2 — Find the eigenvalues (spectrum):**
  Take the determinant:
  $$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$
  This is an $n$-th degree polynomial in $\lambda$. The $n$ roots (counting multiplicity) are the eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$.

- **Step 3 — Find the eigenvectors (eigenspaces):**
  For each $\lambda_i$, solve:
  $$(A - \lambda_i I) \vert v_i \rangle = 0$$
  The set of all solutions forms the **eigenspace** $E_{\lambda_i} = \ker(A - \lambda_i I)$ of dimension equal to the geometric multiplicity.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Key Formulas for Board Exams**

| # | Property | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Eigenvalue equation | $A \vert v \rangle = \lambda \vert v \rangle$ | Core definition |
| 2 | Characteristic polynomial | $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$ | Roots $\to$ eigenvalues |
| 3 | Trace identity | $\text{Tr}(A) = \sum_{i} \lambda_i$ | Sum of eigenvalues |
| 4 | Determinant identity | $\det(A) = \prod_{i} \lambda_i$ | Product of eigenvalues |
| 5 | Power of matrix | $A^{k} \vert v \rangle = \lambda^{k} \vert v \rangle$ | Diagonalizable repeatedly |
| 6 | Hermitian property | $H = H^{\dagger} \Rightarrow \lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ | Real eigenvalues |
| 7 | Unitary property | $U^{\dagger} U = I \Rightarrow \vert \lambda \vert = 1$ | Spectrum on unit circle |
| 8 | Spectral theorem | $A = \sum_{i} \lambda_i \vert v_i \rangle \langle v_i \vert$ | For normal matrices |
| 9 | Orthogonality | $\langle v_i \vert v_j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$ | For Hermitian eigenbasis |
| 10 | Expectation value | $\langle v \vert A \vert v \rangle = \lambda$ | If $A \vert v \rangle = \lambda \vert v \rangle$ |
| 11 | Shift identity | $(A + cI)\vert v \rangle = (\lambda + c)\vert v \rangle$ | All eigenvalues shift by $c$ |
| 12 | Commuting operators | $[A,B] = 0 \Rightarrow$ shared eigenbasis | Crucial for Q. Mech. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In quantum hardware, every **qubit readout** depends on the eigenvalues of the **Pauli operator** $\sigma_z = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, which has eigenvalues $\pm 1$ corresponding to logical 0 and 1. The **decomposition of unitary gates** (e.g., Solovay-Kitaev, QFT) relies on the spectral theorem applied to $U$.

## 2.3 Why Eigen-Decomposition Matters in Quantum Computing

- **Measurement postulate**: When you measure an observable $H$, the outcome is always an eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ of $H$, and the state collapses to the corresponding eigenstate $\vert v_i \rangle$.
- **Diagonalization**: Any Hermitian $H$ can be written as $H = P D P^{-1}$, where $D$ is diagonal. This **simplifies matrix exponentiation** $e^{-iHt/\hbar}$, which is the heart of quantum simulation algorithms.
- **Stability analysis**: Whether a quantum operation is stable under noise is determined by the spectrum of the noise channel's **Kraus operators**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Characteristic Polynomial for a 2×2 Matrix

**Problem Setup:** Let
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}, \quad I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1:** Form the matrix $A - \lambda I$:
$$A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} a - \lambda & b \\ c & d - \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the determinant using the standard 2×2 formula $\det\begin{pmatrix} p & q \\ r & s \end{pmatrix} = ps - qr$:
$$p(\lambda) = (a - \lambda)(d - \lambda) - (b)(c)$$

**Step 3:** Expand the product:
$$p(\lambda) = ad - a\lambda - d\lambda + \lambda^{2} - bc$$

**Step 4:** Regroup by powers of $\lambda$:
$$p(\lambda) = \lambda^{2} - (a + d)\lambda + (ad - bc)$$

**Step 5:** Identify with the trace and determinant:
- Trace: $\text{Tr}(A) = a + d$
- Determinant: $\det(A) = ad - bc$

Therefore:
$$p(\lambda) = \lambda^{2} - \text{Tr}(A)\,\lambda + \det(A) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result (Quadratic Formula):**
> $$\lambda = \frac{\text{Tr}(A) \pm \sqrt{(\text{Tr}(A))^{2} - 4\det(A)}}{2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Eigenvalues of the Pauli-Z Matrix

**Given:** The Pauli-Z operator
$$\sigma_z = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1:** Build the characteristic matrix:
$$\sigma_z - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - \lambda & 0 \\ 0 & -1 - \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Take the determinant (this is diagonal, so it's just the product of diagonal entries):
$$\det(\sigma_z - \lambda I) = (1 - \lambda)(-1 - \lambda) - (0)(0)$$

**Step 3:** Expand:
$$p(\lambda) = -(1 - \lambda) - \lambda(1 - \lambda) = -(1 - \lambda)(1 + \lambda)$$

**Step 4:** Set the polynomial to zero:
$$-(1 - \lambda)(1 + \lambda) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1 - \lambda)(1 + \lambda) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve the two linear factors:
- From $(1 - \lambda) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = +1$
- From $(1 + \lambda) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_2 = -1$

**Result:** $\sigma_z$ has eigenvalues $\boxed{\lambda_1 = +1, \quad \lambda_2 = -1}$ — these are precisely the **two outcome states of a qubit measurement** in the computational basis.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Finding the Eigenvectors of Pauli-Z

For $\lambda_1 = +1$:
$$(\sigma_z - (+1)I) \vert v_1 \rangle = 0 \Rightarrow \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ -2y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

This gives $y = 0$, $x$ free. Normalize: $\vert x \vert^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow x = 1$.
$$\vert v_1 \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \vert 0 \rangle$$

For $\lambda_2 = -1$:
$$(\sigma_z - (-1)I) \vert v_2 \rangle = 0 \Rightarrow \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2x \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

This gives $x = 0$, $y$ free. Normalize: $\vert y \vert^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow y = 1$.
$$\vert v_2 \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \vert 1 \rangle$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Spectral Decomposition of $\sigma_z$:**
> $$\sigma_z = (+1)\vert 0 \rangle\langle 0 \vert + (-1)\vert 1 \rangle\langle 1 \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$ ✓ (self-consistent)

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Trace-Identity and Determinant-Identity

**Claim:** $\text{Tr}(A) = \sum_i \lambda_i$ and $\det(A) = \prod_i \lambda_i$.

**Proof sketch:** From the characteristic polynomial:
$$p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I) = (-1)^{n}\left[\lambda^{n} - \text{Tr}(A)\lambda^{n-1} + \cdots + (-1)^{n}\det(A)\right]$$

Comparing with the **Vieta's formulas** for the roots $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n$ of the polynomial $\prod_{i=1}^{n}(\lambda - \lambda_i)$:
- Sum of roots = $\sum_i \lambda_i$ = coefficient of $\lambda^{n-1}$ = $\text{Tr}(A)$
- Product of roots = $\prod_i \lambda_i$ = constant term = $\det(A)$ ∎

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: The Eigenvalue Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Square Matrix A] --> B[Form Matrix A minus lambda I]
    B --> C[Compute Characteristic Polynomial det A minus lambda I]
    C --> D[Set Polynomial Equal to Zero]
    D --> E[Find n Roots lambda 1 lambda 2 ... lambda n]
    E --> F{For Each lambda i}
    F --> G[Solve A minus lambda i I times v equals 0]
    G --> H[Extract Non Trivial Kernel]
    H --> I[Normalize Eigenvectors to Unit Length]
    I --> J[Verify A times v equals lambda times v]
    J --> K[Report Spectrum and Eigenspace]
    F --> L{All Eigenvalues Processed}
    L --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Role of Eigen-Properties in Quantum Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Quantum State ket psi] --> O[Observable Operator H Hermitian]
    O --> SP[Spectral Decomposition H equals sum lambda i Pi i]
    SP --> M1[Projector P0 onto lambda 1]
    SP --> M2[Projector P1 onto lambda 2]
    SP --> M3[Projector Pn onto lambda n]
    M1 --> R1[Outcome lambda 1 with prob p1]
    M2 --> R2[Outcome lambda 2 with prob p2]
    M3 --> R3[Outcome lambda n with prob pn]
    R1 --> COL[Post Measurement State Collapses to Corresponding Eigenvector]
    R2 --> COL
    R3 --> COL
```

## 4.3 Taxonomy Diagram: Classification of Operators by Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP[Linear Operator A] --> N1{Is A Hermitian A dagger equals A}
    N1 -- Yes --> H1[Real Eigenvalues]
    H1 --> H2[Orthogonal Eigenvectors]
    H2 --> H3[Diagonalizable via Unitary U]
    N1 -- No --> N2{Is A Unitary A dagger A equals I}
    N2 -- Yes --> U1[Eigenvalues on Unit Circle mod lambda equals 1]
    U1 --> U2[Orthonormal Eigenbasis]
    N2 -- No --> N3{Is A Normal A dagger A equals A A dagger}
    N3 -- Yes --> NM1[Diagonalizable by Unitary]
    N3 -- No --> NM2[May Not Be Diagonalizable Defective]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. Define eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix. State and prove the trace identity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **eigenvalue** $\lambda$ of a square matrix $A$ is a scalar such that the equation $A\vert v \rangle = \lambda \vert v \rangle$ has a non-trivial solution $\vert v \rangle \neq 0$. The non-zero vector $\vert v \rangle$ is the corresponding **eigenvector**.

**Trace Identity:** The sum of the eigenvalues equals the trace of the matrix:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \text{Tr}(A)$$

**Proof:** The characteristic polynomial expands as $p(\lambda) = \lambda^{n} - \text{Tr}(A)\lambda^{n-1} + \cdots$. By Vieta's formula, the sum of its roots equals the coefficient of $\lambda^{n-1}$ with sign reversed, which is $\text{Tr}(A)$. ∎

### **Q2. Why are the eigenvalues of a Hermitian matrix always real? Mention its significance in quantum mechanics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Let $H = H^{\dagger}$ be Hermitian and $H\vert v \rangle = \lambda \vert v \rangle$. Taking the inner product with $\langle v \vert$:
$$\langle v \vert H \vert v \rangle = \lambda \langle v \vert v \rangle$$

Taking the complex conjugate and using $\langle v \vert H \vert v \rangle^{*} = \langle v \vert H^{\dagger} \vert v \rangle = \langle v \vert H \vert v \rangle$ (Hermitian), we get $\lambda = \lambda^{*}$, i.e., $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Significance:** Observables in quantum mechanics are represented by Hermitian operators, ensuring that **measurement outcomes are always real numbers** (e.g., energy, spin, position).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern | Module 2]**

---

### 📘 **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the matrix
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}.$$
Verify the trace and determinant identities. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Characteristic polynomial**
$$p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I) = (4-\lambda)(3-\lambda) - (1)(2)$$
$$= 12 - 4\lambda - 3\lambda + \lambda^{2} - 2 = \lambda^{2} - 7\lambda + 10$$

**Step 2: Solve $p(\lambda) = 0$**
$$\lambda^{2} - 7\lambda + 10 = (\lambda - 2)(\lambda - 5) = 0$$
$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = 2, \quad \lambda_2 = 5}$$

**Step 3: Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 2$**
$$(A - 2I)\vert v_1 \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow 2x + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = -2x$$
Normalize: $x^{2} + 4x^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow x = 1/\sqrt{5}$
$$\vert v_1 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 5$**
$$(A - 5I)\vert v_2 \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow -x + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = x$$
Normalize: $2x^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow x = 1/\sqrt{2}$
$$\vert v_2 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Verification of identities**
- Trace identity: $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 2 + 5 = 7 = \text{Tr}(A) = 4 + 3$ ✓
- Determinant identity: $\lambda_1 \cdot \lambda_2 = 2 \times 5 = 10 = \det(A) = (4)(3) - (1)(2)$ ✓

#### **(b)** A quantum observable is given by the Hamiltonian
$$H = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -i \\ i & 1 \end{pmatrix}.$$
Show that $H$ is Hermitian, find its eigenvalues, and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Verify Hermiticity**
$$H^{\dagger} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -i \\ i & 1 \end{pmatrix}^{\dagger} = \begin{pmatrix} 1^{*} & (i)^{*} \\ (-i)^{*} & 1^{*} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -i \\ i & 1 \end{pmatrix} = H \quad \checkmark$$
(Recall: for a matrix, $(M^{*})^{T} = M^{\dagger}$; complex conjugate + transpose.)

**Step 2: Eigenvalues**
$$p(\lambda) = (1-\lambda)^{2} - (-i)(i) = (1-\lambda)^{2} - 1$$
Set to zero:
$$(1-\lambda)^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow 1 - \lambda = \pm 1 \Rightarrow \lambda = 0 \text{ or } \lambda = 2$$
$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = 0, \quad \lambda_2 = 2}$$

**Step 3: Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 0$**
$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -i \\ i & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow x - iy = 0 \Rightarrow x = iy$$
Normalize: $\vert y \vert^{2} + \vert y \vert^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow y = 1/\sqrt{2}$
$$\vert v_1 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} i \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 2$**
$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & -i \\ i & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow -x - iy = 0 \Rightarrow x = -iy$$
Normalize:
$$\vert v_2 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} -i \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**
The observable $H$ has two possible measurement outcomes: **0** and **2** energy units. The corresponding eigenstates are $\vert v_1 \rangle$ and $\vert v_2 \rangle$. When a quantum system is in state $\vert v_1 \rangle$, measuring $H$ deterministically yields **0** (and similarly **2** for $\vert v_2 \rangle$). For any superposition state, the measurement outcome is probabilistic with probabilities given by $|\langle v_i \vert \psi \rangle|^{2}$ (Born rule).

---

### 📗 **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a)** State and prove the **Spectral Theorem** for Hermitian operators. Using it, decompose the matrix
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}.$$
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Spectral Theorem Statement**
> If $H$ is an $n \times n$ Hermitian matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n$ and corresponding orthonormal eigenvectors $\vert v_1 \rangle, \ldots, \vert v_n \rangle$, then:
> $$H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \vert v_i \rangle \langle v_i \vert$$
> Equivalently, $H = U D U^{\dagger}$, where $U$ is unitary and $D$ is diagonal.

**Step 2: Proof Outline**
- Construct $U = [\vert v_1 \rangle \vert v_2 \rangle \cdots \vert v_n \rangle]$ whose columns are eigenvectors. Since $H$ is Hermitian, eigenvectors are orthonormal, so $U^{\dagger}U = I$, i.e., $U$ is unitary.
- $HU = UD$ (each column of $UD$ is $\lambda_i \vert v_i \rangle$).
- Multiplying on the right by $U^{\dagger}$: $H = UDU^{\dagger}$.
- Outer-product form: $H = \sum_i \lambda_i \vert v_i \rangle\langle v_i \vert$ ∎

**Step 3: Apply to given matrix**
Characteristic polynomial:
$$p(\lambda) = (3-\lambda)^{2} - 1 = \lambda^{2} - 6\lambda + 8 = (\lambda - 2)(\lambda - 4) = 0$$
Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 2, \quad \lambda_2 = 4$

Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = 2$:
$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow x + y = 0 \Rightarrow \vert v_1 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = 4$:
$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow x = y \Rightarrow \vert v_2 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Spectral Decomposition**
$$A = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} + 4 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$
$$= \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \quad \checkmark$$

#### **(b)** A quantum gate is given by the unitary matrix
$$U = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \quad \text{(Hadamard Gate)}.$$
Find its eigenvalues. What do they tell you about the gate's action? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Check unitarity**
$U U^{\dagger} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = I$ ✓

**Step 2: Eigenvalues**
$$p(\lambda) = \det(U - \lambda I) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \det\begin{pmatrix} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \lambda & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$
For simplicity, use the trace and determinant:
- $\text{Tr}(U) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0$
- $\det(U) = \frac{1}{2}\left[(-1) - (1)\right] = -1$

So: $\lambda^{2} - 0 \cdot \lambda + (-1) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda^{2} = 1$
$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = +1, \quad \lambda_2 = -1}$$

**Step 3: Verify $\vert \lambda \vert = 1$**
Both eigenvalues have unit modulus, as **expected for any unitary matrix** ($U^{\dagger}U = I$ forces $\vert \lambda \vert = 1$).

**Step 4: Eigenvectors**
For $\lambda_1 = +1$:
$$(U - I)\vert v_1 \rangle = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 - \sqrt{2} & 1 \\ 1 & -1 - \sqrt{2} \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 0$$
Using proportionality from row 1: $x(1 - \sqrt{2}) + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = (\sqrt{2} - 1)x$
Normalized: $\vert v_1 \rangle \propto \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ \sqrt{2} - 1 \end{pmatrix}$

For $\lambda_2 = -1$: $x(1 + \sqrt{2}) + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = -(1 + \sqrt{2})x$
$\vert v_2 \rangle \propto \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 - \sqrt{2} \end{pmatrix}$

**Step 5: Interpretation**
- The eigenvalues $\pm 1$ are **real and unit-modulus**, confirming $H$ is both Hermitian-like and unitary (Hadamard is its own inverse: $H^{2} = I$).
- The gate creates **superposition** when applied to computational basis states: $H\vert 0 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle)$ and $H\vert 1 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle - \vert 1 \rangle)$.
- The eigenstates of $H$ are special states that are reproduced (up to a sign) by the gate — these are exactly the **$X$-basis states** $\vert + \rangle$ and $\vert - \rangle$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Forgetting to set $p(\lambda) = 0$** — Many students compute the determinant but never equate it to zero. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **Not normalizing eigenvectors** — The KTU valuation key explicitly requires unit-norm eigenvectors $\langle v \vert v \rangle = 1$ unless stated otherwise. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Confusing $H^{\dagger}$ with $H^{T}$** — In quantum computing, the dagger is **conjugate-transpose**, not just transpose. $(M^{\dagger})_{ij} = (M_{ji})^{*}$ (complex conjugate + transpose).
> 4. **Assuming all matrices are diagonalizable** — Always check for **defective matrices** (geometric multiplicity < algebraic multiplicity). If defective, the matrix is **not** diagonalizable.
> 5. **Dropping the minus sign** in $\det(A - \lambda I)$ — The standard form is $(A - \lambda I)$, not $(\lambda I - A)$. Reversing them flips the sign of every odd-power coefficient.
> 6. **Skipping verification** — When a question says "verify," you must substitute the eigenvalue and eigenvector back into $A\vert v \rangle = \lambda \vert v \rangle$ explicitly. *(−1 mark otherwise)*

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ **Eigenvalue equation:** $A \vert v \rangle = \lambda \vert v \rangle$, with $\vert v \rangle \neq 0$.
- ✅ **Characteristic polynomial:** $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$; roots give eigenvalues.
- ✅ **Two identities to memorize:**
  - $\sum_i \lambda_i = \text{Tr}(A)$
  - $\prod_i \lambda_i = \det(A)$
- ✅ **Hermitian matrices** ($H = H^{\dagger}$) → **real eigenvalues** + **orthogonal eigenvectors** (real spectrum is essential for QM observables).
- ✅ **Unitary matrices** ($U^{\dagger}U = I$) → eigenvalues satisfy $\vert \lambda \vert = 1$ (spectrum lies on the **unit circle** in $\mathbb{C}$).
- ✅ **Normal matrices** ($A^{\dagger}A = AA^{\dagger}$) → unitarily diagonalizable: $A = UDU^{\dagger}$.
- ✅ **Spectral theorem:** $H = \sum_i \lambda_i \vert v_i \rangle \langle v_i \vert$ — the workhorse of quantum measurement and matrix exponentiation.
- ✅ **Commuting observables** $[A, B] = 0$ → share a **common eigenbasis** (foundation of quantum error correction).
- ✅ **Pauli matrices** $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ all have eigenvalues $\pm 1$ — they are the generators of single-qubit rotations on the Bloch sphere.
- ✅ **Power rule:** $A^{n} \vert v \rangle = \lambda^{n} \vert v \rangle$ — diagonalization is essential for computing $e^{-iHt}$ in quantum simulation.
- ✅ **Algorithm to find spectrum:**
  (1) Form $(A - \lambda I)$ →
  (2) Take determinant →
  (3) Solve polynomial →
  (4) Back-substitute to find eigenvectors →
  (5) Normalize.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love questions that ask you to **verify both trace AND determinant identities** in one go. Practice a minimum of 5 problems covering 2×2, 3×3, Hermitian, and unitary matrices before your ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adjoints and Hermitian Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT634 - QUANTUM COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Adjoints and Hermitian Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Adjoint (Conjugate Transpose) of a Matrix / Operator**
Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ complex matrix acting on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space $\mathbb{C}^n$. The **adjoint** (also called the **Hermitian conjugate** or **dagger**) of $A$, denoted $A^\dagger$, is defined as the $n \times n$ matrix obtained by taking the transpose of $A$ and then the complex conjugate of every entry. Formally,

$$A^\dagger = (\overline{A})^{T} = \overline{A^{T}}$$

where the overline denotes element-wise complex conjugation and the superscript $T$ denotes the transpose.

**Hermitian (Self-Adjoint) Operator**
An operator $A$ is called **Hermitian** (or **self-adjoint**) if and only if it equals its own adjoint:

$$A = A^\dagger$$

That is, $A_{ij} = \overline{A_{ji}}$ for every $i,j$.

**Unitary Operator**
An operator $U$ is **unitary** if its adjoint equals its inverse:

$$U^\dagger U = UU^\dagger = I$$

**Normal Operator**
An operator $N$ is **normal** if it commutes with its own adjoint:

$$NN^\dagger = N^\dagger N$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** In quantum computing, observables (position, momentum, energy, spin) are represented by **Hermitian operators**. The evolution of a quantum state is governed by **unitary operators**. The spectral theorem applies specifically to normal operators on finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **rotating wheel with mirrored inscriptions**:

- The **adjoint** is like rotating the wheel $180^\circ$ and flipping it over so the text becomes a mirror image — what was on the left is now on the right, and every engraved direction is reversed.
- A **Hermitian operator** is a special wheel that looks *exactly* the same after this rotate-and-flip operation. Its "mirror twin" is itself.
- A **unitary operator** is like a perfectly balanced gyroscope — it preserves all lengths and angles. Nothing about the system's "size" changes after it acts.
- In quantum mechanics, real-world *measurements* always yield **real numbers** (not complex ones). The mathematical reason is that observables are Hermitian: their eigenvalues are forced to be real.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Dagger notation** $A^\dagger$ — read as "A-dagger"
- **Inner product symbol** $\langle \phi \vert \psi \rangle$ (Dirac / bra-ket notation)
- **Identity operator** $I_n$ — the $n \times n$ identity matrix
- **Hilbert space dimension** $n = 2^n$ qubits for an $n$-qubit system

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEITT634 Module 2):** Adjoints and Hermitian operators form the algebraic backbone of quantum measurement theory. Mastery of these definitions is essential before tackling the postulates of quantum mechanics in later modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Breakdown of Operational Concepts

**Step A — Why the Adjoint Exists:**
Every linear operator $A$ on an inner-product space has a unique adjoint that captures "backwards multiplication" relative to the inner product.

$$\langle \phi \vert A \psi \rangle = \langle A^\dagger \phi \vert \psi \rangle$$

**Step B — Why Hermitian Operators Matter:**
Quantum measurement theory demands that every physically measurable quantity (an observable) corresponds to an operator whose eigenvalues — the only possible measurement outcomes — are real numbers. Only Hermitian operators guarantee this reality of eigenvalues.

**Step C — Why Unitary Operators Matter:**
Quantum state evolution must preserve probability ($\sum_i \vert c_i \vert^2 = 1$). Operators that preserve the inner product are exactly the unitaries, hence time evolution is unitary by the Schrödinger equation.

**Step D — The 'How' Behind the Definitions:**
For a matrix $A = [a_{ij}]$, we mechanically compute $A^\dagger = [\overline{a_{ji}}]$ — flip across the main diagonal and conjugate each complex entry.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Key Property | Unit / Type |
|---|---------|---------------------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | Adjoint (matrix) | $A^\dagger = \overline{A^{T}}$ | Entry: $(A^\dagger)_{ij} = \overline{A_{ji}}$ | Operator |
| 2 | Adjoint (bra-ket) | $\langle \phi \vert A \psi \rangle = \langle A^\dagger \phi \vert \psi \rangle$ | Sesquilinear inner product | Scalar |
| 3 | Hermitian | $A = A^\dagger$ | Diagonal entries real; $A_{ij} = \overline{A_{ji}}$ | Observable |
| 4 | Unitary | $U^\dagger U = I$ | $U^{-1} = U^\dagger$; preserves inner product | Evolution |
| 5 | Normal | $A A^\dagger = A^\dagger A$ | Diagonalizable unitarily | Spectral |
| 6 | Eigenvalues of Hermitian | $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ | All eigenvalues real | Real scalar |
| 7 | Eigenvectors (distinct $\lambda$) | $\langle v_i \vert v_j \rangle = 0$ for $\lambda_i \neq \lambda_j$ | Orthogonal | — |
| 8 | Inverse relation | $(AB)^\dagger = B^\dagger A^\dagger$ | Anti-homomorphism | — |
| 9 | Scalar relation | $(cA)^\dagger = \overline{c}\, A^\dagger$ | Conjugate scalar | — |
| 10 | Projector | $P = P^\dagger = P^2$ | Hermitian and idempotent | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe Symbol Note:** In the table above, absolute value / inner-product bars have been written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ and $\langle \cdot \vert \cdot \rangle$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Avoid writing raw $\vert x \vert$ inside table cells.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quantum Hardware Design:** Every quantum gate (Hadamard $H$, Pauli $X, Y, Z$, CNOT, Phase $S, T$) is unitary — engineers verify gate correctness via the unitarity condition $U^\dagger U = I$.
- **NMR & MRI Medical Imaging:** The nuclear spin Hamiltonian is Hermitian; resonance frequencies are eigenvalues of Hermitian matrices.
- **Quantum Error Correction:** Stabilizer codes use commuting Hermitian operators whose measurement eigenvalues detect errors without collapsing logical information.
- **Spectroscopy & Molecular Modeling:** Electronic energies of molecules are eigenvalues of the Hamiltonian operator $H$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Theorem 1: Eigenvalues of a Hermitian Operator Are Real

**Statement:** If $A$ is Hermitian ($A = A^\dagger$) and $A \vert v \rangle = \lambda \vert v \rangle$ for some non-zero vector $\vert v \rangle$, then $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Derivation:**

We start from the eigenvalue equation and take the inner product with $\vert v \rangle$ from the left.

$$A \vert v \rangle = \lambda \vert v \rangle$$

Take the inner product of both sides with $\vert v \rangle$ on the left:

$$\langle v \vert A \vert v \rangle = \langle v \vert \lambda \vert v \rangle = \lambda \langle v \vert v \rangle$$

This is Equation (1).

Now take the **complex conjugate** of both sides of the original eigenvalue equation. Recall that complex conjugation turns the column vector $\vert v \rangle$ into a bra $\langle v \vert$ and replaces scalars with their conjugates:

$$\overline{A \vert v \rangle} = \overline{\lambda \vert v \rangle} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \langle v \vert A^\dagger = \overline{\lambda} \langle v \vert$$

Now take the inner product of this conjugated equation with $\vert v \rangle$ on the right:

$$\langle v \vert A^\dagger \vert v \rangle = \overline{\lambda} \langle v \vert v \rangle$$

This is Equation (2).

**Apply the Hermitian property** $A^\dagger = A$ to Equation (2). The left-hand side becomes $\langle v \vert A \vert v \rangle$:

$$\langle v \vert A \vert v \rangle = \overline{\lambda} \langle v \vert v \rangle$$

But Equation (1) tells us $\langle v \vert A \vert v \rangle = \lambda \langle v \vert v \rangle$. Substituting:

$$\lambda \langle v \vert v \rangle = \overline{\lambda} \langle v \vert v \rangle$$

Since $\vert v \rangle \neq 0$, the norm $\langle v \vert v \rangle \neq 0$, so we can divide both sides:

$$\lambda = \overline{\lambda}$$

A complex number equals its own conjugate **iff** it is purely real. Hence:

$$\boxed{\lambda \in \mathbb{R}}$$

---

### 3.2 Theorem 2: Eigenvectors of a Hermitian Operator (Distinct Eigenvalues) Are Orthogonal

**Statement:** If $A = A^\dagger$ and $A \vert v_i \rangle = \lambda_i \vert v_i \rangle$, $A \vert v_j \rangle = \lambda_j \vert v_j \rangle$ with $\lambda_i \neq \lambda_j$, then $\langle v_i \vert v_j \rangle = 0$.

**Derivation:**

We begin with the eigenvalue equation for $\vert v_i \rangle$ and take the inner product with $\vert v_j \rangle$:

$$\langle v_j \vert A \vert v_i \rangle = \lambda_i \langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle$$

This is Equation (3).

Now use the eigenvalue equation for $\vert v_j \rangle$ conjugated: $A^\dagger \vert v_j \rangle = \lambda_j \vert v_j \rangle$ (using reality of $\lambda_j$ from Theorem 1, so $\overline{\lambda_j} = \lambda_j$). Apply $A = A^\dagger$:

$$A \vert v_j \rangle = \lambda_j \vert v_j \rangle$$

Take the inner product of this with $\vert v_i \rangle$:

$$\langle v_i \vert A \vert v_j \rangle = \lambda_j \langle v_i \vert v_j \rangle$$

Taking the complex conjugate of the left side and using $\overline{\langle v_i \vert A \vert v_j \rangle} = \langle v_j \vert A^\dagger \vert v_i \rangle$:

$$\langle v_j \vert A^\dagger \vert v_i \rangle = \overline{\lambda_j} \langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle = \lambda_j \langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle$$

Since $A^\dagger = A$:

$$\langle v_j \vert A \vert v_i \rangle = \lambda_j \langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle$$

This is Equation (4).

Now compare Equation (3) and Equation (4). Both have the same left-hand side $\langle v_j \vert A \vert v_i \rangle$. Therefore:

$$\lambda_i \langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle = \lambda_j \langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle$$

Rearranging:

$$(\lambda_i - \lambda_j) \langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle = 0$$

By hypothesis, $\lambda_i \neq \lambda_j$, so $\lambda_i - \lambda_j \neq 0$. Therefore the only possibility is:

$$\boxed{\langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle = 0}$$

Hence, $\vert v_i \rangle$ and $\vert v_j \rangle$ are orthogonal. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Verify Hermitian & Unitary

**Given:** 
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & i \\ -i & 2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad U = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $A^\dagger$:** Transpose then conjugate each entry.

$$A^T = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -i \\ i & 2 \end{pmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A^\dagger = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & i \\ -i & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Since $A^\dagger = A$, the matrix $A$ is **Hermitian**.

**Step 2 — Compute eigenvalues of $A$:** Solve $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$.

$$\det \begin{pmatrix} 1 - \lambda & i \\ -i & 2 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = (1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - (i)(-i) = (1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 1$$

Expanding:

$$\lambda^2 - 3\lambda + 2 - 1 = \lambda^2 - 3\lambda + 1 = 0$$

By the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{9 - 4}}{2} = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}$$

Both eigenvalues are **real**, confirming Theorem 1.

**Step 3 — Verify $U$ is Unitary:** Compute $U^\dagger U$.

$$U^\dagger = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \quad (\text{real matrix, so } U^\dagger = U^T)$$

$$U^\dagger U = \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = I$$

Hence $U$ is unitary. (This is the **Hadamard gate** — the most important single-qubit gate in quantum computing.)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Operators in Quantum Computing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Linear Operators on Hilbert Space] --> B[Normal Operators]
    A --> C[Anti-Normal Operators]
    B --> D[Hermitian / Self-Adjoint]
    B --> E[Unitary]
    B --> F[Others Normal]
    D --> D1["A = A dagger - real eigenvalues - orthogonal eigenvectors"]
    D --> D2["Observables - measurement operators - Hamiltonians - Pauli X Y Z"]
    E --> E1["U dagger U = I - inverse equals adjoint - preserves inner product"]
    E --> E2["Quantum gates - Hadamard - CNOT - time evolution operator exp iHt"]
    D --> G{Special Intersection}
    E --> G
    G --> G1["Hermitian AND Unitary - Involutions - A square = I - Pauli X Y Z and Hadamard"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Flow of Quantum Measurement via Hermitian Observables

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Quantum State psi in Hilbert Space] --> S2[Observable represented by Hermitian A]
    S2 --> S3[Spectral Decomposition A = sum lambda_i P_i]
    S3 --> S4[Measure Project onto eigenspace]
    S4 --> S5[Outcome lambda_i with probability p_i]
    S5 --> S6[State collapses to normalized eigenvector]
    S2 -.real eigenvalues.-> S3
    S2 -.orthogonal eigenspaces.-> S6
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Adjoint Operation Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Matrix A with complex entries a_ij] --> T[Step 1 Transpose A to get A transpose]
    T --> C[Step 2 Take complex conjugate of every entry]
    C --> R[Result A dagger with entries a bar sub ji]
    R --> P1[Property 1: Anti-homomorphism: AB dagger = B dagger A dagger]
    R --> P2[Property 2: Inner product duality: phi A psi = A dagger phi psi]
    R --> P3[Property 3: Double dagger is identity: A double dagger = A]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

Define a Hermitian operator. Prove that the eigenvalues of a Hermitian operator are always real.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** An operator $A$ is called Hermitian if $A = A^\dagger$, i.e., $A_{ij} = \overline{A_{ji}}$ for all $i,j$. Equivalently, in Dirac notation, $\langle \phi \vert A \psi \rangle = \langle A \phi \vert \psi \rangle$ for all $\vert \phi \rangle, \vert \psi \rangle$.

**Proof (2 Marks):** Let $A \vert v \rangle = \lambda \vert v \rangle$ with $\vert v \rangle \neq 0$. Then $\langle v \vert A \vert v \rangle = \lambda \langle v \vert v \rangle$. Taking the complex conjugate: $\langle v \vert A^\dagger \vert v \rangle = \overline{\lambda} \langle v \vert v \rangle$. Since $A^\dagger = A$, we get $\lambda \langle v \vert v \rangle = \overline{\lambda} \langle v \vert v \rangle$. Dividing by the non-zero norm gives $\lambda = \overline{\lambda}$, which means $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$.

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

What is a unitary operator? Show that the Pauli-$X$ matrix is unitary.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** An operator $U$ is unitary if $U^\dagger U = UU^\dagger = I$, i.e., $U^{-1} = U^\dagger$.

**Verification (2 Marks):** The Pauli-$X$ matrix is $X = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$. Since $X$ is real and symmetric, $X^\dagger = X^T = X$. Then $X^\dagger X = X^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = I$. Hence $X$ is unitary. (Note: $X$ is also Hermitian and $X^2 = I$.)

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the adjoint of a matrix. For the matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1+i \\ 1-i & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, compute $A^\dagger$ and verify whether $A$ is Hermitian.

**(b) [7 Marks]** State and prove that eigenvectors of a Hermitian operator corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal. Apply this to find two orthogonal eigenvectors of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The adjoint $A^\dagger$ of a complex matrix $A$ is obtained by transposing $A$ and then taking the complex conjugate of every entry, i.e., $(A^\dagger)_{ij} = \overline{A_{ji}}$.

**Compute $A^\dagger$ (3 Marks):**

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1+i \\ 1-i & 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

Taking the transpose:

$$A^T = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1-i \\ 1+i & 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

Taking the complex conjugate:

$$A^\dagger = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1+i \\ 1-i & 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Hermitian Check (2 Marks):** Since $A^\dagger = A$, the matrix $A$ **is Hermitian**. Additionally, the diagonal entries $2$ and $3$ are real, and the off-diagonal entries are complex conjugates of each other — confirming the Hermitian property.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Statement (1 Mark):** If $A = A^\dagger$ and $A \vert v_i \rangle = \lambda_i \vert v_i \rangle$, $A \vert v_j \rangle = \lambda_j \vert v_j \rangle$ with $\lambda_i \neq \lambda_j$ (both real by Theorem 1), then $\langle v_i \vert v_j \rangle = 0$.

**Proof (4 Marks):** From $\langle v_j \vert A \vert v_i \rangle = \lambda_i \langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle$ and using $A^\dagger = A$, also $\langle v_j \vert A \vert v_i \rangle = \lambda_j \langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle$. Equating: $(\lambda_i - \lambda_j) \langle v_j \vert v_i \rangle = 0$. Since $\lambda_i \neq \lambda_j$, we conclude $\langle v_i \vert v_j \rangle = 0$.

**Application (2 Marks):** For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, the eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = 1$ and $\lambda_2 = -1$ (diagonal entries). The corresponding eigenvectors are $\vert v_1 \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\vert v_2 \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$. Their inner product is $0$, confirming orthogonality.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a unitary operator. Prove that a unitary operator preserves the inner product of two quantum states.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the operator $U = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, verify it is both unitary and Hermitian. Compute its eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and verify they are orthogonal.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Definition (2 Marks):** An operator $U$ is unitary if $U^\dagger U = UU^\dagger = I$. Equivalently, $U^{-1} = U^\dagger$.

**Proof — Inner Product Preservation (5 Marks):** Let $\vert \psi \rangle$ and $\vert \phi \rangle$ be two quantum states. The inner product between the transformed states $U \vert \psi \rangle$ and $U \vert \phi \rangle$ is:

$$\langle U \psi \vert U \phi \rangle = \langle \psi \vert U^\dagger U \vert \phi \rangle$$

By the unitarity condition $U^\dagger U = I$:

$$\langle U \psi \vert U \phi \rangle = \langle \psi \vert I \vert \phi \rangle = \langle \psi \vert \phi \rangle$$

Hence, the inner product — and therefore the norm — is preserved by any unitary transformation. This is crucial because probability amplitudes must remain normalized.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Unitarity Check (2 Marks):** $U$ is real and symmetric, so $U^\dagger = U$. Then $U^\dagger U = U^2$:

$$U^2 = \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = I$$

So $U$ is unitary.

**Hermitian Check (1 Mark):** Since $U^\dagger = U$, $U$ is also Hermitian.

**Eigenvalues (2 Marks):** Solve $\det(U - \lambda I) = 0$:

$$\det \begin{pmatrix} \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \lambda & \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = \left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \lambda\right)\left(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \lambda\right) - \tfrac{1}{2} = \lambda^2 - 1 = 0$$

So $\lambda_1 = +1$ and $\lambda_2 = -1$ (both real, as expected).

**Eigenvectors (1 Mark):** $\vert v_1 \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$, $\vert v_2 \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Orthogonality Check (1 Mark):** $\langle v_1 \vert v_2 \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-1)) = 0$. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the complex conjugate** when computing the adjoint — only the transpose is done, costing 1–2 marks. Remember: $A^\dagger = \overline{A^T}$, not just $A^T$.
> 2. **Confusing Hermitian with Unitary** — a Hermitian operator satisfies $A = A^\dagger$; a unitary satisfies $U^\dagger U = I$. Pauli-$X$ is both; Pauli-$Y$ is Hermitian but not unitary in the strict sense unless normalized (which it is, since $Y^2 = I$).
> 3. **Skipping the proof of reality of eigenvalues** — stating the theorem without derivation loses 2 marks. Always include the $\lambda = \overline{\lambda}$ argument.
> 4. **Using the wrong order in $(AB)^\dagger$** — it is $B^\dagger A^\dagger$, not $A^\dagger B^\dagger$. This is the most common order-mistake.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **adjoint** $A^\dagger$ is the transpose of $A$ followed by element-wise complex conjugation.
- ✅ An operator is **Hermitian** iff $A = A^\dagger$ — observable quantities in QM are Hermitian.
- ✅ An operator is **unitary** iff $U^\dagger U = I$ — quantum gates and time evolution are unitary.
- ✅ An operator is **normal** iff $A A^\dagger = A^\dagger A$ — Hermitian and unitary are special cases of normal.
- ✅ **Eigenvalues of Hermitian operators are always real** — critical for measurement outcomes.
- ✅ **Eigenvectors of Hermitian operators with distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal** — basis of measurement postulates.
- ✅ **Anti-homomorphism property:** $(AB)^\dagger = B^\dagger A^\dagger$ — note the order reversal.
- ✅ **Pauli matrices** $X, Y, Z$ are all Hermitian, unitary, and involutory ($A^2 = I$).
- ✅ **Hadamard gate** $H$ is both Hermitian and unitary — the workhorse of quantum algorithms.
- ✅ **Projectors** satisfy $P = P^\dagger = P^2$ — used in spectral decomposition.
- ✅ For a $2 \times 2$ matrix, compute $A^\dagger$ in $\leq 30$ seconds by inspection of the four entries.
- ✅ Always check the **diagonal entries are real** as a quick Hermitian verification for small matrices.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tensor Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT634 - QUANTUM COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tensor Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In quantum computing, the **tensor product** (also called the **Kronecker product** or **direct product**) is a mathematical operation that combines two quantum systems into a single, larger composite quantum system. If we have a quantum system $A$ with state space $V_A$ of dimension $m$ and a quantum system $B$ with state space $V_B$ of dimension $n$, the combined composite system $AB$ lives in a new state space $V_A \otimes V_B$ of dimension $m \cdot n$.

Formally, for two vectors $\vert \psi \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_m \end{pmatrix} \in V_A$ and $\vert \phi \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ \vdots \\ b_n \end{pmatrix} \in V_B$, the tensor product is defined as:

$$
\vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert \phi \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_m \end{pmatrix} \otimes \begin{pmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ \vdots \\ b_n \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 b_1 \\ a_1 b_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_1 b_n \\ a_2 b_1 \\ a_2 b_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_2 b_n \\ \vdots \\ a_m b_1 \\ \vdots \\ a_m b_n \end{pmatrix}
$$

The result is a single column vector of dimension $m \cdot n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The tensor product is the foundational mathematical tool used to describe **multi-qubit systems**, **entangled states**, and **composite quantum operators**. Without tensor products, you cannot analyze circuits with more than one qubit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Conventions Used in KTU Exams:**
> - $\vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert \phi \rangle$ — explicit notation
> - $\vert \psi \rangle \vert \phi \rangle$ — abbreviated form (most common in textbooks)
> - $\vert \psi \phi \rangle$ — compact Dirac notation
>
> All three notations mean the **exact same operation** in quantum mechanics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **two separate music players**, each with its own volume knob.

- **Player A** has volume settings corresponding to a 2-level system: *low* or *high* (a 2D vector).
- **Player B** has volume settings: *soft* or *loud* (also a 2D vector).

When you play them **independently**, you have a limited number of combinations. But the moment you **combine them into a stereo system**, suddenly the joint system has $2 \times 2 = 4$ distinct configurations: (low, soft), (low, loud), (high, soft), (high, loud). The combined "state space" is now **four-dimensional**.

The tensor product is the mathematical "wiring diagram" that lets us build this larger, composite sound system from two smaller, independent ones. In quantum computing, each "player" is a **qubit**, and the tensor product tells us how to describe the *joint behavior* of multiple qubits acting together.

| Property | Single Qubit | Two Independent Qubits (Tensor Product) |
|----------|--------------|----------------------------------------|
| Dimension of state space | $2$ | $2 \times 2 = 4$ |
| Basis states | $\vert 0 \rangle, \vert 1 \rangle$ | $\vert 00 \rangle, \vert 01 \rangle, \vert 10 \rangle, \vert 11 \rangle$ |
| Independent parameters | $2$ (one complex amplitude per basis) | $4$ (one complex amplitude per basis) |
| Constraint | $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$ | Sum of 4 squared moduli = **1** |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** $n$ qubits in the most general (entangled) case require $2^n$ complex amplitudes to describe fully. Tensor products let us build this exponential scaling cleanly.

## 1.3 Why Tensor Products Matter in Quantum Computing

The tensor product is **not** a luxury notation — it is the engine that drives all multi-qubit quantum mechanics. Here is why it is unavoidable:

1. **Multi-qubit states must be described.** A single qubit $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$ lives in $\mathbb{C}^2$. Two qubits together need to live in a 4-dimensional space. The tensor product $\mathbb{C}^2 \otimes \mathbb{C}^2 = \mathbb{C}^4$ is the only mathematically consistent way to construct this larger space.

2. **Composite operators must be defined.** When qubit A undergoes a Hadamard gate and qubit B undergoes an X gate, the joint operation is $H \otimes X$ — a $4 \times 4$ matrix acting on $\mathbb{C}^4$. You cannot write this without the tensor product.

3. **Entanglement is a tensor product phenomenon.** Bell states like $\vert \Phi^+ \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 11 \rangle)$ **cannot** be written as a simple product of two single-qubit states. They are *genuinely* composite objects that emerge only in the tensor product space. Entanglement — the resource powering quantum speedups — is mathematically a property of $\mathbb{C}^4$ that does not exist in two copies of $\mathbb{C}^2$ taken separately.

4. **It scales exponentially with the number of qubits.** A 10-qubit register lives in a $2^{10} = 1024$-dimensional Hilbert space. This is why simulating a 50-qubit quantum computer is already infeasible on classical hardware.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Rules of the Tensor Product

The tensor product is a **bilinear** map. This single property (bilinearity) plus linearity generates every formula you will ever need. The rules below are stated in the order KTU examiners expect students to apply them.

### Rule 1: Bilinearity (Distributivity over Addition)

For vectors $u, v \in V_A$ and $w, x \in V_B$:

$$
(u + v) \otimes w = u \otimes w + v \otimes w
$$

$$
u \otimes (w + x) = u \otimes w + u \otimes x
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most student errors come from forgetting that the tensor product is *bilinear*, not *linear* in both arguments. You cannot pull something out of *both* sides simultaneously unless it is a scalar.

### Rule 2: Scalar Multiplication

For any complex scalar $c \in \mathbb{C}$:

$$
c \cdot (u \otimes v) = (c \cdot u) \otimes v = u \otimes (c \cdot v)
$$

A scalar "floats freely" between either side of the tensor product.

### Rule 3: Tensor Product of Operators (Matrices)

If $A$ is an $m \times m$ matrix and $B$ is an $n \times n$ matrix, then $A \otimes B$ is an $(mn) \times (mn)$ matrix defined entry-wise as:

$$
A \otimes B = \begin{pmatrix} a_{11} B & a_{12} B & \cdots & a_{1m} B \\ a_{21} B & a_{22} B & \cdots & a_{2m} B \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ a_{m1} B & a_{m2} B & \cdots & a_{mm} B \end{pmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Formula:** Each element $a_{ij}$ of $A$ is replaced by the *entire* matrix $a_{ij} B$. This block-replacement rule is what KTU examiners test in Part B derivations.

### Rule 4: Action on a Vector

If $A$ acts on system $A$ and $B$ acts on system $B$, then the joint operator $A \otimes B$ acting on $\vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert \phi \rangle$ gives:

$$
(A \otimes B)(\vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert \phi \rangle) = (A \vert \psi \rangle) \otimes (B \vert \phi \rangle)
$$

### Rule 5: Basis Construction

If $\{\vert e_1 \rangle, \ldots, \vert e_m \rangle\}$ is a basis for $V_A$ and $\{\vert f_1 \rangle, \ldots, \vert f_n \rangle\}$ is a basis for $V_B$, then the tensor product basis for $V_A \otimes V_B$ is:

$$
\{\vert e_i \rangle \otimes \vert f_j \rangle : 1 \leq i \leq m,\ 1 \leq j \leq n\}
$$

This basis has $mn$ elements.

## 2.2 Important Algebraic Properties

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Consequence for Quantum Computing |
|----------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Associativity** | $(A \otimes B) \otimes C = A \otimes (B \otimes C)$ | We can drop parentheses and write $A \otimes B \otimes C$ unambiguously. |
| **Bilinearity** | Distributive over vector/matrix addition in each slot | Lets us expand products of sums. |
| **Mixed-product property** | $(A \otimes B)(C \otimes D) = (AC) \otimes (BD)$ | Lets us simplify products of joint operators. |
| **Transpose** | $(A \otimes B)^T = A^T \otimes B^T$ | Useful in proving properties of joint operators. |
| **Conjugate transpose** | $(A \otimes B)^\dagger = A^\dagger \otimes B^\dagger$ | Preserves unitarity: if $U, V$ are unitary, so is $U \otimes V$. |
| **Kronecker sum identity** | $\text{tr}(A \otimes B) = \text{tr}(A) \cdot \text{tr}(B)$ | Used in computing expectation values. |
| **Non-commutativity in general** | $A \otimes B \neq B \otimes A$ in general | Order of qubits matters: $\vert 01 \rangle \neq \vert 10 \rangle$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mixed-Product Property — the Most Tested Identity in KTU Exams:** For compatible matrices,
> $$
> (A \otimes B)(C \otimes D) = (AC) \otimes (BD)
> $$
> where $AC$ and $BD$ are ordinary matrix products. This lets you compute the action of a large joint gate on a state by working with the smaller pieces.

## 2.3 Standard Single-Qubit Operators (Reminder for Tensor Problems)

| Operator | Symbol | Matrix Representation |
|----------|--------|----------------------|
| Identity | $I$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ |
| Pauli-X (NOT) | $X$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ |
| Pauli-Y | $Y$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -i \\ i & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ |
| Pauli-Z | $Z$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ |
| Hadamard | $H$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ |
| Phase | $S$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & i \end{pmatrix}$ |
| T gate | $T$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & e^{i\pi/4} \end{pmatrix}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The tensor product is not abstract for its own sake. It directly enables:

- **Quantum circuit simulation:** Classical simulators of quantum computers represent the joint state of $n$ qubits as a $2^n$-dimensional complex vector, built by iteratively applying tensor products.
- **Quantum algorithm design:** Every multi-qubit gate in a real circuit (CNOT, Toffoli, SWAP, controlled rotations) is mathematically a $2^n \times 2^n$ unitary matrix constructed via tensor products of single- and two-qubit gates.
- **Quantum communication:** The density matrix formalism for bipartite systems uses $\rho_{AB} \in \mathcal{H}_A \otimes \mathcal{H}_B$. Entanglement measures (concurrence, negativity) are computed on these tensor product spaces.
- **Quantum error correction:** The stabilizer formalism — the backbone of codes like Shor's 9-qubit code and the surface code — is built on tensor products of Pauli operators.
- **VLSI / hardware mapping:** Modern quantum compilers (e.g., Qiskit, Cirq) decompose arbitrary unitaries into sequences of single- and two-qubit gates by exploiting the tensor product structure of multi-qubit operations.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the KTU Part B questions on this topic. Memorize the entries marked with ★.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| ★ 1 | Tensor product of two vectors | $\vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert \phi \rangle$ yields an $mn$-dim vector | Each component of $\vert \psi \rangle$ multiplies the entire $\vert \phi \rangle$ |
| ★ 2 | Tensor product of matrices | $A \otimes B$ is $(mn) \times (mn)$ with blocks $a_{ij} B$ | Block-replacement rule |
| ★ 3 | Mixed-product property | $(A \otimes B)(C \otimes D) = (AC) \otimes (BD)$ | The "KTU favorite" identity |
| ★ 4 | Bilinearity | $(u+v) \otimes w = u\otimes w + v \otimes w$ | Distributive in each slot |
| 5 | Scalar factor | $c(u \otimes v) = (cu) \otimes v = u \otimes (cv)$ | Scalars commute freely |
| 6 | Conjugate transpose | $(A \otimes B)^\dagger = A^\dagger \otimes B^\dagger$ | Preserves unitarity |
| 7 | Trace | $\text{tr}(A \otimes B) = \text{tr}(A) \cdot \text{tr}(B)$ | Used for expectation values |
| ★ 8 | $I \otimes I$ | $I_2 \otimes I_2 = I_4$ (the $4 \times 4$ identity) | Common in CNOT decompositions |
| ★ 9 | Dimension rule | $\dim(V \otimes W) = \dim V \cdot \dim W$ | $n$ qubits $\Rightarrow 2^n$ dim |
| 10 | Basis size | If $\dim V = m$, $\dim W = n$, basis size is $mn$ | Direct combinatorial |
