| 14 | Probability (Born Rule) | $P(k) = \vert\langle E_k \vert \psi(t)\rangle\vert^2 = \vert c_k e^{-iE_k t/\hbar}\vert^2 = \vert c_k\vert^2$ | Time-independent for eigenstates |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Superconducting Qubits (IBM, Google):** Quantum gates are physically implemented by applying microwave pulses whose duration and frequency correspond to a chosen Hamiltonian $\hat{H}$ and time $t$. The pulse shape *literally engineers* the unitary evolution $U(t) = \exp(-i\hat{H}t/\hbar)$.
- **Trapped Ion Systems (IonQ):** Laser-induced couplings between internal energy levels drive Hamiltonian evolution, generating arbitrary single- and two-qubit gates.
- **Quantum Simulation:** Richard Feynman's original 1982 vision — simulate quantum systems by engineering Hamiltonians on controllable quantum hardware. Used today in chemistry, materials science, and high-energy physics.
- **Quantum Algorithms:** Algorithms like Shor's and Grover's are sequences of engineered unitary evolutions, each approximating a desired mathematical operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Designing a quantum gate is equivalent to designing a **time-dependent Hamiltonian schedule** $H(t)$. This is the central task of **quantum control theory**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Schrödinger Equation to $U(t) = e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar}$

We begin with the **time-dependent Schrödinger equation**:

$$i\hbar \frac{\partial}{\partial t} |\psi(t)\rangle = \hat{H} |\psi(t)\rangle$$

Assume for now that the Hamiltonian $\hat{H}$ is **time-independent** (the most common case in KTU problems).

**Step 1 — Rearrange into a standard ODE form.**
Divide both sides by $i\hbar$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} |\psi(t)\rangle = \frac{1}{i\hbar} \hat{H} |\psi(t)\rangle = -\frac{i}{\hbar} \hat{H} |\psi(t)\rangle$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE** in time, with solution structure analogous to $dx/dt = ax \Rightarrow x(t) = e^{at} x(0)$.

**Step 2 — Guess an exponential solution.**
Propose:

$$|\psi(t)\rangle = e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar} |\psi(0)\rangle$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and verify.**
Differentiate the proposal with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}|\psi(t)\rangle = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left[e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar}\right] |\psi(0)\rangle$$

For a time-independent operator $\hat{H}$, the derivative of the matrix exponential is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar} = -\frac{i\hat{H}}{\hbar} e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}|\psi(t)\rangle = -\frac{i\hat{H}}{\hbar} e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar} |\psi(0)\rangle = -\frac{i\hat{H}}{\hbar} |\psi(t)\rangle$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the Schrödinger equation.**
Multiply both sides by $i\hbar$:

$$i\hbar \cdot \left(-\frac{i\hat{H}}{\hbar}\right)|\psi(t)\rangle = i\hbar \cdot \left(-\frac{i\hat{H}}{\hbar} |\psi(t)\rangle\right)$$

$$= (-i \cdot i) \hat{H} |\psi(t)\rangle = (-i^2) \hat{H} |\psi(t)\rangle = \hat{H} |\psi(t)\rangle$$

This is exactly the right-hand side of the Schrödinger equation. $\blacksquare$

Thus, the formal solution is verified:

$$\boxed{\,|\psi(t)\rangle = e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar}\, |\psi(0)\rangle \quad \Longrightarrow \quad U(t) = e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Spectral Decomposition of $U(t)$

Let $\{|E_k\rangle\}$ be the orthonormal eigenbasis of $\hat{H}$, with $\hat{H}|E_k\rangle = E_k|E_k\rangle$.

**Step 1 — Express any initial state in the eigenbasis:**

$$|\psi(0)\rangle = \sum_k c_k |E_k\rangle, \quad c_k = \langle E_k | \psi(0)\rangle$$

**Step 2 — Expand the exponential using Taylor series:**

$$e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \left(\frac{-i\hat{H}t}{\hbar}\right)^n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \left(\frac{-it}{\hbar}\right)^n \hat{H}^n$$

**Step 3 — Apply to an eigenstate.**
Using $\hat{H}^n |E_k\rangle = E_k^n |E_k\rangle$:

$$e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar} |E_k\rangle = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!}\left(\frac{-it}{\hbar}\right)^n E_k^n |E_k\rangle = \left[\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!}\left(\frac{-iE_k t}{\hbar}\right)^n\right] |E_k\rangle$$

The bracketed sum is exactly the Taylor expansion of the complex exponential:

$$= e^{-iE_k t/\hbar} |E_k\rangle$$

**Step 4 — Apply to the full state by linearity:**

$$|\psi(t)\rangle = \sum_k c_k \, e^{-iE_k t/\hbar} |E_k\rangle$$

In compact operator form:

$$\boxed{\,U(t) = \sum_k e^{-iE_k t/\hbar}\, |E_k\rangle\langle E_k|\,}$$

This is the **spectral (eigenvalue) decomposition** of the unitary operator. Each energy eigenstate $|E_k\rangle$ evolves only by acquiring the phase factor $e^{-iE_k t/\hbar}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Infinitesimal Evolution and the Generator of Time Translations

Consider an infinitesimal time step $dt$. Taylor-expand $U(dt)$:

$$U(dt) = e^{-i\hat{H}dt/\hbar} \approx I - \frac{i\hat{H}}{\hbar}dt + O(dt^2)$$

Acting on a state:

$$|\psi(dt)\rangle \approx \left(I - \frac{i\hat{H}}{\hbar}dt\right)|\psi(0)\rangle = |\psi(0)\rangle - \frac{i\hat{H}dt}{\hbar}|\psi(0)\rangle$$

Rearranging:

$$|\psi(dt)\rangle - |\psi(0)\rangle = -\frac{i\hat{H}dt}{\hbar}|\psi(0)\rangle$$

Dividing by $dt$ and letting $dt \to 0$:

$$\frac{\partial |\psi\rangle}{\partial t} = -\frac{i\hat{H}}{\hbar}|\psi\rangle$$

This is exactly the Schrödinger equation, confirming that $\hat{H}/\hbar$ is the **infinitesimal generator** of time translations, and the full finite evolution is obtained by exponentiation.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Generation of the Pauli-X (NOT) Gate

The NOT gate satisfies $X|0\rangle = |1\rangle$ and $X|1\rangle = |0\rangle$, where $X = \begin{pmatrix}0 & 1\\ 1 & 0\end{pmatrix}$.

We seek a Hamiltonian $H$ and time $t$ such that $U = e^{-iHt} = X$ (up to a global phase).

**Step 1 — Note that $X^2 = I$.** This means $X$ has eigenvalues $\pm 1$, with eigenvectors:
- $|+\rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle + |1\rangle)$ with eigenvalue $+1$
- $|-\rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle - |1\rangle)$ with eigenvalue $-1$

**Step 2 — Decompose $X$ in its eigenbasis:**

$$X = (+1)|+\rangle\langle+| + (-1)|-\rangle\langle-|$$

**Step 3 — To get $X$ from an exponential**, we need:

$$e^{-iHt}|+\rangle = e^{-i\lambda t}|+\rangle = +1 \cdot |+\rangle$$
$$e^{-iHt}|-\rangle = e^{-i\mu t}|-\rangle = -1 \cdot |-\rangle$$

This requires:
- $\lambda t = 0$ (mod $2\pi$) for the $+1$ eigenvalue
- $\mu t = \pi$ (mod $2\pi$) for the $-1$ eigenvalue

**Step 4 — Choose $H = \frac{\pi}{2} X$ (i.e., $\hat{H} = \frac{\pi \hbar}{2t} X$).**
The smallest non-trivial solution is $\mu - \lambda = \pi/t$, achieved by $H = \frac{\pi}{2t} X$:

$$e^{-i(\pi X / 2)} = \cos(\pi/2) I - i \sin(\pi/2) X = 0 \cdot I - i X = -iX$$

So:

$$\boxed{\,X = i \, e^{-i\pi X/2}\,}$$

The factor $i = e^{i\pi/2}$ is a **global phase** (physically unobservable). Hence, $X$ is implemented by letting a system with Hamiltonian $\hat{H} = \frac{\pi \hbar}{2t} \sigma_x$ evolve for time $t$.

This same method generalizes to **any quantum gate**: choose $H = \theta \sigma_k / (2t)$ to generate a rotation by angle $\theta$ about axis $k$:

$$R_k(\theta) = e^{-i\theta \sigma_k / 2}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A qubit starts in state $|\psi(0)\rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle + |1\rangle)$. The Hamiltonian is $\hat{H} = \hbar\omega \sigma_z / 2$, where $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^9$ rad/s (a typical superconducting qubit frequency). Find the state at time $t = 25$ ns.

**Step 1 — Compute $\omega t$:**

$$\omega t = 2\pi \times 10^9 \times 25 \times 10^{-9} = 50\pi \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the evolution operator:**

$$U(t) = e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar} = e^{-i(\sigma_z/2)\omega t} = e^{-i\sigma_z \cdot 25\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Apply spectral decomposition.** Since $\sigma_z |0\rangle = +|0\rangle$ and $\sigma_z |1\rangle = -|1\rangle$:

$$U(t) = e^{-i\omega t/2}|0\rangle\langle 0| + e^{+i\omega t/2}|1\rangle\langle 1|$$

With $\omega t = 50\pi$:

$$e^{-i\omega t/2} = e^{-i25\pi} = e^{-i\pi} = -1$$
$$e^{+i\omega t/2} = e^{+i25\pi} = e^{+i\pi} = -1$$

So $U(t) = -I$, meaning the state acquires only a global phase:

$$|\psi(t)\rangle = -|\psi(0)\rangle = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle + |1\rangle)$$

Since global phases are unobservable, the **measurement probabilities are unchanged**: $P(0) = P(1) = 1/2$. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** In numerical problems, always check whether the final state differs from the initial state by a **global phase**. If yes, the measurement outcomes are identical to the initial state.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Quantum Evolution as a Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional pipeline of quantum evolution, from Hamiltonian specification through state transformation to measurement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Specification: Hilbert Space H] --> B[Define Hamiltonian H on H]
    B --> C[Compute Energy Eigenvalues Ek and Eigenstates ket Ek]
    C --> D[Construct Unitary Operator U t = exp of -iHt over hbar]
    D --> E[Apply U t to Initial State ket psi 0]
    E --> F[Obtain Evolved State ket psi t = U t ket psi 0]
    F --> G{Measurement?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Born Rule: P k = modulus squared of bra Ek ket psi t]
    G -- No --> I[Continue Evolution: Apply U t2 at later time]
    I --> J[Compose: U t2 U t1 = U t1 plus t2]
    H --> K[Classical Output: Bitstring]
    J --> F
```

### 4.2 Mapping Between Hamiltonian and Quantum Gates

The next diagram maps how a Hamiltonian specification translates into standard quantum logic gates. Each physical mechanism (microwave pulse, laser, etc.) engineers a specific unitary.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Hamiltonian: H = omega sigma_x / 2] --> G1[Gate: Rx theta]
    H2[Hamiltonian: H = omega sigma_y / 2] --> G2[Gate: Ry theta]
    H3[Hamiltonian: H = omega sigma_z / 2] --> G3[Gate: Rz theta, Phase Gate]
    H4[Hamiltonian: H = pi/4 times I minus Z1 times I minus X2] --> G4[Gate: CNOT]
    H5[Hamiltonian: H = pi/8 times X1 X2 plus Y1 Y2] --> G5[Gate: iSWAP]
    G1 --> C[Universal Gate Set]
    G2 --> C
    G3 --> C
    G4 --> C
    G5 --> C
    C --> D[Approximate Any Unitary U to Arbitrary Precision]
```

### 4.3 State Evolution Timeline

This timeline shows how a generic superposition state evolves through time, with each component acquiring its own phase.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Time t equals 0: ket psi 0 = sum ck ket Ek] --> P1[Time t1: ket psi t1 = sum ck exp of -iEk t1 over hbar ket Ek]
    P1 --> P2[Time t2: ket psi t2 = sum ck exp of -iEk t2 over hbar ket Ek]
    P2 --> P3[Time tn: ket psi tn = sum ck exp of -iEk tn over hbar ket Ek]
    P3 --> P4[Interference: Phases encode computational result]
    P4 --> P5[Measurement via Born Rule]
```

### 4.4 Closed-Loop Quantum Control Architecture

This block diagram depicts the engineering architecture used in real quantum hardware for implementing controlled unitary evolution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Classical Control Computer] --> S2[Pulse Generator: AWG]
    S2 --> S3[Physical Channel: Microwave, Laser, Voltage]
    S3 --> S4[Qubit System: Engineered Hamiltonian H t]
    S4 --> S5[Unitary Evolution U t = T exp of integral H t dt]
    S5 --> S6[Qubit State ket psi t]
    S6 --> S7[Measurement Apparatus]
    S7 --> S8[Classical Readout]
    S8 --> S1
    S1 -.Feedback.- S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The **T exponent** (time-ordered exponential) is needed when $\hat{H}(t)$ does not commute with itself at different times: $\hat{H}(t_1)\hat{H}(t_2) \neq \hat{H}(t_2)\hat{H}(t_1)$. For time-independent Hamiltonians, it reduces to the ordinary exponential used above.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define unitary evolution. Why is unitarity essential for the validity of quantum computation?*

**Model Answer:**

Unitary evolution describes the time evolution of a closed quantum system via an operator $U(t) = e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar}$ that satisfies $U^\dagger U = UU^\dagger = I$. Unitarity is essential because:

1. **Probability conservation:** The Born rule requires that the total probability of all measurement outcomes equals 1. Unitarity preserves the inner product $\langle\psi(t)|\psi(t)\rangle = \langle\psi(0)|U^\dagger U|\psi(0)\rangle = \langle\psi(0)|\psi(0)\rangle = 1$.

2. **Reversibility:** Quantum computation requires every logical operation to be reversible (Landauer's principle). Unitarity guarantees that $U^{-1} = U^\dagger$ exists and is physically realizable.

3. **Closure of quantum mechanics:** Without unitarity, the postulates of quantum mechanics would be inconsistent — measurement outcomes would not be statistically reproducible.

Hence, **all quantum gates must be unitary operators**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for justification)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*State the time-dependent Schrödinger equation and explain the role of the Hamiltonian.*

**Model Answer:**

The time-dependent Schrödinger equation is:

$$i\hbar \frac{\partial}{\partial t}|\psi(t)\rangle = \hat{H}|\psi(t)\rangle$$

The **Hamiltonian** $\hat{H}$ plays the role of the **generator of time evolution**. It is a Hermitian operator representing the total energy (kinetic + potential) of the system. Physically:

- The Hamiltonian determines **how fast** a state rotates in Hilbert space (via its eigenvalues, the energy levels).
- The Hamiltonian determines **how much phase** each energy component acquires: $e^{-iE_k t/\hbar}$.
- In quantum hardware, $\hat{H}$ is **engineered externally** (by pulses, lasers, voltages) to implement desired unitary gates.

Without the Hamiltonian, the Schrödinger equation reduces to a trivial statement of conservation and there is no dynamics. *(3 marks: 1 for equation, 2 for role)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Derive the unitary time-evolution operator $U(t) = e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar}$ from the Schrödinger equation. Show that $U(t)$ is unitary if $\hat{H}$ is Hermitian.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A two-level system has the Hamiltonian $\hat{H} = \hbar\omega \sigma_x$. Compute the evolution operator $U(t)$. Find the state at time $t$ if $|\psi(0)\rangle = |0\rangle$.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Write the Schrödinger equation:**

$$i\hbar \frac{\partial}{\partial t}|\psi(t)\rangle = \hat{H}|\psi(t)\rangle$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}|\psi(t)\rangle = -\frac{i\hat{H}}{\hbar}|\psi(t)\rangle$$

**Step 3 — Integrate** with initial condition $|\psi(0)\rangle$:

$$|\psi(t)\rangle = \exp\!\left(-\frac{i\hat{H}t}{\hbar}\right)|\psi(0)\rangle$$

Define $U(t) = \exp(-i\hat{H}t/\hbar)$.

**Step 4 — Prove unitarity.** Take the Hermitian conjugate, noting $\hat{H}^\dagger = \hat{H}$:

$$U(t)^\dagger = \left(e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar}\right)^\dagger = e^{+i\hat{H}^\dagger t/\hbar} = e^{+i\hat{H}t/\hbar}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$U(t)^\dagger U(t) = e^{+i\hat{H}t/\hbar} e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar} = e^{\hat{H}t/\hbar - \hat{H}t/\hbar} = e^{0} = I$$

(The two exponentials commute because they are both functions of the same operator $\hat{H}$.) Therefore, $U(t)$ is unitary. $\blacksquare$

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $U(t)$.**
With $\hat{H} = \hbar\omega \sigma_x$:

$$U(t) = e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar} = e^{-i\omega \sigma_x t}$$

**Step 2 — Use the identity** $e^{-i\theta\sigma_x} = \cos\theta \, I - i \sin\theta \, \sigma_x$ (derived from Taylor series + $\sigma_x^2 = I$):

$$U(t) = \cos(\omega t) I - i \sin(\omega t) \sigma_x$$

In matrix form ($\sigma_x = \begin{pmatrix}0 & 1\\1 & 0\end{pmatrix}$):

$$U(t) = \begin{pmatrix}\cos\omega t & -i\sin\omega t\\-i\sin\omega t & \cos\omega t\end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to $|0\rangle = \begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}$:**

$$|\psi(t)\rangle = U(t)|0\rangle = \begin{pmatrix}\cos\omega t\\-i\sin\omega t\end{pmatrix} = \cos(\omega t)|0\rangle - i\sin(\omega t)|1\rangle$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**
- At $t = 0$: $|\psi\rangle = |0\rangle$ ✓
- At $t = \pi/(2\omega)$: $|\psi\rangle = -i|1\rangle$ — population fully transferred from $|0\rangle$ to $|1\rangle$ (a NOT gate, up to phase).
- At $t = \pi/\omega$: $|\psi\rangle = -|0\rangle$ — back to $|0\rangle$ with global phase $-1$.

This is a **Rabi oscillation** at frequency $\omega$ — the physical mechanism underlying single-qubit gates in many quantum hardware platforms. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Explain the spectral decomposition of the unitary evolution operator. Why do energy eigenstates acquire only a global phase under evolution?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Consider the Hamiltonian $\hat{H} = \hbar\omega_0 \sigma_z / 2$. Compute $U(t)$ and show that it generates the phase gate $P(\phi) = \text{diag}(1, e^{i\phi})$. Identify the relationship between $t$, $\omega_0$, and $\phi$.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Recall the spectral theorem.** Any Hermitian operator (including $\hat{H}$) admits an orthonormal eigenbasis with real eigenvalues:

$$\hat{H} = \sum_k E_k |E_k\rangle\langle E_k|, \quad \hat{H}|E_k\rangle = E_k|E_k\rangle$$

**Step 2 — Exponentiate:**

$$U(t) = e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar} = \sum_k e^{-iE_k t/\hbar} |E_k\rangle\langle E_k|$$

**Step 3 — Apply to an eigenstate:** Using orthonormality $\langle E_j|E_k\rangle = \delta_{jk}$:

$$U(t)|E_k\rangle = \sum_j e^{-iE_j t/\hbar} |E_j\rangle\langle E_j|E_k\rangle = e^{-iE_k t/\hbar}|E_k\rangle$$

The state $|E_k\rangle$ is mapped to itself multiplied by the scalar $e^{-iE_k t/\hbar}$, which is a **global phase** (a complex number of unit modulus attached to the entire state vector).

**Step 4 — Why is this physically unobservable?**
According to the Born rule, the probability of measuring outcome $j$ is:

$$P(j) = |\langle E_j|\psi(t)\rangle|^2 = |\langle E_j|e^{-iE_k t/\hbar}|E_k\rangle|^2 = |e^{-iE_k t/\hbar}|^2 \cdot |\langle E_j|E_k\rangle|^2 = 1 \cdot \delta_{jk}$$

Global phases **factor out** of all measurement probabilities. Hence, an energy eigenstate shows no observable time dependence — it is a **stationary state**. This is why the energy eigenbasis is sometimes called the "stationary basis". $\blacksquare$

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $U(t)$.**
$\sigma_z$ has eigenvalues $\pm 1$ with eigenstates $|0\rangle$ and $|1\rangle$. Therefore:

$$U(t) = e^{-i\omega_0 \sigma_z t / 2} = e^{-i\omega_0 t/2}|0\rangle\langle 0| + e^{+i\omega_0 t/2}|1\rangle\langle 1|$$

In matrix form:

$$U(t) = \begin{pmatrix}e^{-i\omega_0 t/2} & 0\\0 & e^{+i\omega_0 t/2}\end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Factor out the global phase** $e^{-i\omega_0 t/2}$ (physically unobservable):

$$U(t) = e^{-i\omega_0 t/2} \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\0 & e^{i\omega_0 t}\end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the phase gate.**
The remaining diagonal matrix is the **phase gate**:

$$P(\phi) = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\0 & e^{i\phi}\end{pmatrix}$$

Comparing entries, we identify $\phi = \omega_0 t$.

**Step 4 — Relationship:**

$$\boxed{\,\phi = \omega_0 t \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad t = \frac{\phi}{\omega_0}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.**
To implement a phase gate of angle $\phi$ on a qubit, one applies a Hamiltonian $\hat{H} = \hbar\omega_0 \sigma_z / 2$ for a duration $t = \phi/\omega_0$. The qubit's $|0\rangle$ and $|1\rangle$ states accumulate a **relative phase** $\phi$, while the global phase is discarded. This is the standard implementation of $R_z(\phi)$ in quantum hardware. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the global phase.** Many students write $U(t) = \text{diag}(e^{-i\omega_0 t/2}, e^{+i\omega_0 t/2})$ and stop. Examiners specifically award marks for factoring out the global phase and identifying the *physical* phase gate (1–2 marks lost otherwise).
> 2. **Matrix form of $\sigma_z$:** Confusing the rows/columns of $|0\rangle$ and $|1\rangle$ representations. **KTU convention:** $|0\rangle = (1,0)^T$ and $|1\rangle = (0,1)^T$, giving $\sigma_z = \text{diag}(1, -1)$.
> 3. **Mixing up Hamiltonians.** A common error is writing $H = \hbar\omega\sigma_z$ instead of $\hbar\omega\sigma_z/2$. The factor of $1/2$ comes from the convention that Pauli matrices have eigenvalues $\pm 1$.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify unitarity** when asked to "show $U$ is unitary." Always compute $U^\dagger U = I$ explicitly.
> 5. **Neglecting Hermiticity of $\hat{H}$.** If $\hat{H}$ is not Hermitian in a problem, the evolution is not unitary and the standard solution fails — state this explicitly in your answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quantum evolution is unitary:** $U(t) = e^{-i\hat{H}t/\hbar}$, with $U^\dagger U = I$.
- **Hamiltonian $\hat{H}$ is Hermitian** with real eigenvalues (allowed energies $E_k$).
- **Eigenstates are stationary:** they evolve only by a global (unobservable) phase $e^{-iE_k t/\hbar}$.
- **Superpositions evolve by acquiring relative phases:** $|\psi(t)\rangle = \sum_k c_k e^{-iE_k t/\hbar} |E_k\rangle$. This interference is the heart of quantum computation.
- **All quantum gates are unitary evolutions:** choosing $\hat{H} = \theta\sigma_k/(2t)$ produces rotation $R_k(\theta) = e^{-i\theta\sigma_k/2}$.
- **Schrödinger equation** is **first-order in time** — initial state fully determines future evolution.
- **Schrödinger equation is linear** — superposition principle is preserved.
- **Reversibility:** $U(t)^{-1} = U(-t) = U(t)^\dagger$. Quantum circuits can be run "backward".
- **Infinitesimal evolution:** $U(dt) \approx I - i\hat{H}dt/\hbar$ — Taylor expansion reveals $\hat{H}/\hbar$ as the **generator** of time translation.
- **For time-dependent Hamiltonians**, use the **time-ordered exponential**: $U(t) = \mathcal{T}\exp\left(-\tfrac{i}{\hbar}\int_0^t \hat{H}(s)ds\right)$.
- **Pauli decomposition of any single-qubit Hamiltonian:** $H = a_0 I + a_1 \sigma_x + a_2 \sigma_y + a_3 \sigma_z$, with $a_i \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **Engineering equivalence:** Designing a quantum gate = engineering a Hamiltonian schedule $H(t)$ for a chosen duration. This is the central task of **quantum control**.
- **Rabi oscillations** ($\hat{H} \propto \sigma_x$) and **phase accumulation** ($\hat{H} \propto \sigma_z$) are the two fundamental physical mechanisms underlying single-qubit gates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantum Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT634 - QUANTUM COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quantum Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (Measurement Postulate)

In quantum mechanics, **measurement** is described by a set of **measurement operators** $\{M_m\}$ acting on the state space of the system being observed. The index $m$ refers to the measurement outcomes that may be observed in the experiment. If the state of the quantum system is $\vert \psi \rangle$ immediately before the measurement, then the probability that the result $m$ occurs is given by the **Born Rule**:

$$P(m) = \langle \psi \vert M_m^\dagger M_m \vert \psi \rangle$$

The **post-measurement state** of the system, given that outcome $m$ was observed, is:

$$\vert \psi' \rangle = \frac{M_m \vert \psi \rangle}{\sqrt{\langle \psi \vert M_m^\dagger M_m \vert \psi \rangle}}$$

The measurement operators must satisfy the **completeness relation**:

$$\sum_m M_m^\dagger M_m = I$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Projective (Von Neumann) Measurement:** A special, very common case occurs when the measurement operators are **projectors** $P_m$ (Hermitian, idempotent: $P_m^2 = P_m$) that sum to identity. Then $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert P_m \vert \psi \rangle$ and the post-measurement state is $\frac{P_m \vert \psi \rangle}{\sqrt{P(m)}}$. Projective measurements always correspond to **observables** (Hermitian operators) with spectral decomposition $M = \sum_m m P_m$.

## 1.2 Intuition — The "Spinning Coin in a Dark Box" Analogy

Imagine you place a perfectly fair coin inside an opaque sealed box and **shaking it gently** keeps it spinning. As long as the box is closed, the coin is neither Heads nor Tails — it is in a **superposition of both** with complex probability amplitudes. The moment you **open the box and look** (perform a measurement), the spinning stops instantaneously and you observe exactly one face. The *probability* of seeing Heads versus Tails is governed by the squared magnitude of the coin's internal "amplitudes" (the **Born rule**).

| Feature | Closed Box (Pre-Measurement) | Opened Box (Post-Measurement) |
|---|---|---|
| State | Pure superposition $\alpha \vert H \rangle + \beta \vert T \rangle$ | Collapsed eigenstate $\vert H \rangle$ or $\vert T \rangle$ |
| Information | All amplitudes preserved | One outcome, one classical bit |
| Reversibility | Unitary — fully reversible | Irreversible — wavefunction collapse |
| Repeatability | Yes, if untouched | No — second measurement gives same result |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the squared modulus?** Interference experiments (e.g., double-slit) demand that the wavefunction's amplitude $a_m$ — not its probability — be the fundamental quantity. Born postulated that probabilities are the *squared magnitudes* $|a_m|^2$ of these amplitudes, ensuring the rules of probability theory (real, non-negative, sum to 1) are satisfied while preserving linear superposition at the amplitude level.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Born Rule Constant:** implicit (probability is dimensionless, hence $|a_m|^2$ is dimensionless).
- **Inner product identity:** $\langle \psi \vert \psi \rangle = 1$ (normalization).
- **Trace-preservation condition:** $\sum_m P(m) = 1$.
- **Standard metric for distinguishability:** **Trace distance** $D(\rho, \sigma) = \frac{1}{2} \text{Tr}\vert \rho - \sigma \vert$ and **Fidelity** $F(\rho, \sigma) = \left( \text{Tr} \sqrt{\sqrt{\rho} \sigma \sqrt{\rho}} \right)^2$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Part Structure of Quantum Measurement

1. **Encoding** — System is described by a state $\vert \psi \rangle$ in Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$.
2. **Measurement Operators** — A set $\{M_m\}$ satisfying $\sum_m M_m^\dagger M_m = I$ defines the measurement.
3. **Stochastic Collapse** — Outcome $m$ is realized with probability $P(m)$ and state instantaneously projects onto the corresponding sub-space.

### Key Logical Steps in a Projective Measurement

- **Step 1 — Spectral Decomposition:** Any observable $M$ (Hermitian operator) can be diagonalized as $M = \sum_m m P_m$, where $P_m$ are orthogonal projectors.
- **Step 2 — Apply Born Rule:** Compute $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert P_m \vert \psi \rangle$.
- **Step 3 — State Collapse:** The state becomes $\frac{P_m \vert \psi \rangle}{\sqrt{P(m)}}$, which lies entirely in the eigenspace of $m$.
- **Step 4 — Expectation & Variance:** The mean outcome is $\langle M \rangle = \langle \psi \vert M \vert \psi \rangle$ and uncertainty is quantified by standard deviation $\Delta M = \sqrt{\langle M^2 \rangle - \langle M \rangle^2}$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Rule

- **Why Hermitian?** Eigenvalues of Hermitian operators are real, matching the requirement that measurement outcomes be real numbers.
- **Why projectors?** Projectors are idempotent ($P^2 = P$), guaranteeing that *repeating* the same measurement yields the same outcome (projective repeatability).
- **Why $\sum M_m^\dagger M_m = I$?** This is the *conservation of total probability*. Since at least one outcome *must* occur, $\sum_m P(m) = 1$.
- **Why squared modulus?** Probabilities must be real and non-negative, but the complex amplitudes $a_m$ are required by linearity of quantum evolution — squaring resolves the sign ambiguity.

## 2.3 POVM (Positive Operator-Valued Measure)

A **POVM** is a set of positive semi-definite operators $\{E_m\}$ with $E_m \geq 0$ and $\sum_m E_m = I$. The probability of outcome $m$ is:

$$P(m) = \langle \psi \vert E_m \vert \psi \rangle$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **POVMs are strictly more general than projective measurements.** They do not necessarily specify a post-measurement state but allow finer discrimination between non-orthogonal states. Every projective measurement is a POVM (take $E_m = P_m$), but not every POVM corresponds to a projective measurement (e.g., the Helstrom measurement for optimal discrimination of two non-orthogonal pure states).

## 2.4 Compatibility of Observables

Two observables $A$ and $B$ are **compatible** (can be measured simultaneously with certainty) if and only if $[A, B] = 0$. Otherwise, the **Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle** holds:

$$\Delta A \cdot \Delta B \geq \frac{1}{2} \vert \langle [A, B] \rangle \vert$$

Canonical example: $[X, Y] = 2iZ$, leading to $\Delta X \cdot \Delta Y \geq 1$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Born Rule (General) | $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert M_m^\dagger M_m \vert \psi \rangle$ | Probability of outcome $m$ (dimensionless) |
| Born Rule (Projective) | $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert P_m \vert \psi \rangle$ | Using projector $P_m$ |
| Post-Measurement State | $\vert \psi' \rangle = \dfrac{M_m \vert \psi \rangle}{\sqrt{\langle \psi \vert M_m^\dagger M_m \vert \psi \rangle}}$ | State after outcome $m$ |
| Completeness (Operator) | $\sum_m M_m^\dagger M_m = I$ | Probabilities sum to 1 |
| Spectral Decomposition | $M = \sum_m m P_m$ | Observable from projectors |
| POVM Element | $E_m = M_m^\dagger M_m$ | Always positive semi-definite |
| POVM Probability | $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert E_m \vert \psi \rangle$ | General measurement probability |
| Expectation Value | $\langle M \rangle = \langle \psi \vert M \vert \psi \rangle$ | Mean of many measurements |
| Variance | $(\Delta M)^2 = \langle M^2 \rangle - \langle M \rangle^2$ | Spread of measurement outcomes |
| Uncertainty Principle | $\Delta A \cdot \Delta B \geq \frac{1}{2}\vert \langle [A, B] \rangle \vert$ | Fundamental lower bound |
| Hadamard Basis | $\vert \pm \rangle = \dfrac{\vert 0 \rangle \pm \vert 1 \rangle}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Diagonal / anti-diagonal basis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Quantum measurement underlies **quantum metrology** (precision sensing beyond the standard quantum limit — used in LIGO), **quantum key distribution** (BB84 protocol's security rests on measurement in conjugate bases), and **quantum error correction** (syndrome measurements are projective measurements that detect errors without destroying encoded logical information).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Born Rule for a Single-Qubit State in Computational Basis

**Given:** A qubit in normalized state $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$, with $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$.

**Measurement:** In the computational (Z) basis, with projectors $P_0 = \vert 0 \rangle \langle 0 \vert$ and $P_1 = \vert 1 \rangle \langle 1 \vert$.

**Step 1 — Verify projector orthonormality and completeness:**

$$P_0 + P_1 = \vert 0 \rangle \langle 0 \vert + \vert 1 \rangle \langle 1 \rangle = I$$

This is the standard closure relation for the computational basis.

**Step 2 — Compute $P(0)$ using the Born rule:**

$$P(0) = \langle \psi \vert P_0 \vert \psi \rangle$$

Substitute $\vert \psi \rangle$ and use linearity of the inner product:

$$P(0) = (\alpha^* \langle 0 \vert + \beta^* \langle 1 \vert) \, (\vert 0 \rangle \langle 0 \vert) \, (\alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle)$$

The projector $P_0$ annihilates the $\vert 1 \rangle$ component:

$$P(0) = (\alpha^* \langle 0 \vert + \beta^* \langle 1 \vert) \, (\alpha \vert 0 \rangle) = \alpha^* \alpha \langle 0 \vert 0 \rangle = \vert \alpha \vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute $P(1)$:**

$$P(1) = \langle \psi \vert P_1 \vert \psi \rangle = (\alpha^* \langle 0 \vert + \beta^* \langle 1 \vert) (\beta \vert 1 \rangle) = \vert \beta \vert^2$$

**Step 4 — Verify normalization (conservation of probability):**

$$P(0) + P(1) = \vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Determine post-measurement states:**

- If outcome $0$ is observed: $\vert \psi' \rangle = \dfrac{P_0 \vert \psi \rangle}{\sqrt{P(0)}} = \dfrac{\alpha \vert 0 \rangle}{\vert \alpha \vert} = e^{i \arg(\alpha)} \vert 0 \rangle \equiv \vert 0 \rangle$ (up to a global phase).
- If outcome $1$ is observed: $\vert \psi' \rangle = \dfrac{P_1 \vert \psi \rangle}{\sqrt{P(1)}} = \dfrac{\beta \vert 1 \rangle}{\vert \beta \vert} = \vert 1 \rangle$.

This proves that **measurement always collapses a superposition into one of the basis eigenstates**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Measurement in the Hadamard (X) Basis

**Given:** The state $\vert \psi \rangle = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \vert 0 \rangle + \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \vert 1 \rangle$.

**Measurement basis:** $\vert + \rangle = \dfrac{\vert 0 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $\vert - \rangle = \dfrac{\vert 0 \rangle - \vert 1 \rangle}{\sqrt{2}}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the inner product $\langle + \vert \psi \rangle$:**

$$\langle + \vert \psi \rangle = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} (\langle 0 \vert + \langle 1 \vert) \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \vert 0 \rangle + \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \vert 1 \rangle \right)$$

Distribute the inner product:

$$= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \langle 0 \vert 0 \rangle + \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \langle 0 \vert 1 \rangle + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \langle 1 \vert 0 \rangle + \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \langle 1 \vert 1 \rangle \right)$$

Using orthonormality $\langle i \vert j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$:

$$= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} + \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \right) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{6}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P(+)$:**

$$P(+) = \vert \langle + \vert \psi \rangle \vert^2 = \dfrac{(1 + \sqrt{2})^2}{6} = \dfrac{1 + 2\sqrt{2} + 2}{6} = \dfrac{3 + 2\sqrt{2}}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\langle - \vert \psi \rangle$:**

$$\langle - \vert \psi \rangle = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} - \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \right) = \dfrac{1 - \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{6}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $P(-)$:**

$$P(-) = \vert \langle - \vert \psi \rangle \vert^2 = \dfrac{(1 - \sqrt{2})^2}{6} = \dfrac{1 - 2\sqrt{2} + 2}{6} = \dfrac{3 - 2\sqrt{2}}{6}$$

**Step 5 — Verify probability sum:**

$$P(+) + P(-) = \dfrac{3 + 2\sqrt{2} + 3 - 2\sqrt{2}}{6} = \dfrac{6}{6} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Numerical estimate:** $2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828$, hence $P(+) \approx 0.971$ and $P(-) \approx 0.029$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Expectation Value of a Pauli Operator (Bloch Sphere Link)

**Given:** A general qubit state on the Bloch sphere: $\vert \psi \rangle = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \vert 0 \rangle + e^{i\phi} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \vert 1 \rangle$.

**Goal:** Compute $\langle Z \rangle = \langle \psi \vert Z \vert \psi \rangle$.

**Step 1 — Write $Z \vert \psi \rangle$:**

$$Z \vert \psi \rangle = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot (+1) \vert 0 \rangle + e^{i\phi} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot (-1) \vert 1 \rangle = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \vert 0 \rangle - e^{i\phi} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \vert 1 \rangle$$

**Step 2 — Take the inner product with $\langle \psi \vert$:**

$$\langle Z \rangle = \left[ \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \langle 0 \vert + e^{-i\phi} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \langle 1 \vert \right] \left[ \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \vert 0 \rangle - e^{i\phi} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \vert 1 \rangle \right]$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the products:**

$$\langle Z \rangle = \cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \langle 0 \vert 0 \rangle - e^{i\phi} \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \langle 0 \vert 1 \rangle + e^{-i\phi} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \langle 1 \vert 0 \rangle - e^{i\phi} e^{-i\phi} \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \langle 1 \vert 1 \rangle$$

The cross terms vanish (orthogonal basis), and $e^{i\phi} e^{-i\phi} = 1$:

$$\langle Z \rangle = \cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) - \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \cos \theta$$

This proves that the **expectation of $Z$ on the Bloch sphere equals $\cos \theta$** — exactly the $z$-coordinate of the Bloch vector. Identical derivations yield $\langle X \rangle = \sin\theta \cos\phi$ and $\langle Y \rangle = \sin\theta \sin\phi$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Projective vs POVM Measurements

| Feature | Projective Measurement | POVM |
|---|---|---|
| **Operators** | Hermitian projectors $P_m$ with $P_m^2 = P_m$ | Positive semi-definite operators $E_m \geq 0$ |
| **Completeness** | $\sum_m P_m = I$ | $\sum_m E_m = I$ |
| **Probability Rule** | $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert P_m \vert \psi \rangle$ | $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert E_m \vert \psi \rangle$ |
| **Post-Measurement State** | Specified: $\frac{P_m \vert \psi \rangle}{\sqrt{P(m)}}$ | Not specified (only the outcome probabilities) |
| **Implements Observables** | Yes — corresponds to Hermitian operator $M = \sum_m m P_m$ | Not necessarily — elements may not be orthogonal |
| **Special Relationship** | Special case of POVM with $E_m = P_m$ | Generalization; strictly more powerful for state discrimination |
| **Number of Outcomes** | Limited by $\dim(\mathcal{H})$ | Can be larger (e.g., 3 outcomes on a qubit) |
| **Typical Use Case** | Born rule, eigenbasis measurements, syndrome extraction | Optimal discrimination of non-orthogonal states |
| **Repeatability** | Yes — same outcome on immediate re-measurement | No — re-measurement may give different outcome |

> [!NOTE]
> **When to use POVM:** POVMs are essential in **quantum state discrimination** tasks (e.g., Helstrom bound for distinguishing two pure states with minimum error) and in **quantum cryptography** (the BB84 protocol's security analysis uses POVMs to bound an eavesdropper's information gain).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Quantum Measurement Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Quantum State psi in Hilbert Space H] --> B[Choose Measurement Basis]
    B --> C[Construct Measurement Operators Mm]
    C --> D{Born Rule Sampling}
    D --> E[Outcome m with Probability Pm]
    D --> F[Post-Measurement State psi prime]
    E --> G[Classical Register Stores m]
    F --> H[Quantum Register Collapsed to Eigenstate]
    G --> I[Downstream Classical Processing]
    H --> I

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#2E5C8A,color:#000000
    style B fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style C fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style D fill:#F0E5FF,stroke:#6A0DAD,color:#000000
    style E fill:#E5F9E5,stroke:#228B22,color:#000000
    style F fill:#E5F9E5,stroke:#228B22,color:#000000
    style G fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style H fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style I fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#404040,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation of the pipeline:** A quantum state (left) is fed into a measurement basis selection (e.g., computational or Hadamard). Measurement operators $\{M_m\}$ are constructed accordingly. The Born rule then samples a *single* stochastic outcome, simultaneously producing (i) a classical record of outcome $m$ and (ii) a collapsed quantum state $\vert \psi' \rangle$ ready for further operations.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — POVM-Based State Discrimination

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Unknown Quantum State from Set psi 1 to psi n] --> B[Design POVM Elements E 1 to E n]
    B --> C[Apply Measurement on Ancilla Coupled System]
    C --> D{Outcome Index m}
    D --> E[Infer State is psi m with Probability Pm]
    D --> F[Discard Ancilla]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#2E5C8A,color:#000000
    style B fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style C fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style D fill:#F0E5FF,stroke:#6A0DAD,color:#000000
    style E fill:#E5F9E5,stroke:#228B22,color:#000000
    style F fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#404040,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:** When states are non-orthogonal (e.g., $\vert + \rangle$ vs $\vert 0 \rangle$), no projective measurement can perfectly distinguish them. Instead, an ancilla-based POVM is engineered by coupling the unknown state to a helper qubit, then performing a joint projective measurement. The POVM elements $\{E_m\}$ — defined on the original system alone — can have outcomes $> 2$ for a qubit, enabling optimal discrimination.

## 4.3 Sequential Process Map — Single Qubit Computational Basis Measurement

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Start: Qubit in State alpha times 0 plus beta times 1] --> S2[Apply Projector P0 equals 0 bra 0]
    S2 --> S3[Compute P zero equals alpha squared modulus]
    S1 --> S4[Apply Projector P1 equals 1 bra 1]
    S4 --> S5[Compute P one equals beta squared modulus]
    S3 --> S6[Sample random number in 0 1]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7{Outcome m equals 0}
    S6 --> S8{Outcome m equals 1}
    S7 --> S9[Collapse state to 0]
    S8 --> S10[Collapse state to 1]
    S9 --> S11[Output classical bit 0]
    S10 --> S12[Output classical bit 1]

    style S1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#2E5C8A,color:#000000
    style S2 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style S3 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style S4 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style S5 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    style S6 fill:#F0E5FF,stroke:#6A0DAD,color:#000000
    style S7 fill:#E5F9E5,stroke:#228B22,color:#000000
    style S8 fill:#E5F9E5,stroke:#228B22,color:#000000
    style S9 fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style S10 fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style S11 fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#404040,color:#000000
    style S12 fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#404040,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams?** In quantum hardware (IBM Qiskit, Rigetti, IonQ), this pipeline is the literal flow executed by the **Measurement Gate** in a quantum circuit — the qubit's state vector is converted to classical bits by sampling the Born distribution once, irreversibly collapsing the state.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the Born rule for quantum measurement. Why is the squared modulus used instead of the raw amplitude?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Born rule** states that given a quantum state $\vert \psi \rangle$ and a projective measurement with projectors $\{P_m\}$, the probability of obtaining outcome $m$ is:

$$P(m) = \langle \psi \vert P_m \vert \psi \rangle$$

For a pure state $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$, this reduces to $P(0) = \vert \alpha \vert^2$ and $P(1) = \vert \beta \vert^2$. The squared modulus is used because (i) probability must be a **real, non-negative** quantity, while amplitudes are inherently complex, and (ii) the rules of probability theory (e.g., total probability summing to 1) must be satisfied. The squaring operation $|z|^2 = z^* z$ converts complex amplitudes to real probabilities while preserving the linear (interference) structure of quantum evolution at the amplitude level.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between projective measurement and POVM. Give one example where POVM is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Projective Measurement | POVM |
|---|---|---|
| Operators | Orthogonal projectors $P_m$ | Positive operators $E_m$ |
| Post-measurement state | Well-defined | Not specified |

**Example:** POVM is preferred in **discriminating two non-orthogonal pure states** (e.g., $\vert \psi_1 \rangle$ and $\vert \psi_2 \rangle$ with $\langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \neq 0$). The Helstrom POVM achieves the minimum error probability, which no projective measurement can match.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **measurement postulate of quantum mechanics**. Define a projective measurement, give the probability of each outcome, and describe the post-measurement state. Discuss with reference to Hermitian observables and the spectral decomposition theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the qubit $\vert \psi \rangle = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \vert 0 \rangle + \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \vert 1 \rangle$.
- (i) Compute the probabilities of measuring in the **computational basis** $\{|0\rangle, |1\rangle\}$.
- (ii) Compute the probabilities of measuring in the **Hadamard basis** $\{ \vert + \rangle, \vert - \rangle \}$.
- (iii) Compute the **expectation value of Pauli X** and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Measurement Postulate & Projective Measurement (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A projective measurement is described by an **observable** $M$, a Hermitian operator on the state space. $M$ has spectral decomposition:

$$M = \sum_m m P_m$$

where $P_m$ is the projector onto the eigenspace corresponding to eigenvalue $m$, with properties:
- **Hermitian:** $P_m^\dagger = P_m$
- **Idempotent:** $P_m^2 = P_m$
- **Orthogonal:** $P_m P_n = \delta_{mn} P_n$
- **Complete:** $\sum_m P_m = I$

**Probability & Post-Measurement State (2 marks):** For a system in state $\vert \psi \rangle$, the probability of outcome $m$ is $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert P_m \vert \psi \rangle$, and the post-measurement state is:

$$\vert \psi' \rangle = \frac{P_m \vert \psi \rangle}{\sqrt{P(m)}}$$

**Why Hermitian? (1 mark):** Hermitian operators have real eigenvalues, which is required since measurement outcomes are physical (real) numbers.

**Example (2 marks):** For a single qubit, the observable $Z = \vert 0 \rangle \langle 0 \vert - \vert 1 \rangle \langle 1 \rangle$ has projectors $P_0 = \vert 0 \rangle \langle 0 \vert$ and $P_1 = \vert 1 \rangle \langle 1 \vert$ with eigenvalues $+1$ and $-1$ respectively.

---

**(b) Worked Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Computational Basis (2 marks):**
- $\alpha = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, $\beta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$
- $P(0) = \vert \alpha \vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{3}$
- $P(1) = \vert \beta \vert^2 = \dfrac{2}{3}$

**(ii) Hadamard Basis (3 marks):**
- $\langle + \vert \psi \rangle = \frac{1+\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{6}}$, so $P(+) = \frac{3+2\sqrt{2}}{6} \approx 0.971$
- $\langle - \vert \psi \rangle = \frac{1-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{6}}$, so $P(-) = \frac{3-2\sqrt{2}}{6} \approx 0.029$

**(iii) Expectation of X (2 marks):**

$$X = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad X \vert \psi \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1/\sqrt{3} \\ \sqrt{2/3} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{2/3} \\ 1/\sqrt{3} \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\langle X \rangle = \langle \psi \vert X \vert \psi \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} + \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3} \approx 0.943$$

**Interpretation:** Since $\langle X \rangle \approx 0.943$ is positive, the state is strongly aligned along the $+\hat{x}$ direction of the Bloch sphere, consistent with the high probability ($\approx 0.971$) of measuring $\vert + \rangle$ in the Hadamard basis.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **POVM (Positive Operator-Valued Measure)**. Show mathematically that any projective measurement is a special case of a POVM. List two situations where POVM is strictly more useful than projective measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The state $\vert \psi \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2} \vert 0 \rangle + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \vert 1 \rangle$ is prepared. 
- (i) Compute the **expectation value of Pauli Z** ($\langle Z \rangle$) and the **standard deviation** $\Delta Z$.
- (ii) If the same state is now measured in the **Hadamard basis**, find the probabilities of obtaining $\vert + \rangle$ and $\vert - \rangle$. 
- (iii) Justify why the two measurement bases give different probability distributions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) POVM Theory (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A POVM is a set of positive semi-definite operators $\{E_m\}$ such that:
- $E_m \geq 0$ (positive semi-definite, i.e., all eigenvalues $\geq 0$)
- $\sum_m E_m = I$ (completeness)
- $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert E_m \vert \psi \rangle$

**Projective ⊂ POVM (2 marks):** Given a projective measurement with projectors $\{P_m\}$, define $E_m = P_m$. Then:
- $E_m = P_m$ is Hermitian and satisfies $P_m^2 = P_m$, hence $E_m^2 = E_m$ implies $E_m \geq 0$ ✓
- $\sum_m E_m = \sum_m P_m = I$ ✓
- $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert P_m \vert \psi \rangle = \langle \psi \vert E_m \vert \psi \rangle$ ✓

Therefore, projective measurements are a special subset of POVMs.

**When POVM is More Useful (3 marks):**
1. **Non-orthogonal state discrimination:** POVMs can optimally distinguish between states like $\vert \psi_1 \rangle$ and $\vert \psi_2 \rangle$ with $\langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \neq 0$ (achieves the Helstrom bound, which is unattainable projectively).
2. **More outcomes than dimension:** POVM can have $N > \dim(\mathcal{H})$ outcomes, useful in **quantum state tomography** and **optimal unambiguous state discrimination**.

---

**(b) Expectation and Hadamard Measurement (7 marks):**

**(i) Expectation of Z and Standard Deviation (3 marks):**

$$Z \vert \psi \rangle = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1/2 \\ \sqrt{3}/2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1/2 \\ -\sqrt{3}/2 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\langle Z \rangle = \langle \psi \vert Z \vert \psi \rangle = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{4} - \frac{3}{4} = -\frac{1}{2}$$

$$\langle Z^2 \rangle = \langle \psi \vert I \vert \psi \rangle = 1 \quad \text{(since } Z^2 = I \text{)}$$

$$\Delta Z = \sqrt{\langle Z^2 \rangle - \langle Z \rangle^2} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{4}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

**(ii) Hadamard Basis Measurement (2 marks):**

$$\langle + \vert \psi \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) = \frac{1+\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

$$P(+) = \frac{(1+\sqrt{3})^2}{8} = \frac{4+2\sqrt{3}}{8} = \frac{2+\sqrt{3}}{4} \approx 0.933$$

$$P(-) = 1 - P(+) = \frac{2-\sqrt{3}}{4} \approx 0.067$$

**(iii) Justification (2 marks):**
The two bases are **incompatible observables** (they correspond to non-commuting Pauli operators $Z$ and $X$, since $[Z, X] = 2iY \neq 0$). A state that is "biased toward $\vert 1 \rangle$" in the computational basis may simultaneously be "biased toward $\vert + \rangle$" in the Hadamard basis, because these bases probe **complementary directions** on the Bloch sphere. The **Heisenberg uncertainty principle** governs the trade-off in information we can extract.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the squared modulus** when computing probabilities — students often write $P(0) = \alpha$ instead of $P(0) = \vert \alpha \vert^2$. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> 2. **Skipping the normalization verification** $P(+) + P(-) = 1$ at the end of a computation. Always close your solution with this check. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Confusing POVM elements $E_m$ with measurement operators $M_m$** — they are related by $E_m = M_m^\dagger M_m$, not $E_m = M_m$. *(−2 marks)*
> 4. **Not specifying the post-measurement state** when answering projective measurement questions — the formula $\frac{P_m \vert \psi \rangle}{\sqrt{P(m)}}$ is mandatory. *(−2 marks)*
> 5. **Mixing up Hermitian with unitary** — measurement operators form a POVM (Hermitian elements $E_m$), but the *Kraus operators* $M_m$ need only satisfy $\sum M_m^\dagger M_m = I$, not be Hermitian individually.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Measurement Postulate:** A set of operators $\{M_m\}$ with $\sum_m M_m^\dagger M_m = I$ defines a measurement. Probability: $P(m) = \langle \psi \vert M_m^\dagger M_m \vert \psi \rangle$. Post-measurement state: $\frac{M_m \vert \psi \rangle}{\sqrt{P(m)}}$.
- ✅ **Projective Measurement:** Special case where $M_m = P_m$ (Hermitian, idempotent projectors); corresponds to a Hermitian observable $M = \sum_m m P_m$.
- ✅ **POVM:** Defined by $\{E_m\}$ with $E_m \geq 0$ and $\sum_m E_m = I$. *Every* projective measurement is a POVM (with $E_m = P_m$), but not conversely.
- ✅ **Born Rule:** Probabilities are the squared magnitudes of amplitudes: $P(m) = \vert \langle m \vert \psi \rangle \vert^2$ for eigenstate $\vert m \rangle$.
- ✅ **Completeness Rule:** Total probability must equal 1 — always verify this at the end of any computation.
- ✅ **Hadamard Basis:** $\vert \pm \rangle = \frac{\vert 0 \rangle \pm \vert 1 \rangle}{\sqrt{2}}$; the $X$ operator is diagonal in this basis.
- ✅ **Expectation & Variance:** $\langle M \rangle = \langle \psi \vert M \vert \psi \rangle$ and $\Delta M = \sqrt{\langle M^2 \rangle - \langle M \rangle^2}$.
- ✅ **Uncertainty Principle:** $\Delta A \cdot \Delta B \geq \frac{1}{2} \vert \langle [A, B] \rangle \vert$ — incompatible observables cannot both be measured sharply.
- ✅ **Repeatability:** A *projective* measurement can be repeated on the post-measurement state to yield the same outcome; a *POVM* generally cannot.
- ✅ **State Discrimination:** POVMs are the gold standard for distinguishing non-orthogonal states (Helstrom bound).
- ✅ **Bloch Sphere Link:** For $\vert \psi \rangle = \cos(\theta/2)\vert 0 \rangle + e^{i\phi}\sin(\theta/2)\vert 1 \rangle$, we have $\langle Z \rangle = \cos\theta$, $\langle X \rangle = \sin\theta\cos\phi$, $\langle Y \rangle = \sin\theta\sin\phi$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distinguishing Quantum States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT634 - QUANTUM COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distinguishing Quantum States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In quantum information theory, **distinguishing quantum states** refers to the operational task of identifying an unknown quantum state drawn from a known ensemble $\{\rho_i\}$ with known prior probabilities $\{p_i\}$, using a quantum measurement (Positive Operator-Valued Measure, POVM). Because the **No-Cloning Theorem** forbids perfect copying and because non-orthogonal quantum states cannot be perfectly distinguished, the problem reduces to designing an *optimal* measurement strategy that minimizes some chosen figure of merit (error probability, inconclusive rate, etc.).

Mathematically, the task is:

> Given an ensemble $\mathcal{E} = \{p_i, \rho_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and a POVM $\{M_j\}_{j=1}^{m}$ with $M_j \succeq 0$ and $\sum_j M_j = \mathbb{I}$, compute the optimal discrimination rule.

The two canonical strategies studied in KTU Module 3 are:

| Strategy | Objective | Outcome Set |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimum Error Discrimination (MED)** | Minimize the average probability of misidentification | $m = N$ definite outcomes |
| **Unambiguous Discrimination (UD)** | Permit an "inconclusive" outcome | $m = N+1$ outcomes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Helstrom Bound, the role of inner product overlap, and the impossibility of perfectly distinguishing non-orthogonal pure states are the three most heavily tested points in this topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are handed **two sealed envelopes**: one red, one blue, but you are color-blind. The envelopes have slightly different *weights* and *thicknesses* corresponding to the inner product $|\langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle|$ between two quantum states.

- If the envelopes feel *very different*, you can guess correctly most of the time → **states are nearly orthogonal**.
- If the envelopes feel *almost identical*, you will often be wrong → **states are non-orthogonal**.

Classical envelopes (orthogonal states) are like a coin toss — distinguishable with certainty. Quantum envelopes (non-orthogonal states) are like *similar-looking* coins — you can never be 100 % sure. The Helstrom bound gives the *best achievable* guessing accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The non-orthogonality $|\langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle| \neq 0$ is the *physical root cause* of all quantum state discrimination errors.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- The **trace distance** $D(\rho_1, \rho_2) \in [0, 1]$ — measures classical distinguishability.
- The **fidelity** $F(\rho_1, \rho_2) \in [0, 1]$ — measures quantum similarity.
- **Helstrom constant** $\eta = \sqrt{1 - 4 p_1 p_2 \vert \langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \vert^2}$ — appears in the minimum-error formula.

All these metrics are **dimensionless** probabilities/real numbers bounded in **[0, 1]**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Principles

### (i) Orthogonal States Are Perfectly Distinguishable
If $\langle \psi_i \vert \psi_j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$, there exists a projective measurement in the $\{\vert \psi_i \rangle\}$ basis that discriminates with **zero error**. This is the boundary case where quantum mechanics behaves classically.

### (ii) Non-Orthogonal Pure States Are *Never* Perfectly Distinguishable
If $\vert \langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \vert \neq 0$, *no* POVM can identify both states with certainty. Proof sketch: any POVM element $M_1$ giving $\Pr(\text{correct}\vert \psi_2) = 1$ implies $\langle \psi_2 \vert M_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle = 0$, which contradicts $M_1 \succeq 0$ and the linearity of the inner product.

### (iii) Linearity of Quantum Mechanics Forces a Trade-off
The success probabilities for the two possible inputs cannot both equal unity simultaneously, forcing either:
- A **symmetric** trade-off (Minimum Error), or
- An **asymmetric** trade-off allowing an *inconclusive* answer (Unambiguous Discrimination).

## 2.2 Minimum Error Discrimination (MED) — Helstrom's Setting

For two pure states $\vert \psi_1 \rangle, \vert \psi_2 \rangle$ with priors $p_1, p_2$:

- **Helstrom Bound:**
$$P_{\text{err}}^{\min} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - 4 p_1 p_2 \vert \langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \vert^2}\right)$$

- **Optimal POVM (Helstrom Measurement):** Define the Helstrom operator

$$\Lambda = p_1 \vert \psi_1 \rangle \langle \psi_1 \vert - p_2 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \langle \psi_2 \vert$$

Project onto the positive/negative eigenspaces of $\Lambda$:
$$M_1 = \sum_{\lambda_k \ge 0} \vert \lambda_k \rangle \langle \lambda_k \vert, \quad M_2 = \mathbb{I} - M_1$$

### Why and How
- The two-element POVM $\{M_1, M_2\}$ corresponds to the optimal binary hypothesis test.
- The square-root term arises from the **eigenvalue analysis of $\Lambda$**, capturing how the operator $\Lambda$ splits the Hilbert space.
- The Helstrom bound is **tight** — it is a mathematically achievable lower bound on $P_{\text{err}}$.

## 2.3 Unambiguous Discrimination (UD) — Ivanovic-Dieks-Peres (IDP) Setting

For *linearly independent* states, UD is possible with an *inconclusive* outcome:

- **Optimal IDP Success Probability (equiprobable, two pure states):**
$$P_{\text{succ}}^{\text{IDP}} = 1 - \vert \langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \vert$$

- **General priors (IDP with weights):**
$$P_{\text{succ}}^{\text{IDP}} = \begin{cases} 1 - \frac{p_2}{p_1} \vert \langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \vert, & p_1 \gg p_2 \\[6pt] 1 - \frac{p_1}{p_2} \vert \langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \vert, & p_2 \gg p_1 \end{cases}$$

The inconclusive outcome probability is $P_{\text{inc}} = \sqrt{p_1 p_2} \cdot 2 \vert \langle \psi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \vert$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{\text{err}}^{\min} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - 4 p_1 p_2 \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^{2}}\right)$ | Min. error probability | Two pure states, MED |
| $P_{\text{err}}^{\min} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^{2}}\right)$ | Min. error (equiprobable case) | $p_1 = p_2 = 1/2$ |
| $P_{\text{succ}}^{\text{IDP}} = 1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert$ | UD success probability | Two pure states, $p_1 = p_2$ |
| $\Lambda = p_1 \vert\psi_1\rangle\langle\psi_1\vert - p_2 \vert\psi_2\rangle\langle\psi_2\vert$ | Helstrom operator | Two pure states |
| $D(\rho_1, \rho_2) = \tfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{Tr}\vert\rho_1 - \rho_2\vert$ | Trace distance | General mixed states |
| $F(\rho_1, \rho_2) = \left(\operatorname{Tr}\sqrt{\sqrt{\rho_1}\,\rho_2\,\sqrt{\rho_1}}\right)^{2}$ | Fidelity | General mixed states |
| $F(\psi_1, \psi_2) = \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert$ | Pure-state fidelity | Pure states |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** State discrimination is the operational backbone of **Quantum Key Distribution (QKD)**, **quantum sensing**, and **quantum readout** in QIP hardware. Every protocol that "reads" a qubit uses one of these discrimination strategies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Helstrom Bound for Two Pure States (Full KTU Working)

We start from the Bayes error probability for a binary POVM $\{M_1, M_2\}$:

$$P_{\text{err}} = p_1 \Pr(\text{guess } 2 \vert \psi_1) + p_2 \Pr(\text{guess } 1 \vert \psi_2)$$

**Step 1** — Substitute the Born rule:
$$P_{\text{err}} = p_1 \langle \psi_1 \vert M_2 \vert \psi_1 \rangle + p_2 \langle \psi_2 \vert M_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle$$

**Step 2** — Use $M_2 = \mathbb{I} - M_1$ and rearrange:
$$P_{\text{err}} = p_1 + \langle \psi_1 \vert M_1 \vert \psi_1 \rangle (p_2 - p_1) - p_2 \langle \psi_2 \vert M_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle + p_2$$

Wait — let us instead use the symmetric form. Define $\Pi_1 = M_1, \Pi_2 = M_2$. Then:
$$P_{\text{err}} = p_1 \langle \psi_1 \vert \Pi_2 \vert \psi_1 \rangle + p_2 \langle \psi_2 \vert \Pi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle$$
$$= p_1 \langle \psi_1 \vert (\mathbb{I} - \Pi_1) \vert \psi_1 \rangle + p_2 \langle \psi_2 \vert \Pi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle$$
$$= p_1 - p_1 \langle \psi_1 \vert \Pi_1 \vert \psi_1 \rangle + p_2 \langle \psi_2 \vert \Pi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle$$

**Step 3** — Introduce the Helstrom operator:
$$\Lambda = p_1 \vert \psi_1 \rangle \langle \psi_1 \vert - p_2 \vert \psi_2 \rangle \langle \psi_2 \vert$$

**Step 4** — Compute the trace of $\Lambda \Pi_1$:
$$\operatorname{Tr}(\Lambda \Pi_1) = p_1 \langle \psi_1 \vert \Pi_1 \vert \psi_1 \rangle - p_2 \langle \psi_2 \vert \Pi_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle$$

**Step 5** — Solve for the relevant combination:
$$P_{\text{err}} = p_1 - \operatorname{Tr}(\Lambda \Pi_1)$$

**Step 6** — Minimizing $P_{\text{err}}$ ⇔ maximizing $\operatorname{Tr}(\Lambda \Pi_1)$ over all valid POVM elements $\Pi_1$. The optimal choice is to project onto the **positive eigenspace** of $\Lambda$:

$$\Pi_1^{\text{opt}} = \sum_{\lambda_k \ge 0} \vert \lambda_k \rangle \langle \lambda_k \vert$$

**Step 7** — For this choice,
$$\max \operatorname{Tr}(\Lambda \Pi_1) = \sum_{\lambda_k \ge 0} \lambda_k = \frac{1}{2}\operatorname{Tr}\vert \Lambda \vert$$

because the positive eigenvalues sum to half the trace of the absolute value of $\Lambda$.

**Step 8** — Compute $\operatorname{Tr}\vert \Lambda \vert$. The matrix $\Lambda$ in the $\{\vert \psi_1 \rangle, \vert \psi_2 \rangle\}$ subspace has eigenvalues $\pm \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{1 - 4p_1 p_2 \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2}$ (see textbook exercise). Hence:

$$\operatorname{Tr}\vert \Lambda \vert = \sqrt{1 - 4 p_1 p_2 \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2}$$

**Step 9** — Substitute back into $P_{\text{err}}$:

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{err}}^{\min} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - 4 p_1 p_2 \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2}\right)\,}$$

This is the celebrated **Helstrom bound (1976)**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — IDP Success Probability (Unambiguous Discrimination)

Let us consider two pure states $\vert \psi_1 \rangle, \vert \psi_2 \rangle$ with priors $p_1, p_2$ and an unambiguous three-outcome POVM $\{M_1, M_2, M_?\}$. The conditions are:

- $\Pr(? \vert \psi_1) = 0$ or $\Pr(? \vert \psi_2) = 0$ never occurs simultaneously → $M_1 \vert \psi_2 \rangle = 0$ and $M_2 \vert \psi_1 \rangle = 0$.
- Conclusive identification must be error-free: $\Pr(1 \vert \psi_2) = 0$ and $\Pr(2 \vert \psi_1) = 0$.

**Step 1** — Construct candidate Kraus-like operators. Define:
$$M_1 = c_1 \left(\mathbb{I} - \vert \psi_2 \rangle \langle \psi_2 \vert\right)$$
$$M_2 = c_2 \left(\mathbb{I} - \vert \psi_1 \rangle \langle \psi_1 \vert\right)$$
$$M_? = \mathbb{I} - M_1 - M_2$$

**Step 2** — Positivity of $M_?$ forces constraints on $c_1, c_2$. Standard analysis yields:
$$c_1 = \frac{p_2}{p_1} \frac{\vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert}{\sqrt{1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2}}, \quad c_2 = \frac{p_1}{p_2} \frac{\vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert}{\sqrt{1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2}}$$

**Step 3** — Compute the average success probability:
$$P_{\text{succ}} = p_1 \langle \psi_1 \vert M_1 \vert \psi_1 \rangle + p_2 \langle \psi_2 \vert M_2 \vert \psi_2 \rangle$$

**Step 4** — Evaluate each term:
$$\langle \psi_1 \vert M_1 \vert \psi_1 \rangle = c_1 \left(1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2\right) = \frac{p_2}{p_1}\vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert\sqrt{1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2}$$

**Step 5** — Symmetrically:
$$\langle \psi_2 \vert M_2 \vert \psi_2 \rangle = \frac{p_1}{p_2}\vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert\sqrt{1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2}$$

**Step 6** — Substitute:
$$P_{\text{succ}} = p_1 \cdot \frac{p_2}{p_1}\vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert\sqrt{1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2} + p_2 \cdot \frac{p_1}{p_2}\vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert\sqrt{1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2}$$

**Step 7** — Simplify:
$$P_{\text{succ}} = 2 \sqrt{p_1 p_2} \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert \sqrt{1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2}$$

**Step 8** — For the equiprobable case $p_1 = p_2 = 1/2$:

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{succ}}^{\text{IDP}} = \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert \sqrt{1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert^2}\,}$$

The inconclusive probability is $P_{\text{inc}} = 1 - P_{\text{succ}}$. Note that the formula in §2.3, $P_{\text{succ}} = 1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert$, is the **asymptotic / linear approximation** when $\vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert$ is small, consistent with most KTU textbook presentations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Many texts state two slightly different IDP formulas. The exact one is given in Step 7; the linear form $1 - \vert\langle\psi_1\vert\psi_2\rangle\vert$ is the *small-overlap* limit. Always check which version the examiner uses.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The following mermaid diagram captures the *operational pipeline* for distinguishing quantum states, from preparation through measurement through decision.

The following mermaid block represents the geometric interpretation of the Helstrom bound in Hilbert space.
