> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Hamiltonian Simulation:** Convert $e^{-iHt}$ into elementary unitaries via **Trotter-Suzuki**; complexity $\mathcal{O}(L^2 t^2/\epsilon)$ first order.
- **Lloyd's Universal Simulator:** Local Hamiltonians are efficiently simulable because each $H_k$ acts on $\mathcal{O}(1)$ qubits.
- **Trotter 1st Order:** $e^{(A+B)t} \approx (e^{At/r}e^{Bt/r})^{r}$; error $\mathcal{O}(t^2/r)$.
- **Trotter 2nd Order (Symmetric):** $S_2(t) = e^{At/2}e^{Bt}e^{At/2}$; error $\mathcal{O}(t^3/r^2)$.
- **QFT Definition:** Unitary map $\vert x\rangle \to \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_y \omega_N^{xy}\vert y\rangle$.
- **QFT Gate Count:** $n$ Hadamards + $\binom{n}{2}$ controlled rotations = $\mathcal{O}(n^2) = \mathcal{O}(\log^2 N)$.
- **QFT Factored Form:** Output tensor factors are $(\vert 0\rangle + e^{2\pi i 0.x_{n-k+1}\cdots x_n}\vert 1\rangle)/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Phase Kickback:** Controlled-$U^{2^k}$ from eigenstate register imprints $e^{2\pi i j\varphi}$ on counting register amplitudes.
- **Inverse QFT:** Converts phase information in amplitudes to bit-string probabilities; the heart of QPE.
- **QPE Exact Case:** $\varphi = b/2^n$ yields correct answer with probability = 1.
- **QPE General Case:** $t = n + \mathcal{O}(\log(1/\epsilon))$ extra bits give success $\geq 1 - 2\epsilon$.
- **QPE Output:** An $n$-bit classical estimate of $\varphi$ that approximates $e^{2\pi i\varphi}$ to precision $2^{-n}$.
- **Spectral Role:** QPE is the standard tool for computing eigenvalues of unitaries — the *single most important* subroutine in quantum algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application: Order Finding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT634 - QUANTUM COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application: Order Finding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Order-Finding Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order-Finding Problem (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Given two co-prime integers $a$ and $N$ with $1 < a < N$, the **order** of $a$ modulo $N$ is the smallest positive integer $r$ such that:
> $$a^r \equiv 1 \pmod{N}$$
> The order-finding problem is to determine $r$ efficiently, where "efficiently" implies polynomial time complexity in the number of bits $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$.

The function $f(x) = a^x \bmod N$ is a **periodic function** with period $r$. Thus, order-finding is mathematically equivalent to **period-finding** in modular arithmetic — this is the key insight that enables a quantum speedup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Co-primarity condition** $\gcd(a, N) = 1$ ensures that the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_N^*$ has well-defined order. If $\gcd(a, N) > 1$, then $a$ shares a non-trivial factor with $N$, which would already solve factoring for $N$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing in front of a giant clock with **N hour positions** (numbered $0$ to $N-1$), and you start at position $1$. Each time you "tick" the clock, you multiply your current position by $a$ and reduce modulo $N$ (you wrap around the clock).

> **The Analogy:** You are looking for the **first time** your position returns back to exactly **1** after some number of ticks.

For example, with $N = 15$ and $a = 7$:
- Tick 0: position = 1
- Tick 1: position = 7
- Tick 2: position = 7 × 7 mod 15 = 49 mod 15 = **4**
- Tick 3: position = 4 × 7 mod 15 = 28 mod 15 = **13**
- Tick 4: position = 13 × 7 mod 15 = 91 mod 15 = **1** ← returned!

So the order is $r = 4$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** No classical algorithm is known to find $r$ in polynomial time. The **best classical algorithm** runs in roughly $O(\sqrt{r})$ operations, which is super-polynomial when $r$ is exponentially large. **Shor's quantum algorithm** solves it in $O((\log N)^3)$ gate operations — an **exponential speedup**.

## 1.3 Engineering & Cryptographic Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Order-finding is the **quantum heart of Shor's factoring algorithm**. If we can find the order of $a$ modulo $N$, then with high probability we can extract a non-trivial factor of $N$. This has direct implications for breaking **RSA encryption**, which secures most internet communication. The standard public key size is **2048 bits** (≈ **2 × 10³ bits**) — factoring it is infeasible classically but theoretically tractable on a sufficiently large fault-tolerant quantum computer.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|-----------|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Modulus size | $\log_2 N$ | **2048 bits** | RSA-2048 key size |
| Order range | $r$ | up to $N-1$ | Output of algorithm |
| Success probability | $p$ | ≥ **0.4** per run | With classical repetition |
| Quantum gate complexity | $T$ | $O((\log N)^3)$ | Exponential speedup vs classical |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classical Reduction: From Factoring to Order-Finding

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Reduction Theorem:**
> Let $N$ be a composite integer and choose $a$ uniformly at random with $1 < a < N - 1$. If $r = \text{ord}_N(a)$ is the order of $a$ modulo $N$ and $r$ is **even**, then at least one of:
> $$\gcd\bigl(a^{r/2} - 1,\ N\bigr) \quad \text{or} \quad \gcd\bigl(a^{r/2} + 1,\ N\bigr)$$
> yields a non-trivial factor of $N$ with probability $\geq \frac{1}{2}$.

This reduction is the bridge: a quantum order-finding subroutine → polynomial-time integer factorization.

## 2.2 Quantum Strategy — High-Level Steps

The quantum order-finding algorithm exploits **quantum parallelism** and **quantum interference** to evaluate the periodic function $f(x) = a^x \bmod N$ on a superposition of all inputs simultaneously, then uses the **Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT)** to extract the period.

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

1. **Register initialization**: Allocate two quantum registers — a **first register** of $t = 2\lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$ qubits (the "control" register) and a **second register** of $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ qubits (the "target" register).

2. **Superposition creation**: Apply Hadamard gates to all qubits in the first register to create an equal superposition of all integers $x$ from $0$ to $2^t - 1$.

3. **Modular exponentiation oracle**: Apply the unitary $U_a$ that computes $|x\rangle \vert 0\rangle \rightarrow |x\rangle \vert a^x \bmod N\rangle$, entangling both registers.

4. **Inverse QFT on the first register**: This concentrates amplitude on states whose phases encode the period $r$.

5. **Measurement of the first register**: Yields an integer $y$ that is a multiple of $\frac{2^t}{r}$, i.e., $y \approx \frac{k \cdot 2^t}{r}$ for some unknown integer $k$.

6. **Classical post-processing via continued fractions**: Use the continued-fraction expansion of $\frac{y}{2^t}$ to recover a candidate $\frac{k}{r}$, and extract $r$ from the denominator.

7. **Verification**: Check that $a^r \equiv 1 \pmod N$. If not, repeat with new measurement.

## 2.3 The Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) — Core Subroutine

The QFT acts on an orthonormal basis $\{|0\rangle, |1\rangle, \dots, \vert 2^t - 1\rangle\}$ as:

$$QFT: \vert x\rangle \rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^t}} \sum_{y=0}^{2^t - 1} e^{2\pi i x y / 2^t} \vert y\rangle$$

For the period-finding application, the QFT is applied to a state of the form:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2^t}} \sum_{x=0}^{2^t - 1} \vert x\rangle \vert f(x)\rangle$$

After measuring the second register (collapsing the periodic structure), the first register is in a state peaked at integer multiples of $\frac{2^t}{r}$. The inverse QFT then transforms phase information into measurement-probability peaks.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas required for KTU ESE problems on Order-Finding (no `|` pipe characters — using `\vert` instead):**

| # | Formula / Theorem | Statement | Application |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Order definition | $a^r \equiv 1 \pmod N$ | Find smallest $r$ |
| 2 | Period equivalence | $f(x) = a^x \bmod N$, period $= r$ | Period-finding reformulation |
| 3 | Inverse QFT output | $y \approx \dfrac{k \cdot 2^t}{r}$, $0 \leq k < r$ | Extract phase information |
| 4 | QFT action | $QFT \vert x\rangle = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2^t}}\sum_{y=0}^{2^t-1} e^{2\pi i x y / 2^t} \vert y\rangle$ | Basis transformation |
| 5 | Continued fraction bound | If $\left\vert \dfrac{y}{2^t} - \dfrac{k}{r} \right\vert < \dfrac{1}{2r^2}$, then $\dfrac{k}{r}$ appears in CF expansion | Recovery of $r$ |
| 6 | First register size | $t = 2 \lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$ | Minimum qubits for success |
| 7 | Success probability | $p \geq \dfrac{4}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\log r}$ per run (lower bound) | Repeat $O(\log \log r)$ times |
| 8 | Factoring reduction | $\gcd\bigl(a^{r/2} \pm 1,\ N\bigr)$ gives non-trivial factor | Bridge to RSA |
| 9 | Modular exponentiation gate count | $O(n^3)$ where $n = \log N$ | Total $O(n^3 \log n)$ |
| 10 | Best classical complexity | $O(\exp(c \cdot n^{1/3} (\log n)^{2/3}))$ | Sub-exponential (Number Field Sieve) |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering applications of order-finding beyond cryptography:**
> - **Cryptanalysis**: Breaking RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and elliptic-curve variants.
> - **Discrete logarithm problem**: Equivalent in difficulty to factoring.
> - **Hidden subgroup problem**: Order-finding is a special case of the more general abelian HSP over $\mathbb{Z}$.
> - **Post-quantum security research**: NIST standardization of lattice-based cryptography (e.g., CRYSTALS-Kyber, **512-bit** security level) is a direct response to quantum order-finding.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantum State Evolution Through the Circuit

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Setup:** We will trace the state vector at each stage of the circuit for $N = 15$, $a = 7$, $t = 5$ (5-qubit first register), $n = 4$ (4-qubit second register). The order is $r = 4$.

### Step 1 — Initial State

$$\vert \psi_0\rangle = \vert 0\rangle^{\otimes t} \otimes \vert 0\rangle^{\otimes n} = \vert 00000\rangle \otimes \vert 0000\rangle$$

### Step 2 — Apply Hadamard to First Register

We apply $H^{\otimes t}$ to the first register:

$$\vert \psi_1\rangle = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2^t}} \sum_{x=0}^{2^t - 1} \vert x\rangle\right) \otimes \vert 0\rangle^{\otimes n}$$

Substituting $t = 5$:

$$\vert \psi_1\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{32}} \sum_{x=0}^{31} \vert x\rangle \otimes \vert 0000\rangle$$

This is the **uniform superposition** — the heart of quantum parallelism. Every integer from $0$ to $31$ exists simultaneously.

### Step 3 — Apply Modular Exponentiation Oracle $U_a$

The unitary $U_a$ acts as $U_a \vert x\rangle \vert y\rangle = \vert x\rangle \vert y \oplus (a^x \bmod N)\rangle$. With the second register initialized to $\vert 0\rangle^{\otimes n}$:

$$\vert \psi_2\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{32}} \sum_{x=0}^{31} \vert x\rangle \otimes \vert a^x \bmod 15\rangle$$

### Step 4 — Examine the Periodicity in the Second Register

For $a = 7$ and $N = 15$, compute $7^x \bmod 15$:

$$\begin{aligned}
7^0 \bmod 15 &= 1 \\
7^1 \bmod 15 &= 7 \\
7^2 \bmod 15 &= 49 \bmod 15 = 4 \\
7^3 \bmod 15 &= 28 \bmod 15 = 13 \\
7^4 \bmod 15 &= 91 \bmod 15 = 1 \\
7^5 \bmod 15 &= 7 \cdot 1 = 7 \\
7^6 \bmod 15 &= 7 \cdot 7 = 4 \\
7^7 \bmod 15 &= 7 \cdot 4 = 13 \\
\end{aligned}$$

The period is confirmed: $r = 4$. So the second register cycles through $\{1, 7, 4, 13, 1, 7, 4, 13, \dots\}$.

Rewriting the state grouping inputs by $f(x)$ value:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert \psi_2\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{32}} \Big[
& \bigl(\vert 0\rangle + \vert 4\rangle + \vert 8\rangle + \dots + \vert 28\rangle\bigr) \otimes \vert 1\rangle \\
+\ & \bigl(\vert 1\rangle + \vert 5\rangle + \vert 9\rangle + \dots + \vert 29\rangle\bigr) \otimes \vert 7\rangle \\
+\ & \bigl(\vert 2\rangle + \vert 6\rangle + \vert 10\rangle + \dots + \vert 30\rangle\bigr) \otimes \vert 4\rangle \\
+\ & \bigl(\vert 3\rangle + \vert 7\rangle + \vert 11\rangle + \dots + \vert 31\rangle\bigr) \otimes \vert 13\rangle
\Big]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Measurement of the Second Register (Conceptual)

Suppose we measure the second register and obtain some value $f_0 \in \{1, 7, 4, 13\}$. The first register **collapses** to an equal superposition over the pre-images of $f_0$:

$$\vert \psi_3\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^t / r}} \sum_{j=0}^{(2^t / r) - 1} \vert x_0 + j r\rangle$$

where $x_0$ is the smallest $x$ with $a^x \bmod N = f_0$. For example, if we measured $f_0 = 7$:

$$\vert \psi_3\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{8}} \bigl(\vert 1\rangle + \vert 5\rangle + \vert 9\rangle + \vert 13\rangle + \vert 17\rangle + \vert 21\rangle + \vert 25\rangle + \vert 29\rangle\bigr)$$

This state contains a superposition of $8$ basis states equally spaced by $r = 4$.

### Step 6 — Apply Inverse Quantum Fourier Transform

The QFT of a periodic sequence with spacing $r$ concentrates probability on states $\vert y\rangle$ where $y \approx \frac{k \cdot 2^t}{r}$. Explicitly, applying $QFT^{-1}$:

$$\vert \psi_4\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{r}} \sum_{k=0}^{r - 1} e^{-2\pi i x_0 k / r} \left\vert \frac{k \cdot 2^t}{r}\right\rangle$$

For our example $r = 4$, $t = 5$, $2^t = 32$, the peaks occur at:
- $k = 0$: $y = 0$
- $k = 1$: $y = 8$
- $k = 2$: $y = 16$
- $k = 3$: $y = 24$

Measurement yields one of $\{0, 8, 16, 24\}$ uniformly at random.

### Step 7 — Classical Post-Processing via Continued Fractions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continued Fraction Algorithm for extracting $r$:**
> Given a measurement outcome $y \in \{0, 1, \dots, 2^t - 1\}$, compute the continued fraction expansion of $\frac{y}{2^t}$. Find a convergent $\frac{k'}{r'}$ with $r' < N$. Verify $a^{r'} \equiv 1 \pmod N$. If yes, $r = r'$.

**Worked example:** Suppose measurement yields $y = 8$.

Compute the rational $\frac{y}{2^t} = \frac{8}{32} = \frac{1}{4}$.

Continued fraction of $\frac{1}{4}$:

$$\frac{1}{4} = [0; 4]$$

The convergents are: $\frac{0}{1}$ and $\frac{1}{4}$.

The denominator of the convergent is $r' = 4$. Verification: $7^4 \bmod 15 = 1$. ✓ So $r = 4$.

**Another example:** Suppose measurement yields $y = 16$.

Compute $\frac{16}{32} = \frac{1}{2}$.

Continued fraction: $\frac{1}{2} = [0; 2]$, convergent is $\frac{1}{2}$, so $r' = 2$. But $7^2 \bmod 15 = 4 \neq 1$, so $r' = 2$ is rejected. The next convergent is $\frac{0}{1}$, also rejected.

> [!WARNING]
> **Failure mode:** A measurement like $y = 16$ gives a non-trivial factor $r' = 2$ of the *denominator*, but $a^{r'} \not\equiv 1 \pmod N$. In this case, the algorithm must be **rerun** with fresh inputs. The probability of such failure is bounded but non-zero.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Probability of Obtaining a "Good" Measurement

We want to bound the probability that the measurement outcome $y$ yields a convergent $\frac{k}{r}$ such that $\left\vert \frac{y}{2^t} - \frac{k}{r} \right\vert \leq \frac{1}{2r^2}$.

Let $\phi = \frac{y}{2^t}$. Define $\delta_k = \left\vert \phi - \frac{k}{r} \right\vert$ for $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, r - 1\}$. The amplitude at each peak is proportional to $\frac{1}{r} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi \cdot \delta_k \cdot r)}{\sin(\pi \cdot \delta_k \cdot 2^t / r)}$. Using standard Fourier analysis:

$$P(y) = \frac{1}{r^2} \cdot \left\vert \frac{\sin(\pi \cdot \delta_k \cdot 2^t)}{\sin(\pi \cdot \delta_k \cdot 2^t / r)} \right\vert^2$$

Summing over all "good" $y$ (those within $\frac{1}{2r^2}$ of some $\frac{k}{r}$):

$$P_{\text{good}} \geq \frac{4}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{1}{r} \cdot (\text{number of good } y)$$

With the first-register size $t = 2 \lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$, the standard bound gives:

$$P_{\text{good}} \geq \frac{4}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\log r}$$

After $O(\log \log N)$ repetitions, the success probability approaches **1**.

## 3.3 Complete Worked Example — Factor $N = 15$

**Step-by-step factoring using order-finding:**

1. Choose $a = 7$ randomly with $1 < a < 14$. Compute $\gcd(7, 15) = 1$. ✓
2. Run order-finding quantum subroutine: obtain $r = 4$.
3. Verify $r$ is even: $r = 4$ is even. ✓
4. Compute $a^{r/2} = 7^2 = 49$.
5. Compute $\gcd(7^2 - 1, 15) = \gcd(48, 15) = 3$. ✓ Non-trivial factor found!
6. Other factor: $15 / 3 = 5$.
7. Factorization complete: $15 = 3 \times 5$.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification check:** If $\gcd(a^{r/2} - 1, N) = 1$ or $\gcd(a^{r/2} - 1, N) = N$, then $a$ was unlucky — pick a new $a$ and retry. This happens with probability at most $\frac{1}{2}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Quantum Circuit Architecture for Order-Finding

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-level functional architecture** of Shor's order-finding circuit. Each horizontal "wire" represents a qubit register. Gates flow left-to-right.

```mermaid
graph TD
    init1["First Register t qubits initialised to 0"]
    init2["Second Register n qubits initialised to 0"]
    H["H tensor t Hadamard on First Register"]
    UE["Modular Exponentiation Oracle U_a computes a to x mod N"]
    MEAS2["Measure Second Register discard result classical post selection"]
    QFTI["Inverse QFT on First Register QFT to the power of minus 1"]
    MEAS1["Measure First Register outcome y in 0 to 2 to t minus 1"]
    CF["Classical Continued Fraction Expansion of y over 2 to t"]
    VER["Verify a to r prime mod N equals 1 candidate order r"]
    OUT["Output order r repeat on failure"]

    init1 --> H
    init2 --> UE
    H --> UE
    UE --> MEAS2
    MEAS2 --> QFTI
    QFTI --> MEAS1
    MEAS1 --> CF
    CF --> VER
    VER --> OUT
    OUT -.retry.-> H
```

## 4.2 Algorithmic Flow — Classical Outer Loop + Quantum Inner Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["INPUT composite integer N"]
    B["Classical pre check if N even return factor 2"]
    C["Classical pre check if N is prime power use classical algorithm"]
    D["Randomly choose a in 2 to N minus 2"]
    E["Compute gcd of a and N if greater than 1 return factor"]
    F["QUANTUM subroutine OrderFinder returns r"]
    G{"Is r even"}
    H["Compute x = a to r over 2 mod N"]
    I{"x plus 1 mod N equals 0"}
    J["Compute gcd of x minus 1 and N returns non trivial factor"]
    K["Compute gcd of x plus 1 and N returns non trivial factor"]
    L["Failure pick new a retry loop back to D"]
    M["OUTPUT factors of N"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G -- no --> L
    G -- yes --> H
    H --> I
    I -- yes --> K
    I -- no --> J
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> D
```

## 4.3 Modular Exponentiation Sub-Circuit — Functional Decomposition

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering note:** The modular exponentiation oracle $U_a$ is the most resource-intensive component. It is built from $O(n)$ controlled modular multipliers, each requiring $O(n^2)$ gates, totaling $O(n^3)$ gates where $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["Controlled Mult by a to 2 to 0 mod N"]
    A1["Controlled Mult by a to 2 to 1 mod N"]
    A2["Controlled Mult by a to 2 to 2 mod N"]
    A3["Controlled Mult by a to 2 to 3 mod N"]
    Dots["... up to Controlled Mult by a to 2 to t minus 1 mod N"]
    TARGET["Target Register accumulates product a to x mod N"]

    A0 --> TARGET
    A1 --> TARGET
    A2 --> TARGET
    A3 --> TARGET
    A3 --> Dots
    Dots --> TARGET
```

## 4.4 Continued-Fraction Recovery Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    Y["Measurement outcome y"]
    R["Compute rational y over 2 to t"]
    CF["Apply Euclidean algorithm for CF expansion"]
    CONV["Extract convergents k prime over r prime"]
    CHK{"Is r prime less than N"}
    ACC["Accept r prime as candidate order r"]
    REJ["Reject r prime repeat quantum measurement"]
    VRF["Verify a to r mod N equals 1"]

    Y --> R
    R --> CF
    CF --> CONV
    CONV --> CHK
    CHK -- yes --> VRF
    CHK -- no --> REJ
    VRF -- pass --> ACC
    VRF -- fail --> REJ
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** 
**Define the order-finding problem. Explain in one sentence why it is computationally hard on a classical computer.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** Given integers $a$ and $N$ with $\gcd(a, N) = 1$ and $1 < a < N$, the order-finding problem asks for the smallest positive integer $r$ such that $a^r \equiv 1 \pmod N$.
>
> **Classical hardness (1 mark):** No classical algorithm is known to solve this in polynomial time in $\log N$. The best known classical algorithm (related to the Number Field Sieve) runs in sub-exponential time, making it intractable for cryptographically large $N$ (e.g., $N \geq 2^{2048}$).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the Quantum Fourier Transform in Shor's order-finding algorithm?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The QFT (and its inverse) is applied to the first register after modular exponentiation creates an entangled periodic state. The QFT transforms the **phase information** that encodes the period $r$ into **measurable computational-basis amplitudes**. Specifically, after inverse QFT, the first register's measurement concentrates probability on integer multiples of $\frac{2^t}{r}$, allowing classical post-processing (continued fractions) to extract $r$. Without the QFT, the periodicity would remain hidden in the relative phases and would be inaccessible to measurement.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the order-finding problem in detail. Describe the quantum algorithm to solve it with a neat quantum circuit diagram. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Problem Statement (2 marks):**
The order-finding problem is defined as follows: Given two coprime integers $a$ and $N$ (with $1 < a < N$), find the smallest positive integer $r$ such that:

$$a^r \equiv 1 \pmod N$$

This integer $r$ is called the **order** of $a$ modulo $N$, denoted $\text{ord}_N(a)$. The function $f(x) = a^x \bmod N$ is periodic with period $r$.

**(ii) Quantum Circuit (3 marks):**
The quantum order-finding circuit consists of:

- **First register** ($t = 2\lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$ qubits), initialized to $\vert 0\rangle^{\otimes t}$
- **Second register** ($n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ qubits), initialized to $\vert 0\rangle^{\otimes n}$
- $H^{\otimes t}$ applied to first register
- Modular exponentiation oracle $U_a$ that computes $\vert x\rangle \vert 0\rangle \rightarrow \vert x\rangle \vert a^x \bmod N\rangle$
- Inverse QFT on first register
- Measurement of first register

**Circuit Diagram (textual representation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\vert 0\rangle^{\otimes t} \xrightarrow{H^{\otimes t}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^t}} \sum_{x=0}^{2^t - 1} \vert x\rangle \xrightarrow{U_a} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^t}} \sum_{x=0}^{2^t - 1} \vert x\rangle \vert a^x \bmod N\rangle \\
&\xrightarrow{QFT^{-1}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{r}} \sum_{k=0}^{r-1} e^{-2\pi i \phi_k} \left\vert \frac{k \cdot 2^t}{r} \right\rangle \xrightarrow{\text{Measure}} y
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Post-processing (2 marks):**
The measured value $y$ is processed via continued-fraction expansion of $\frac{y}{2^t}$ to recover $\frac{k}{r}$, and hence $r$.

---

**Q.A (b)** With reference to Shor's algorithm, show how the order-finding subroutine reduces integer factoring to a tractable problem. Use $N = 15$, $a = 7$ as a worked example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Classical Reduction (3 marks):**
Let $r = \text{ord}_N(a)$ be the order of $a$ modulo $N$. If $r$ is **even** and $a^{r/2} \not\equiv -1 \pmod N$, then:

$$d = \gcd\bigl(a^{r/2} - 1,\ N\bigr)$$

is a non-trivial factor of $N$ with probability $\geq \frac{1}{2}$. Therefore, given a quantum subroutine for order-finding, factoring reduces to: (1) run the quantum subroutine, (2) check classical conditions, (3) compute a GCD.

**(ii) Worked Example with $N = 15$, $a = 7$ (4 marks):**

Step 1: Verify $\gcd(7, 15) = 1$ ✓.

Step 2: Run quantum order-finder. Obtain $r = 4$ (verified via continued fractions).

Step 3: Check $r$ is even ✓.

Step 4: Compute $a^{r/2} \bmod N = 7^2 \bmod 15 = 49 \bmod 15 = 4$.

Step 5: Compute $\gcd(4 - 1, 15) = \gcd(3, 15) = 3$.

Step 6: Non-trivial factor is **3**, with $15 / 3 = 5$.

**Conclusion:** $15 = 3 \times 5$. The algorithm succeeded in polynomial time.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q.B (a)** Derive the mathematical expression for the quantum state after each major stage of the order-finding circuit. Specifically, trace the evolution for an arbitrary input size $t$ and modular base $N$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

Let $t$ be the size of the first register and $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ be the size of the second register.

**Stage 1 — Initial state:**

$$\vert \psi_0\rangle = \vert 0\rangle^{\otimes t} \otimes \vert 0\rangle^{\otimes n}$$

**Stage 2 — After Hadamard $H^{\otimes t}$ on first register:**

$$\vert \psi_1\rangle = \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^t}} \sum_{x=0}^{2^t - 1} \vert x\rangle \right) \otimes \vert 0\rangle^{\otimes n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^t}} \sum_{x=0}^{2^t - 1} \vert x\rangle \vert 0\rangle^{\otimes n}$$

**Stage 3 — After modular exponentiation oracle $U_a$:**

$$U_a \vert x\rangle \vert y\rangle = \vert x\rangle \vert y \oplus (a^x \bmod N)\rangle$$

Applying to $\vert \psi_1\rangle$:

$$\vert \psi_2\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^t}} \sum_{x=0}^{2^t - 1} \vert x\rangle \vert a^x \bmod N\rangle$$

This state is **entangled** between the two registers.

**Stage 4 — After measuring the second register:**
Suppose the measurement yields $f_0$. The first register collapses to:

$$\vert \psi_3\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{r}{2^t}} \sum_{j=0}^{(2^t / r) - 1} \vert x_0 + j r\rangle$$

where $x_0$ is the smallest integer with $a^{x_0} \equiv f_0 \pmod N$.

**Stage 5 — After inverse QFT:**

$$\vert \psi_4\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{r}} \sum_{k=0}^{r-1} e^{-2\pi i x_0 k / r} \left\vert \left\lfloor \frac{k \cdot 2^t}{r} \right\rfloor \right\rangle$$

Measurement of $\vert \psi_4\rangle$ yields $y \approx \frac{k \cdot 2^t}{r}$ with high probability.

---

**Q.B (b)** Discuss the role of the continued-fraction algorithm in recovering the order from the quantum measurement outcome. Why is $t = 2 \lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$ the minimum register size for guaranteed success? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) Continued-Fraction Algorithm (4 marks):**

Given a measurement outcome $y$ and total range $2^t$, compute the rational number $\frac{y}{2^t} \in [0, 1)$. Apply the Euclidean algorithm to extract its continued-fraction expansion:

$$\frac{y}{2^t} = a_0 + \cfrac{1}{a_1 + \cfrac{1}{a_2 + \dots}}$$

The convergents $\frac{k_j}{r_j}$ are computed iteratively. We seek a convergent with $r_j < N$ satisfying $a^{r_j} \equiv 1 \pmod N$. The key theorem:

> **Three-distance theorem:** If $\left\vert \frac{y}{2^t} - \frac{k}{r} \right\vert \leq \frac{1}{2r^2}$, then $\frac{k}{r}$ is guaranteed to appear as a convergent in the continued-fraction expansion of $\frac{y}{2^t}$.

**Example:** $y = 8$, $2^t = 32$. Then $\frac{y}{2^t} = \frac{1}{4}$. Convergents: $\frac{0}{1}$, $\frac{1}{4}$. Denominator $\frac{1}{4}$'s $r' = 4$ matches the true order.

**(ii) Register size justification (3 marks):**

The first register must satisfy two conditions:
- **Condition 1:** $2^t > N^2$, so $t > 2 \log_2 N$, giving $t \geq 2 \lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$.
- **Condition 2:** The "good" measurement window of width $\frac{1}{2r^2}$ around each $\frac{k}{r}$ must contain at least one integer $y$.

Since $r \leq N - 1 < N$, we need $\frac{1}{2r^2} > \frac{1}{2N^2}$. The spacing between consecutive integers in $[0, 2^t)$ is $\frac{1}{2^t}$. For at least one integer to fall in each window:

$$\frac{1}{2^t} \leq \frac{1}{2N^2} \implies 2^t \geq 2N^2$$

Since $N < 2^{\lceil \log_2 N \rceil}$, we have $2N^2 < 2^{2\lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1}$. Choosing $t = 2\lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$ satisfies this with minimal overhead. Smaller $t$ risks missing the convergent entirely.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions in Order-Finding Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the coprimality check (–1 mark):** Many students compute $a^r \bmod N$ without verifying $\gcd(a, N) = 1$ first. If $\gcd(a, N) > 1$, that GCD is already a non-trivial factor.
>
> 2. **Confusing order with discrete logarithm (–2 marks):** The order is $r$ such that $a^r \equiv 1 \pmod N$. The discrete logarithm is $x$ such that $g^x \equiv h \pmod N$. These are related but distinct problems.
>
> 3. **Not stating register sizes explicitly (–1 mark):** Always specify $t = 2\lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$ for the first register and $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ for the second register.
>
> 4. **Skipping the inverse QFT (–3 marks):** A circuit that only does modular exponentiation followed by measurement does **not** solve order-finding. The QFT is the **key** to extracting periodicity from quantum phase.
>
> 5. **Treating the QFT measurement as deterministic (–2 marks):** The measurement outcome is random; the algorithm relies on classical post-processing (continued fractions) to deduce $r$ probabilistically.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to handle the case $a^{r/2} \equiv -1 \pmod N$ (–1 mark):** In this edge case, both $\gcd(a^{r/2} - 1, N)$ and $\gcd(a^{r/2} + 1, N)$ give trivial results, and the algorithm must retry with a different $a$.
>
> 7. **Using binary logarithm where natural log is meant (–1 mark):** Shor's complexity is $O((\log N)^3)$ — these are **base-2** logarithms for qubit counts, but the arithmetic gate count is sometimes expressed using natural log. Be explicit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Order-Finding (Module 4):**

- ☐ **Order definition:** smallest $r > 0$ with $a^r \equiv 1 \pmod N$, requires $\gcd(a, N) = 1$.
- ☐ **Period equivalence:** $f(x) = a^x \bmod N$ is periodic with period $r$; order-finding = period-finding.
- ☐ **Quantum registers:** first register $t = 2\lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$ qubits; second register $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ qubits.
- ☐ **Three core stages:** Hadamard superposition → Modular exponentiation oracle → Inverse QFT.
- ☐ **Measurement peaks:** outcomes cluster near $y = \frac{k \cdot 2^t}{r}$ for $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, r-1\}$.
- ☐ **Continued fractions:** the **key classical post-processing** step — recover $r$ from convergent of $\frac{y}{2^t}$.
- ☐ **Three-distance bound:** $\left\vert \frac{y}{2^t} - \frac{k}{r} \right\vert \leq \frac{1}{2r^2}$ guarantees convergent recovery.
- ☐ **Success probability per run:** $\geq \frac{4}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\log r}$; requires $O(\log \log N)$ repetitions.
- ☐ **Factoring reduction:** if $r$ even and $a^{r/2} \not\equiv -1 \pmod N$, then $\gcd(a^{r/2} - 1, N)$ is a non-trivial factor.
- ☐ **Quantum complexity:** $O((\log N)^3)$ gates — **exponential** speedup over best classical sub-exponential algorithms.
- ☐ **Cryptographic impact:** breaks RSA, Diffie-Hellman, ECC — central to post-quantum cryptography migration.
- ☐ **Practical caveat:** current hardware (NISQ era) cannot factor cryptographically meaningful $N$; fault-tolerant quantum computers with millions of physical qubits are required.
- ☐ **Worked example to memorize:** $N = 15$, $a = 7$, $r = 4$, factor $= \gcd(48, 15) = 3$.
- ☐ **Failure handling:** if $\gcd$ returns $1$ or $N$, rerun with new random $a$; classical pre/post-processing is essential.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantum Search Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT634 - QUANTUM COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quantum Search Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Quantum Search Algorithm** is a quantum procedure designed to locate a specific "marked" item within an unstructured database of **N** entries in fewer queries than the classical lower bound of **O(N)**. The canonical construction is **Grover's Algorithm** (Lov K. Grover, 1996), which achieves a **quadratic speedup** of **O(√N)** by exploiting quantum mechanical principles—specifically **superposition**, **interference**, and **amplitude amplification**.

The general problem statement is:

> Given a Boolean oracle function $f : \{0,1\}^n \rightarrow \{0,1\}$ that evaluates to **1** on a small set of **M** "marked" states and **0** elsewhere, find any $x$ such that $f(x) = 1$ with high probability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quadratic Speedup Constant:** Grover's algorithm requires only $\approx \frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N/M}$ oracle queries in the worst case, beating the classical bound of $\frac{N}{M}$ by a factor of **√N**. This is **provably optimal** for unstructured quantum search (Bennett, Bernstein, Brassard, Vazirani, 1997).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Quantum Flashlight"

Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into a vast haystack containing exactly **one** golden needle. Classically, you must grab one straw at a time, examining each (worst case: all **N** straws). You have no shortcut because the haystack is *unstructured*—no ordering, no labels, no hints.

Now imagine a **"Quantum Flashlight"** that, when switched on, illuminates the needle *partially* through constructive interference, while simultaneously **dimming** (destructively interfering with) the surrounding straws. Each pulse of the flashlight rotates the spotlight slightly toward the needle. After roughly **√N** pulses, the spotlight is fully focused on the needle—and you can pluck it out with a single measurement.

The two mechanisms of the flashlight are:
- **Oracle Reflection:** Marks the needle (flips its amplitude's sign—effectively a "negative glow").
- **Diffusion (Inversion about the Mean):** Amplifies the marked straw and suppresses the others by reflecting amplitudes about their average value.

Together, these two reflections form a **rotation** in a 2-dimensional subspace, marching the quantum state toward the marked basis vector with each iteration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works without structure:** Unlike classical search, Grover's algorithm does not need any pre-sorting or indexing of the database. The "structure" is *manufactured* on the fly by quantum interference.

### 1.3 Classical vs. Quantum Search — Key Comparison

| Parameter | Classical Linear Search | Grover's Quantum Search |
|---|---|---|
| **Query complexity (worst case)** | **O(N)** | **O(√N)** |
| **Query complexity (M marked items)** | $\frac{N}{M}$ | $\frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{\frac{N}{M}}$ |
| **Information available per query** | 1 deterministic bit | Quantum superposition over all N states |
| **Optimality** | Trivially optimal classically | Provably optimal (BBBV theorem) |
| **Hardware requirement** | Bit-level logic | Coherent qubits + reversible oracle |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Setup of the Search Problem

Let the search space be $\{0,1\}^n$ with size **N = 2^n**. Define the oracle $U_f$ such that:

$$U_f \vert x \rangle = (-1)^{f(x)} \vert x \rangle$$

where $f(x) = 1$ if $x$ is a marked item, and $f(x) = 0$ otherwise. The phase-kickback of $(-1)^{f(x)}$ is the key quantum resource—it marks the target without revealing it.

### 2.2 The Uniform Superposition State

Apply a Hadamard transform $H^{\otimes n}$ to the initial state $\vert 0 \rangle^{\otimes n}$ to obtain the equal superposition:

$$\vert s \rangle = H^{\otimes n}\vert 0 \rangle^{\otimes n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{x=0}^{N-1} \vert x \rangle$$

This is the **"starting spotlight"**—it shines equally on all **N** items with amplitude $1/\sqrt{N}$ each. The probability of being on a marked item initially is only $M/N$.

### 2.3 The Two Fundamental Operators

Grover's iteration is the composition of two reflections:

**Operator 1 — Oracle Reflection ($U_f$):**
$$U_f = I - 2\sum_{w \in \text{marked}} \vert w \rangle\langle w \vert = I - 2P_{\text{marked}}$$

It negates the amplitude of marked (winner) states and leaves unmarked states untouched.

**Operator 2 — Diffusion / Inversion-about-the-Mean Operator ($D$):**
$$D = 2\vert s \rangle\langle s \vert - I$$

It reflects any state about the uniform superposition $\vert s \rangle$—equivalently, inverts the amplitudes about their mean value.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mean Inversion Trick:** If amplitudes are $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_N$ with mean $\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum a_i$, the diffusion operator maps each $a_i \rightarrow 2\mu - a_i$. Amplitudes *above* the mean grow; amplitudes *below* the mean shrink. This is the **amplification engine**.

**Grover Operator (one iteration):**
$$G = D \cdot U_f = (2\vert s \rangle\langle s \vert - I)(I - 2P_{\text{marked}})$$

### 2.4 Geometric Interpretation — Rotation in 2D Subspace

The entire algorithm operates in the 2-dimensional subspace spanned by:

- $\vert w \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\sum_{w \in \text{marked}} \vert w \rangle$ (good subspace)
- $\vert r \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N-M}}\sum_{x \notin \text{marked}} \vert x \rangle$ (bad subspace)

The initial state decomposes as:

$$\vert s \rangle = \sin\theta \vert w \rangle + \cos\theta \vert r \rangle$$

where $\theta$ is defined by the trigonometric identity:

$$\sin\theta = \sqrt{\frac{M}{N}}, \quad \cos\theta = \sqrt{\frac{N-M}{N}}$$

The Grover operator **G** acts as a pure rotation by angle **2θ** in this 2D plane:

$$G = \begin{pmatrix} \cos 2\theta & -\sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & \cos 2\theta \end{pmatrix}_{\{w,r\}}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Search space size | $N = 2^n$ | n = number of qubits |
| 2 | Oracle (phase-kickback) | $U_f \vert x \rangle = (-1)^{f(x)} \vert x \rangle$ | Flips sign of marked states |
| 3 | Oracle as projector | $U_f = I - 2P_{\text{marked}}$ | Hermitian & unitary |
| 4 | Uniform superposition | $\vert s \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_{x} \vert x \rangle$ | Initial state after $H^{\otimes n}$ |
| 5 | Diffusion operator | $D = 2\vert s \rangle\langle s \vert - I$ | Inversion about mean |
| 6 | Grover iteration | $G = D \cdot U_f$ | Single amplification step |
| 7 | Rotation angle | $\sin\theta = \sqrt{M/N}$ | For M marked items |
| 8 | State after k iterations | $\vert \psi_k \rangle = \sin((2k+1)\theta)\vert w \rangle + \cos((2k+1)\theta)\vert r \rangle$ | Geometric rotation by $2k\theta$ |
| 9 | Success probability | $P_{\text{success}}(k) = \sin^2((2k+1)\theta)$ | Probability of measuring a marked item |
| 10 | Optimal # of iterations | $k_{\text{opt}} = \left\lfloor \frac{\pi}{4\theta} \right\rfloor \approx \frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N/M}$ | Worst case (M = 1) |
| 11 | Quantum speedup factor | $\sqrt{N}$ | Quadratic improvement |
| 12 | Lower bound (BBBV) | $\Omega(\sqrt{N})$ | Grover is provably optimal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Memory Aid:** Memorize formulas 2, 5, 6, 7, 9, and 10. The "magic number" $\frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N}$ appears in nearly every exam question on this topic.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Grover's algorithm is not just theoretical; it amplifies the query complexity of **any** quantum procedure that uses an oracle:

- **Cryptanalysis:** Quadratic speedup against symmetric ciphers (e.g., AES) and hash preimage search.
- **NP-complete problem heuristics:** Used as a subroutine in quantum SAT solvers and optimization.
- **Quantum Machine Learning:** Subroutines in quantum k-medians, nearest-neighbor search, and recommendation systems.
- **Database & unstructured data mining:** Quantum associative memory retrieval.
- **Constraint satisfaction problems:** Quantum branch-and-bound solvers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Grover Operator as a 2D Rotation

**Step 1 — Express $\vert s \rangle$ in the $\{$`w`$, $`r`$\}$ basis:**

By definition, $\vert w \rangle$ and $\vert r \rangle$ are orthonormal, and the initial superposition is a uniform amplitude $1/\sqrt{N}$ on every basis state. Decomposing:

$$\vert s \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_{w \in \text{marked}} \vert w \rangle + \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_{x \notin \text{marked}} \vert x \rangle$$

Re-writing in terms of $\vert w \rangle$ and $\vert r \rangle$:

$$\vert s \rangle = \sqrt{\frac{M}{N}}\vert w \rangle + \sqrt{\frac{N-M}{N}}\vert r \rangle$$

This identifies:

$$\sin\theta = \sqrt{\frac{M}{N}}, \quad \cos\theta = \sqrt{\frac{N-M}{N}}$$

so that $\vert s \rangle = \sin\theta \vert w \rangle + \cos\theta \vert r \rangle$. *(End of Step 1.)*

**Step 2 — Action of $U_f$ in the $\{$`w`$, $`r`$\}$ basis:**

The oracle flips the sign of every marked basis state, so:

$$U_f \vert w \rangle = -\vert w \rangle, \quad U_f \vert r \rangle = +\vert r \rangle$$

In matrix form (with basis ordered as $\{\vert w \rangle, \vert r \rangle\}$):

$$U_f = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & +1 \end{pmatrix}$$

This is a reflection about the $\vert r \rangle$ axis. *(End of Step 2.)*

**Step 3 — Express $D$ in the same basis:**

Since $D = 2\vert s \rangle\langle s \vert - I$, substitute $\vert s \rangle = \sin\theta \vert w \rangle + \cos\theta \vert r \rangle$:

$$D = 2(\sin\theta \vert w \rangle + \cos\theta \vert r \rangle)(\sin\theta \langle w \vert + \cos\theta \langle r \vert) - I$$

Expanding:

$$D = 2\sin^2\theta \vert w \rangle\langle w \vert + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta(\vert w \rangle\langle r \vert + \vert r \rangle\langle w \vert) + 2\cos^2\theta \vert r \rangle\langle r \vert - I$$

Using $I = \vert w \rangle\langle w \vert + \vert r \rangle\langle r \rangle$ and $2\sin^2\theta - 1 = -\cos 2\theta$, $2\cos^2\theta - 1 = \cos 2\theta$, $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$D = -\cos 2\theta \vert w \rangle\langle w \vert + \sin 2\theta \vert w \rangle\langle r \vert + \sin 2\theta \vert r \rangle\langle w \vert + \cos 2\theta \vert r \rangle\langle r \vert$$

Hence:

$$D = \begin{pmatrix} -\cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & \cos 2\theta \end{pmatrix}$$

This is a reflection about the $\vert s \rangle$ axis. *(End of Step 3.)*

**Step 4 — Compute $G = D \cdot U_f$:**

$$G = \begin{pmatrix} -\cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & \cos 2\theta \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & +1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ -\sin 2\theta & \cos 2\theta \end{pmatrix}$$

Multiplying by $-1$ on the $\vert w \rangle$ component effectively *reflects about $\vert s \rangle$* followed by *reflection about $\vert r \rangle$*, which together form a **rotation by $2\theta$** (the composition of two reflections equals a rotation through twice the angle between the reflecting axes). *(End of Step 4.)*

### 3.2 Derivation: State after k Grover Iterations

Starting with $\vert \psi_0 \rangle = \vert s \rangle = \sin\theta \vert w \rangle + \cos\theta \vert r \rangle$, each application of $G$ rotates the state by $2\theta$ toward $\vert w \rangle$. By induction:

$$\vert \psi_k \rangle = G^k \vert s \rangle = \sin((2k+1)\theta) \vert w \rangle + \cos((2k+1)\theta) \vert r \rangle$$

The probability of measuring a marked item after **k** iterations is therefore:

$$P_{\text{success}}(k) = \vert \langle w \vert \psi_k \rangle \vert^2 = \sin^2((2k+1)\theta)$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Optimal Number of Iterations

To maximize $P_{\text{success}}(k)$, we need:

$$(2k+1)\theta \approx \frac{\pi}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\pi}{4\theta} - \frac{1}{2}$$

Dropping the constant offset (negligible for large N):

$$k_{\text{opt}} \approx \frac{\pi}{4\theta} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{\theta}$$

For the typical case **M = 1** (one marked item), $\theta \approx \sin\theta = 1/\sqrt{N}$ for large **N**, so:

$$\boxed{k_{\text{opt}} \approx \frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N}}$$

For **M** marked items, generalize to $k_{\text{opt}} \approx \frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N/M}$. *(End of Derivation.)*

### 3.4 Worked Example: N = 4, M = 1 (Exact Solve in 1 Iteration)

Let **N = 4**, so **n = 2** qubits. The marked state is **$\vert 11 \rangle$**.

**Step A — Initialize:**
$$\vert s \rangle = H^{\otimes 2}\vert 00 \rangle = \frac{1}{2}(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 01 \rangle + \vert 10 \rangle + \vert 11 \rangle)$$

**Step B — Compute $\theta$:**
$$\sin\theta = \sqrt{\frac{M}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta = \frac{\pi}{6} = 30^\circ$$

**Step C — Optimal k:**
$$k_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\pi}{4\theta} = \frac{\pi}{4 \cdot \pi/6} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$

Since **k** must be an integer, $k_{\text{opt}} = 1$ (a single iteration gives probability 1).

**Step D — Verify state after 1 iteration:**

The good state is $\vert w \rangle = \vert 11 \rangle$ and the bad subspace state is $\vert r \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 01 \rangle + \vert 10 \rangle)$.

Express $\vert s \rangle$:
$$\vert s \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4}}\vert 11 \rangle + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{4}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 01 \rangle + \vert 10 \rangle) = \frac{1}{2}\vert w \rangle + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\vert r \rangle$$

After k = 1 iteration:
$$\vert \psi_1 \rangle = \sin(3\theta)\vert w \rangle + \cos(3\theta)\vert r \rangle = \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\vert w \rangle + \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\vert r \rangle = 1 \cdot \vert w \rangle + 0 = \vert 11 \rangle$$

**Conclusion:** Measurement yields $\vert 11 \rangle$ with **probability = 1** (100% success in a single Grover iteration). This is one of the special "lucky" cases where $N = 2^n = 4$ is a perfect square matching $\theta = \pi/6$.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Success Probability for Various k

| N | M | $\theta$ | $k_{\text{opt}}$ | $P_{\text{success}}$ at $k_{\text{opt}}$ | Over-rotation issue? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 1 | $\pi/6$ | 1 | **1.000** | No — exact |
| 8 | 1 | $\approx 0.361$ rad | 2 | $\sin^2(5\theta) \approx 0.945$ | Mild |
| 16 | 1 | $\pi/8$ | 2 | $\sin^2(5\pi/8) \approx 0.854$ | Yes — slight overshoot |
| 64 | 1 | $\approx 0.125$ | 6 | $\approx 0.999$ | Negligible |
| 1024 | 1 | $\approx 0.031$ | 25 | $\approx 0.9999$ | Negligible |
| N (general, M=1) | 1 | $1/\sqrt{N}$ | $\lfloor \frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N} \rfloor$ | $\geq 0.999$ for large N | Negligible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overshoot Phenomenon:** For small N, the discrete nature of the rotation can cause *over-rotation*—the state swings past $\vert w \rangle$. The algorithm still works probabilistically, but success is < 100%. In practice, one runs Grover with a "checking" subroutine that measures early to avoid overshoot.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Single Grover Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input: psi_in = a_w w + a_r r"] --> B["Oracle U_f: Flip Sign of Marked States"]
    B --> C["Intermediate: -a_w w + a_r r"]
    C --> D["Diffusion D: Inversion About Mean"]
    D --> E["Output: psi_out = a_w' w + a_r' r"]
    E --> F{"Check: Has angle reached pi/2?"}
    F -- "No" --> A
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Measure: Output is the Marked State"]
```

### 4.2 High-Level Circuit Architecture of Grover's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0["Start: n Qubits in State 0,0...0"] --> A1["Apply H to all n qubits: Create Equal Superposition s"]
    A1 --> A2["Grover Iteration Block 1: Oracle + Diffusion"]
    A2 --> A3["Grover Iteration Block 2: Oracle + Diffusion"]
    A3 --> A4["Dots: Repeat k_opt times"]
    A4 --> A5["Grover Iteration Block k_opt: Oracle + Diffusion"]
    A5 --> A6["Measurement in Computational Basis"]
    A6 --> A7["Classical Post-Processing: Read Out Marked Index x"]
```

### 4.3 Geometric Rotation in 2D Subspace

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0["Initial State s on the r-axis side at angle theta"]
    R1["After 1 Grover Iteration: rotate by 2 theta"]
    R2["After 2 Grover Iterations: rotate by 4 theta"]
    R3["After k Grover Iterations: rotate by 2k theta"]
    R4["Target: w axis at angle pi/2"]
    R0 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
```

### 4.4 Amplitude Amplification Across Iterations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q0["Iteration 0: All amplitudes equal 1/4; P_success = 0.25"]
    Q1["Iteration 1: Marked amplitude boosted; P_success approx 0.78"]
    Q2["Iteration 2: Marked amplitude near maximum; P_success approx 0.94"]
    Q3["Iteration 3: P_success begins to decrease if overshooting"]
    Q0 --> Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Circuit Realisation:** The oracle $U_f$ is implemented using a multi-controlled **phase gate** (CCZ, CCCZ, ...) where the marked state triggers a $\pi$ phase shift. The diffusion operator $D$ is implemented as $H^{\otimes n} \cdot (2\vert 0 \rangle\langle 0 \vert - I) \cdot H^{\otimes n}$, where the inner reflection is a multi-controlled Z gate surrounded by NOTs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**  
Define the **quantum oracle** used in Grover's algorithm. Write the action of the oracle $U_f$ on a basis state $\vert x \rangle$ and explain the concept of **phase kickback**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A quantum oracle is a unitary black-box operator that encodes a Boolean function $f : \{0,1\}^n \rightarrow \{0,1\}$ as a phase on quantum states. Its action on a basis state is:

$$U_f \vert x \rangle = (-1)^{f(x)} \vert x \rangle$$

For marked states, $f(x) = 1$ and a phase of $-1$ is applied; for unmarked states, $f(x) = 0$ and the state is unchanged. **Phase kickback** refers to the phenomenon where the function's value is "kicked" into a global phase of the state vector rather than being stored in a separate output qubit, allowing the algorithm to exploit quantum interference.

*(Award 1 mark for oracle definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for phase kickback explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**  
What is the **optimal number of Grover iterations** required to find a single marked item in a database of N entries? Justify the answer with the rotation angle $\theta$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The optimal number of iterations is:

$$k_{\text{opt}} = \left\lfloor \frac{\pi}{4\theta} \right\rfloor, \quad \text{where} \ \ \sin\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}$$

For large **N**, $\theta \approx 1/\sqrt{N}$, giving the asymptotic result:

$$k_{\text{opt}} \approx \frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N}$$

This is justified because after **k** iterations, the success probability is $P(k) = \sin^2((2k+1)\theta)$. To maximize this, we need $(2k+1)\theta \approx \pi/2$, yielding the formula above. The quadratic speedup over classical search is a direct consequence of this $\sqrt{N}$ scaling.

*(Award 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the rotation justification, 1 mark for the asymptotic form.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Grover Search Algorithm** in detail with a clear block diagram. Derive the expression for the rotation angle $\theta$ and the state of the system after **k** iterations. Show that the probability of finding the marked state is $\sin^2((2k+1)\theta)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a database of size **N = 64** with a single marked item. Compute:  
(i) the rotation angle $\theta$,  
(ii) the optimal number of Grover iterations, and  
(iii) the success probability after the optimal number of iterations.  
Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed Explanation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Problem Setup:** Grover's algorithm solves the unstructured search problem: given $f(x) = 1$ for marked states and $f(x) = 0$ otherwise, find any $x$ with $f(x) = 1$. The search space is $\{0,1\}^n$ with $N = 2^n$ elements. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 2 — Initialization:** Apply $H^{\otimes n}$ to $\vert 0 \rangle^{\otimes n}$ to obtain the uniform superposition:

$$\vert s \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{x=0}^{N-1} \vert x \rangle$$

This state has equal overlap with both the "good" subspace $\vert w \rangle$ (spanned by marked states) and the "bad" subspace $\vert r \rangle$ (spanned by unmarked states). *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Grover Iteration = Two Reflections:** The Grover operator $G = D \cdot U_f$ consists of:

- **Oracle reflection $U_f = I - 2P_{\text{marked}}$** — flips the sign of amplitudes on marked states.
- **Diffusion $D = 2\vert s \rangle\langle s \vert - I$** — reflects the state about $\vert s \rangle$, effectively inverting amplitudes about their mean. *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 4 — Rotation Angle Derivation:** Decompose $\vert s \rangle$ in the $\{$`w`$, $`r`$\}$ basis. Since $\vert s \rangle$ has amplitude $1/\sqrt{N}$ on each of the **M** marked basis states:

$$\vert s \rangle = \sqrt{\frac{M}{N}}\vert w \rangle + \sqrt{\frac{N-M}{N}}\vert r \rangle = \sin\theta \vert w \rangle + \cos\theta \vert r \rangle$$

Thus $\sin\theta = \sqrt{M/N}$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — State after k iterations:** The product of two reflections is a rotation by twice the angle between the reflecting axes. Since $U_f$ reflects about $\vert r \rangle$ and $D$ reflects about $\vert s \rangle$, **G** rotates by $2\theta$. After **k** iterations, the state is:

$$\vert \psi_k \rangle = G^k \vert s \rangle = \sin((2k+1)\theta)\vert w \rangle + \cos((2k+1)\theta)\vert r \rangle$$

**Step 6 — Success Probability:** The probability of collapsing to a marked state upon measurement is:

$$P_{\text{success}}(k) = \vert \langle w \vert \psi_k \rangle \vert^2 = \sin^2((2k+1)\theta)$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 7 — Block Diagram:** (Draw the architecture from Section 4.2 of these notes.) *(0.5 mark)*

---

**(b) Worked Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: **N = 64**, **M = 1**.

**(i) Rotation angle:**

$$\sin\theta = \sqrt{\frac{M}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{64}} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

$$\theta = \arcsin(0.125) \approx 0.1253 \ \text{radians} \approx 7.18^\circ$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Optimal number of iterations:**

$$k_{\text{opt}} = \left\lfloor \frac{\pi}{4\theta} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{3.14159}{4 \times 0.1253} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 6.266 \right\rfloor = 6$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Success probability at $k_{\text{opt}} = 6$:**

$$P_{\text{success}}(6) = \sin^2((2 \cdot 6 + 1)\theta) = \sin^2(13 \times 0.1253) = \sin^2(1.6293)$$

$$\sin(1.6293 \ \text{rad}) \approx \sin(93.36^\circ) \approx 0.9982$$

$$P_{\text{success}} \approx (0.9982)^2 \approx 0.9964 = 99.64\%$$

*(2 marks)*

**Comment:** Grover's algorithm finds the marked state in just **6 oracle queries** with success probability $\approx 99.64\%$, whereas classical search requires on average **32** queries and up to **64** in the worst case. This is a $\sim 5.3\times$ speedup, consistent with the theoretical $\sqrt{64} = 8$ asymptotic bound (the small discrepancy is due to discrete overshoot effects). *(1 mark)*

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** With suitable mathematical expressions, describe the **oracle operator** and the **diffusion operator** in Grover's algorithm. Show that the Grover operator $G = D \cdot U_f$ can be expressed as a 2×2 rotation matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **geometric interpretation** of Grover's algorithm. Using the 2D rotation picture, prove that the amplitude of the marked state increases monotonically with each iteration up to a maximum and then decreases (over-rotation). How is the optimal stopping condition determined? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Oracle, Diffusion, and Rotation Matrix (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Oracle Operator $U_f$:**  
The oracle is a unitary that marks the solution states. It applies a phase of $-1$ to every marked basis vector and leaves unmarked states unchanged:

$$U_f = I - 2\sum_{w \in \text{marked}} \vert w \rangle\langle w \vert = I - 2P_{\text{marked}}$$

In the $\{$`w`$, $`r`$\}$ basis, where $\vert w \rangle$ is the equal superposition of marked states and $\vert r \rangle$ is the equal superposition of unmarked states, this matrix is diagonal:

$$U_f = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & +1 \end{pmatrix}$$

This represents a **reflection about the $\vert r \rangle$ axis**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Diffusion Operator $D$:**  
The diffusion operator inverts amplitudes about their mean. It is constructed as:

$$D = 2\vert s \rangle\langle s \vert - I$$

In the $\{$`w`$, $`r`$\}$ basis, after substituting $\vert s \rangle = \sin\theta \vert w \rangle + \cos\theta \vert r \rangle$ and using double-angle identities, we obtain:

$$D = \begin{pmatrix} -\cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & \cos 2\theta \end{pmatrix}$$

This represents a **reflection about the $\vert s \rangle$ axis**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Grover Operator as Rotation:**  
The product $G = D \cdot U_f$ is:

$$G = \begin{pmatrix} -\cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ \sin 2\theta & \cos 2\theta \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & +1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \sin 2\theta \\ -\sin 2\theta & \cos 2\theta \end{pmatrix}$$

This is precisely a **rotation by angle $2\theta$** in the 2D plane spanned by $\{$`w`$, $`r`$\}$. The composition of two reflections is geometrically equivalent to a rotation by twice the angle between the reflection axes. *(3 marks)*

---

**(b) Geometric Interpretation and Stopping Condition (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup:** The state vector lives in a 2D plane spanned by $\vert w \rangle$ and $\vert r \rangle$. Initially, $\vert s \rangle$ makes an angle $\theta$ with $\vert r \rangle$, where $\sin\theta = \sqrt{M/N}$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Effect of Each Iteration:** Each application of **G** rotates the state vector counter-clockwise by $2\theta$. After **k** iterations, the state makes angle $(2k+1)\theta$ with $\vert r \rangle$, i.e., $(2k+1)\theta - \theta = 2k\theta$ of rotation has occurred. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Monotonic Increase and Overshoot:** The success probability is $\sin^2((2k+1)\theta)$, which increases as $(2k+1)\theta$ approaches $\pi/2$ from below. Once $(2k+1)\theta$ crosses $\pi/2$, the sine begins to decrease, leading to **over-rotation** and reduced success probability. Hence the probability does *not* increase monotonically forever—it rises, peaks near $(2k+1)\theta \approx \pi/2$, then falls. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Optimal Stopping Condition:** The optimal iteration count is:

$$k_{\text{opt}} = \left\lfloor \frac{\pi}{4\theta} \right\rfloor \approx \frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{\frac{N}{M}}$$

For **M = 1**, this gives $k_{\text{opt}} \approx (\pi/4)\sqrt{N}$. At this point, $(2k_{\text{opt}}+1)\theta \approx \pi/2$ and the success probability is maximised. For large **N**, the success probability approaches **1**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Robust Stopping (Checking Trick):** Since overshoot is small for large **N**, a "checking" subroutine can be inserted: at each step, perform a partial measurement on a copy of the state. If the marked item is found, stop; otherwise continue. This avoids the discrete overshoot pitfall. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the rotation angle $\theta$ with the iteration angle $2\theta$.** The Grover operator rotates by **$2\theta$**, but the state angle from $\vert r \rangle$ is $(2k+1)\theta$. Many students write $(2k+1)\theta$ as just $k\theta$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that $\sin\theta = \sqrt{M/N}$, not $1/N$.** The probability is $1/N$, but the *amplitude* (and hence the trigonometric angle) uses the *square root*.
> 3. **Stating "$k = \sqrt{N}$ iterations" without the $\pi/4$ factor.** The correct optimal count is $\frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N}$. Marks deducted for omitting the constant.
> 4. **Mixing up the operator order.** It is **G = D · U_f**, not $U_f \cdot D$. Reversing them rotates in the wrong direction.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention that Grover is provably optimal** (BBBV lower bound). Worth 1 mark in "explain" type questions.
> 6. **Confusing structured vs unstructured search.** Grover applies to *unstructured* databases only. Mentioning that it cannot beat classical search on *sorted* data is a plus point.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Unstructured Search Problem:** Find $x$ such that $f(x) = 1$ in a database of $N = 2^n$ items without any ordering.
- ⚡ **Grover's Algorithm** gives a **quadratic speedup**: $O(\sqrt{N})$ oracle queries vs $O(N)$ classically.
- ⚡ **Two main operators** — Oracle reflection $U_f$ (negates marked amplitudes) and Diffusion $D = 2\vert s \rangle\langle s \vert - I$ (inverts about the mean).
- ⚡ **Grover operator:** $G = D \cdot U_f$ — a rotation by $2\theta$ in the 2D subspace.
- ⚡ **Rotation angle:** $\sin\theta = \sqrt{M/N}$; for $M = 1$ and large $N$, $\theta \approx 1/\sqrt{N}$.
- ⚡ **State after k iterations:** $\vert \psi_k \rangle = \sin((2k+1)\theta)\vert w \rangle + \cos((2k+1)\theta)\vert r \rangle$.
- ⚡ **Success Probability:** $P(k) = \sin^2((2k+1)\theta)$.
- ⚡ **Optimal iterations:** $k_{\text{opt}} \approx \frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N/M}$ (use floor for integer k).
- ⚡ **Overshoot pitfall:** Stopping too late reduces success — use the "checking trick" or compute $k_{\text{opt}}$ carefully.
- ⚡ **Provably optimal:** Grover's $O(\sqrt{N})$ is the theoretical maximum (BBBV theorem).
- ⚡ **Geometric picture:** Two reflections ⇒ rotation; the state vector spirals toward $\vert w \rangle$ in the 2D $\{$`w`$, $`r`$\}$ plane.
- ⚡ **Real-world impact:** Cryptanalysis, NP-hard heuristics, quantum ML subroutines, database search, SAT solvers.
- ⚡ **High-yield exam keywords to memorize:** *phase kickback, amplitude amplification, inversion about the mean, geometric rotation, BBBV lower bound, overshoot, $\pi/4\sqrt{N}$.*

---

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line KTU Summary:** *Grover's algorithm uses two quantum reflections — an oracle and a diffusion — to rotate the uniform superposition toward a marked basis state in $\frac{\pi}{4}\sqrt{N/M}$ iterations, achieving a provably optimal quadratic speedup for unstructured search.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantum Search as a Quantum Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT634 - QUANTUM COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quantum Search as a Quantum Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> *Quantum Search as a Quantum Simulation* refers to the reformulation of Grover's quantum search algorithm as the continuous-time evolution of a quantum state under a specifically crafted Hamiltonian. Rather than viewing search as a sequence of discrete unitary reflections, we interpret it as the Schrödinger dynamics of a two-level quantum subsystem driven by an "oracle Hamiltonian." This approach, pioneered by Farhi and Gutmann, reveals that the quadratic speed-up of Grover's algorithm arises naturally from the energy-gap structure of the search Hamiltonian, providing an elegant bridge between **quantum algorithms** and **quantum simulation**.

### 1.1 The Two Equivalent Paradigms of Search

| Paradigm | Mathematical Object | Time Parameter |
|----------|--------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Discrete Grover** | Iterated unitary operator $G = (2\vert s\rangle\langle s\vert - I)O$ | Discrete iteration count $k$ |
| **Continuous Simulation** | Hamiltonian $H$ with $U(t) = e^{-iHt/\hbar}$ | Continuous time $t$ |

The two paradigms are *deeply connected*: the continuous-time evolution can be discretized via **Trotter–Suzuki decomposition** to recover the standard discrete Grover iteration. Thus, Grover's algorithm is *literally* a quantum simulation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "Rolling a Marble on a Hill"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition**
> Imagine you place a marble on the surface of a hill. The hill's shape is engineered by the search problem: the marked item is at the **peak**, and the uniform superposition $\vert s\rangle$ is at the **valley**. Quantum mechanics says the marble does not roll classically — it *tunnels* and *interferes* with itself. As time flows forward, the marble's probability density naturally accumulates at the peak. The Hamiltonian $H$ is literally the "shape of the hill" — it tells nature how to evolve the system. Your job as an algorithm designer is to *engineer* this Hamiltonian so that nature does the search for you.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Planck's reduced constant:** $\hbar \approx 1.0545718 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s (often set to $\hbar = 1$ in quantum information units)
- **Search space size:** $N = 2^n$ for an $n$-qubit register
- **Marked items:** $M$ (often $M = 1$ for unique solution)
- **Runtime:** $T = \mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N/M})$ — the celebrated **quadratic speed-up**

> [!TIP]
> **Why this viewpoint matters in KTU 2024 syllabus**
> The quantum-simulation perspective unifies *algorithm design* and *physical simulation*, showing that the same hardware performing Hamiltonian simulation (e.g., for chemistry) can also execute search. This is foundational to understanding variational quantum algorithms (VQE, QAOA) and analog quantum computing paradigms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Oracle Hamiltonian — "Engineered Energy Landscape"

To simulate search, we construct a Hamiltonian whose **ground state** (or a marked eigenstate) encodes the solution. The standard construction uses two pieces:

1. **Diagonal Hamiltonian (problem encoding):**
$H_w = \vert w\rangle\langle w\vert$, which assigns energy $1$ to the marked state $\vert w\rangle$ and $0$ to all orthogonal states.

2. **Mixing / Driver Hamiltonian:**
$H_s = \vert s\rangle\langle s\vert$, which assigns energy $1$ to the uniform superposition $\vert s\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_{x=0}^{N-1}\vert x\rangle$ and $0$ otherwise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Farhi–Gutmann Analog Hamiltonian**
> $$\boxed{\,H = \vert w\rangle\langle w\vert + \vert s\rangle\langle s\vert\,}$$
> This Hamiltonian acts non-trivially only on the **two-dimensional subspace** $\mathcal{S} = \text{span}\{\vert w\rangle,\vert w^{\perp}\rangle\}$, where $\vert w^{\perp}\rangle \propto \vert s\rangle - \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\vert w\rangle$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — State Initialization:** Prepare the system in $\vert \psi(0)\rangle = \vert s\rangle$, the uniform superposition.
- **Step 2 — Hamiltonian Engineering:** Define $H = \vert w\rangle\langle w\vert + \vert s\rangle\langle s\vert$ that encodes the search problem into the *energy spectrum*.
- **Step 3 — Schrödinger Evolution:** Let nature evolve the state via
$$\vert \psi(t)\rangle = e^{-iHt/\hbar}\vert s\rangle.$$
- **Step 4 — Measurement:** At the optimal time $T = \pi\hbar\sqrt{N}/(2E)$, measure in the computational basis. Outcome is $\vert w\rangle$ with probability $\approx 1$.
- **Step 5 — Repeat:** Because the evolution is *periodic* (a Rabi-like oscillation), repeat $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N})$ times to drive the success probability to unity.

### 2.3 Eigenstructure of the Search Hamiltonian

Within the 2D subspace $\mathcal{S}$, the two eigenstates of $H$ are:

$$\vert \psi_{\pm}\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\vert s\rangle \pm \vert w^{\perp}\rangle\right)$$

with corresponding eigen-energies:

$$E_{\pm} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)$$

The **energy gap** that drives the search is:

$$\Delta E = E_{+} - E_{-} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}$$

This gap *directly* explains the $\sqrt{N}$ runtime: the oscillation period $T \propto 1/\Delta E = \sqrt{N}$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Use |
|---|----------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $i\hbar\,\partial_t\vert\psi\rangle = H\vert\psi\rangle$ | Time-dependent Schrödinger equation | Foundation of all unitary evolution |
| 2 | $U(t) = e^{-iHt/\hbar}$ | Unitary time-evolution operator | Simulation engine |
| 3 | $H = \vert w\rangle\langle w\vert + \vert s\rangle\langle s\vert$ | Farhi–Gutmann search Hamiltonian | Continuous-time search |
| 4 | $E_{\pm} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 \pm 1/\sqrt{N})$ | Eigen-energies of $H$ in subspace $\mathcal{S}$ | Gap analysis |
| 5 | $\Delta E = 1/\sqrt{N}$ | Energy gap | Runtime derivation |
| 6 | $T = \pi\hbar/(2\Delta E) = \tfrac{\pi\hbar\sqrt{N}}{2}$ | Optimal evolution time | Required runtime |
| 7 | $G = (2\vert s\rangle\langle s\vert - I)(I - 2\vert w\rangle\langle w\vert)$ | Standard discrete Grover operator | Discrete equivalent |
| 8 | $P_{\text{success}}(t) = \sin^{2}(\Delta E\, t/\hbar)$ | Probability of finding $\vert w\rangle$ | Performance metric |
| 9 | $\vert s\rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_{x=0}^{N-1}\vert x\rangle$ | Uniform superposition | Starting state |
| 10 | $G \approx e^{-iH\Delta t} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta t^{2})$ | Trotter link to discrete Grover | Algorithm equivalence proof |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Why an engineer should care**
> - **Variational Quantum Eigensolvers (VQE)** use Hamiltonian simulation to find ground states of molecules — the *same* mathematical machinery as search.
> - **Quantum Annealing** (D-Wave paradigm) literally implements $H(t) = A(t)H_0 + B(t)H_p$, where $H_p$ is a "problem Hamiltonian" identical in spirit to $\vert w\rangle\langle w\vert$.
> - **QAOA (Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm)** alternates problem and mixer Hamiltonians — this is the discrete Trotterized version of continuous-time search.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous Hamiltonian to Grover's Discrete Operator

**Goal:** Show that the discrete Grover iteration $G$ arises from a first-order Trotter approximation of the continuous-time evolution under $H = H_w + H_s$.

**Step 1 — Define the Two Component Hamiltonians.** Write $H = H_w + H_s$, where

$$
H_w = \vert w\rangle\langle w\vert,\qquad H_s = \vert s\rangle\langle s\vert.
$$

These do **not** commute in general:

$$
[H_w, H_s] = H_w H_s - H_s H_w.
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Trotter's First-Order Decomposition.** For small time step $\Delta t$,

$$
e^{-iH\Delta t/\hbar} = e^{-iH_w\Delta t/\hbar}\,e^{-iH_s\Delta t/\hbar} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta t^{2}).
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Each Exponential.** Using $e^{-i\theta A} = I - (1 - e^{-i\theta})A$ for any projector $A = A^{2}$:

$$
e^{-iH_w\Delta t/\hbar} = I + (e^{-i\Delta t/\hbar} - 1)\vert w\rangle\langle w\vert.
$$

For $\Delta t = \pi\hbar$, this becomes

$$
e^{-iH_w\pi} = I - 2\vert w\rangle\langle w\vert = I_w,
$$

the **inversion-about-the-marked-state** operator (the oracle).

**Step 4 — Repeat for the Mixing Hamiltonian.** Similarly,

$$
e^{-iH_s\pi} = I - 2\vert s\rangle\langle s\vert = I_s.
$$

**Step 5 — Compose into the Grover Operator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= I_s \cdot I_w = (2\vert s\rangle\langle s\vert - I)(I - 2\vert w\rangle\langle w\vert).
\end{aligned}
$$

This *exactly* matches the standard Grover operator (up to a global phase). **Hence, Grover's algorithm is precisely a Trotterized quantum simulation of $H$.** $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Success Probability vs. Time

**Goal:** Derive $P_{\text{success}}(t) = \sin^{2}(\Delta E\, t/\hbar)$ for the continuous-time search.

**Step 1 — Decompose the Initial State.** Decompose $\vert s\rangle$ in the energy eigenbasis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert s\rangle &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\vert \psi_{+}\rangle + \vert \psi_{-}\rangle\right) \\
&\quad + \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert\psi_{+}\rangle - \vert\psi_{-}\rangle)\right)\;\text{(correction from \vert w\rangle overlap)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

For large $N$, the dominant projection is onto $\mathcal{S}$:

$$
\vert s\rangle \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert \psi_{+}\rangle + \vert \psi_{-}\rangle) + \mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{N}).
$$

**Step 2 — Evolve Under the Hamiltonian.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \psi(t)\rangle &= e^{-iH t/\hbar}\vert s\rangle \\
&\approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(e^{-iE_{+}t/\hbar}\vert \psi_{+}\rangle + e^{-iE_{-}t/\hbar}\vert \psi_{-}\rangle\right).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Overlap with the Marked State.** Using $\vert w\rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert \psi_{+}\rangle - \vert \psi_{-}\rangle) + \mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{N})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle w\vert \psi(t)\rangle &\approx \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{-iE_{+}t/\hbar} - e^{-iE_{-}t/\hbar}\right) \\
&= \frac{e^{-i(E_{+}+E_{-})t/(2\hbar)}}{2}\cdot 2i\sin\!\left(\tfrac{(E_{+}-E_{-})t}{2\hbar}\right) \\
&= i\,e^{-i\bar{E}t/\hbar}\,\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta E\, t}{2\hbar}\right).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability.**

$$
P_{\text{success}}(t) = \vert\langle w\vert \psi(t)\rangle\vert^{2} = \sin^{2}\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta E\, t}{2\hbar}\right).
$$

For optimal $t = \pi\hbar/(2\Delta E)$, $P_{\text{success}} = 1$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Continuous vs. Discrete Regimes

| Property | Continuous (Simulation) | Discrete (Grover) |
|----------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Time variable** | Continuous $t$ | Integer iteration $k$ |
| **State update** | $\vert \psi\rangle \leftarrow e^{-iHt/\hbar}\vert \psi\rangle$ | $\vert \psi\rangle \leftarrow G^{k}\vert \psi\rangle$ |
| **Hardware realization** | Analog (e.g., quantum annealers, Rydberg arrays) | Digital gate model |
| **Optimal duration** | $T = \pi\sqrt{N}/(2)$ | $k \approx \pi\sqrt{N}/4$ |
| **Success probability** | $\sin^{2}(\Delta E t / 2\hbar)$ | $\sin^{2}((2k+1)\theta)$ with $\sin\theta = 1/\sqrt{N}$ |
| **Limiting factor** | Decoherence over time $T$ | Gate errors accumulating over $k$ gates |
| **Mathematical basis** | Schrödinger evolution | Combinatorial reflection algebra |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For $N = 1024$ ($n = 10$ qubits) and a single marked item:

- Eigen-energies: $E_{\pm} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 \pm 1/\sqrt{1024}) = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 \pm 1/32) = 0.515625,\; 0.484375$.
- Energy gap: $\Delta E = 1/\sqrt{1024} = 1/32 = 0.03125$.
- Optimal time (in units $\hbar = 1$): $T = \pi/(2\Delta E) = \pi \cdot 16 \approx 50.265$.
- Compare: classical search needs $\sim 512$ queries on average; Grover needs $\sim 25$ iterations. **Speed-up = $\sqrt{N} = 32$.**

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: Quantum Search Simulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Search Problem N=2n items] --> B[Phase 1: State Preparation]
    B --> C[Apply Hadamard to all n qubits]
    C --> D[Uniform Superposition s]
    D --> E[Phase 2: Hamiltonian Evolution]
    E --> F[Simulator H = Hw + Hs]
    F --> G[Schrodinger Evolution e to the power of iHt/hbar]
    G --> H{Optimal Time T reached}
    H -- No --> G
    H -- Yes --> I[Phase 3: Measurement]
    I --> J[Measure in Computational Basis]
    J --> K{Outcome x = w?}
    K -- Yes --> L[SUCCESS: marked item found]
    K -- No --> M[FAILURE: restart]
```

```mermaid
        P1 -. oscillates between .-> P2
    end

    EV[Energy Eigenstates] --> EVP[psi_plus E_plus = 1/2 + 1/(2 sqrt N)]
    EV --> EVM[psi_minus E_minus = 1/2 - 1/(2 sqrt N)]
```

### 4.3 Algorithm Architecture Flow: Trotter Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Hamiltonian H = Hw + Hs] --> T1[Trotter Split Step 1]
    T1 --> E1[exp of -i Hw dt by hbar approx equals Oracle Iw]
    T1 --> E2[exp of -i Hs dt by hbar approx equals Mixer Is]
    E1 --> M[Multiply: G = Is * Iw]
    E2 --> M
    M --> R[Apply G^k to initial state s]
    R --> OUT[Discrete Grover Algorithm - O of sqrt N iterations]

    H --> CT[Continuous Time Evolution e to the power of -iHt/hbar]
    CT --> OUT2[Analog Quantum Simulator - run for T = pi sqrt N by 2]
```
