## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the Farhi–Gutmann analog Hamiltonian for quantum search and state its two eigen-energies within the relevant 2D subspace.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Farhi–Gutmann analog Hamiltonian for searching $N = 2^n$ items with a single marked item $\vert w\rangle$ is
> $$H = \vert w\rangle\langle w\vert + \vert s\rangle\langle s\rangle,$$
> where $\vert s\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_{x}\vert x\rangle$ is the uniform superposition. Within the subspace $\mathcal{S} = \text{span}\{\vert w\rangle,\vert w^{\perp}\rangle\}$, the two eigen-energies are
> $$E_{\pm} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right).$$
> **(1 mark)** Hamiltonian definition, **(1 mark)** subspace identification, **(1 mark)** eigen-energies.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional-II Pattern]**
*Explain in one sentence why the runtime of continuous-time Grover search scales as $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N})$.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The energy gap of the search Hamiltonian is $\Delta E = E_{+} - E_{-} = 1/\sqrt{N}$. The state oscillates between $\vert s\rangle$ and $\vert w\rangle$ at a Rabi frequency $\Delta E/\hbar$, so the time required for one full probability transfer is $T = \pi\hbar/(2\Delta E) = \mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N})$.
> **(1 mark)** gap identification, **(1 mark)** Rabi connection, **(1 mark)** asymptotic form.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model QP]*

**(a)** Derive the eigen-energies of the Farhi–Gutmann Hamiltonian $H = \vert w\rangle\langle w\vert + \vert s\rangle\langle s\vert$ in the 2D subspace spanned by $\vert w\rangle$ and $\vert w^{\perp}\rangle$, where $\vert w^{\perp}\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N-1}}\sum_{x \neq w}\vert x\rangle$. Show that $\vert s\rangle$ lies almost entirely along $\vert w^{\perp}\rangle$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Starting from the Schrödinger equation, derive the success probability $P_{\text{success}}(t)$ of finding the marked item after evolution time $t$. Show that the optimal runtime is $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N})$. **[7 marks]**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Solution — Eigen-Energy Derivation:**

Write the orthogonal complement in the *search space* (excluding $\vert w\rangle$) as

$$
\vert w^{\perp}\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N-1}}\sum_{x \neq w}\vert x\rangle.
$$

Express $\vert s\rangle$ in this basis:

$$
\vert s\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\vert w\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{N-1}{N}}\vert w^{\perp}\rangle.
$$

For $N \gg 1$, $\vert s\rangle \approx \vert w^{\perp}\rangle$, with overlap $\langle s\vert w^{\perp}\rangle = \sqrt{(N-1)/N}$.

Now restrict $H$ to $\mathcal{S}$. The matrix of $H$ in the $\{\vert w\rangle, \vert w^{\perp}\rangle\}$ basis:

$$
[H]_{\mathcal{S}} = \begin{pmatrix} \langle w\vert H\vert w\rangle & \langle w\vert H\vert w^{\perp}\rangle \\ \langle w^{\perp}\vert H\vert w\rangle & \langle w^{\perp}\vert H\vert w^{\perp}\rangle \end{pmatrix}.
$$

Computing each element:

- $\langle w\vert H\vert w\rangle = \langle w\vert w\rangle\langle w\vert w\rangle + \langle w\vert s\rangle\langle s\vert w\rangle = 1 + 1/N$.
- $\langle w\vert H\vert w^{\perp}\rangle = \langle w\vert w\rangle\langle w\vert w^{\perp}\rangle + \langle w\vert s\rangle\langle s\vert w^{\perp}\rangle = 0 + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sqrt{(N-1)/N} = \sqrt{(N-1)/N^{2}}$.
- $\langle w^{\perp}\vert H\vert w^{\perp}\rangle = \langle w^{\perp}\vert w\rangle\langle w\vert w^{\perp}\rangle + \langle w^{\perp}\vert s\rangle\langle s\vert w^{\perp}\rangle = 0 + (N-1)/N$.

Simplify by rescaling and dropping $\mathcal{O}(1/N)$ terms (use the standard approximation valid in the KTU treatment):

$$
[H]_{\mathcal{S}} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1/\sqrt{N} \\ 1/\sqrt{N} & 1 \end{pmatrix}.
$$

Eigenvalues: solve $\det(H - \lambda I) = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1-\lambda)^{2} - \tfrac{1}{N} &= 0 \\
\lambda_{\pm} = 1 \pm \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

With proper normalization (treating $\vert s\rangle \approx \vert w^{\perp}\rangle$ on the relevant scale), the eigenvalues reduce to the textbook form:

$$
\boxed{\,E_{\pm} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(1 \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\right)\,} \qquad \text{(7 marks: 2 setup, 2 matrix, 2 determinant, 1 final answer)}.
$$

**(b) Solution — Success Probability:**

Initialize $\vert \psi(0)\rangle = \vert s\rangle$. Evolve under $H$:

$$
\vert \psi(t)\rangle = e^{-iHt/\hbar}\vert s\rangle.
$$

Expand $\vert s\rangle$ in the energy eigenbasis $\{\vert \psi_{+}\rangle, \vert \psi_{-}\rangle\}$:

$$
\vert s\rangle = c_{+}\vert \psi_{+}\rangle + c_{-}\vert \psi_{-}\rangle.
$$

Compute coefficients. The eigenstates are $\vert \psi_{\pm}\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert s\rangle_{\text{approx}} \pm \vert w\rangle_{\text{approx}})$ on $\mathcal{S}$, giving $|c_{\pm}|^{2} = 1/2$.

After evolution:

$$
\vert \psi(t)\rangle = c_{+}e^{-iE_{+}t/\hbar}\vert \psi_{+}\rangle + c_{-}e^{-iE_{-}t/\hbar}\vert \psi_{-}\rangle.
$$

Amplitude on $\vert w\rangle$ (using $\vert w\rangle \approx \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert\psi_{+}\rangle - \vert\psi_{-}\rangle)$):

$$
\langle w\vert \psi(t)\rangle = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{-iE_{+}t/\hbar} - e^{-iE_{-}t/\hbar}\right).
$$

Probability:

$$
P(t) = \frac{1}{4}\left\vert e^{-iE_{+}t/\hbar} - e^{-iE_{-}t/\hbar}\right\vert^{2} = \sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{(E_{+}-E_{-})t}{2\hbar}\right) = \sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\Delta E\, t}{2\hbar}\right).
$$

Since $\Delta E = 1/\sqrt{N}$, the optimal time giving $P = 1$ is:

$$
T = \frac{\pi\hbar}{2\Delta E} = \frac{\pi\hbar\sqrt{N}}{2} = \mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N}).
$$

$\blacksquare$ **(7 marks: 2 expansion, 2 amplitude, 2 probability, 1 conclusion)**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Show using first-order Trotter–Suzuki decomposition that the discrete Grover operator $G = (2\vert s\rangle\langle s\vert - I)(I - 2\vert w\rangle\langle w\vert)$ is equivalent to one time step of continuous-time evolution under $H = \vert w\rangle\langle w\vert + \vert s\rangle\langle s\vert$. State the time step used. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss how the quantum-simulation view of Grover's algorithm informs the design of modern hybrid algorithms such as the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA). Why is the runtime $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N})$ sometimes called the "**optimality of Grover's algorithm**"? **[7 marks]**

##### Model Solution (Sketch — Full Expansion Expected by Students):

**(a)** Split $H = H_w + H_s$ with $H_w = \vert w\rangle\langle w\vert$, $H_s = \vert s\rangle\langle s\vert$. Trotter first order:

$$
e^{-iH\Delta t/\hbar} \approx e^{-iH_w\Delta t/\hbar}\,e^{-iH_s\Delta t/\hbar}.
$$

For any projector $A$, $e^{-i\theta A} = I + (e^{-i\theta}-1)A$. Choose $\Delta t = \pi\hbar$:

- $e^{-i\pi H_w} = I - 2\vert w\rangle\langle w\vert = I_w$.
- $e^{-i\pi H_s} = I - 2\vert s\rangle\langle s\vert = I_s$.

Multiplying: $G = I_s I_w$ — matches Grover. **(7 marks)**.

**(b)** QAOA alternates two Hamiltonians (problem $H_p$ and mixer $H_m$) at discrete times — exactly the Trotter picture of analog search. The $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N})$ runtime is provably optimal among *all* quantum algorithms for unstructured search (Bennett–Bernstein–Brassard–Vazirani lower bound), hence the term "optimality." **(7 marks)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Zones**
> 1. **Forgetting the global phase:** $G = -I_s I_w$, not $+I_s I_w$. Many students drop the minus sign and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mis-identifying the subspace:** $\vert s\rangle$ is **not** exactly $\vert w^{\perp}\rangle$; it has an $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{N})$ overlap with $\vert w\rangle$. Always state the approximation explicitly.
> 3. **Wrong eigen-energy formula:** Students sometimes quote $E_{\pm} = \pm 1/\sqrt{N}$ (forgetting the "$1/2$" offset). Use the boxed form above.
> 4. **Confusing Trotter step with optimal time:** The Trotter step is $\Delta t = \pi\hbar$ (a discretization choice), while the *search* optimal time is $T = \pi\hbar\sqrt{N}/2$. They are different by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why simulation" explanation:** Many answers just write formulas. Always justify *why* this is a simulation: because the search Hamiltonian is implemented by controllable physical interactions in analog quantum hardware.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quantum Search as Quantum Simulation**

- **Core idea:** Grover's algorithm = Trotterized Schrödinger evolution under $H = \vert w\rangle\langle w\vert + \vert s\rangle\langle s\vert$.
- **Two paradigms:** Continuous (Hamiltonian) vs. discrete (Grover iterations) — mathematically equivalent via Trotter.
- **Eigen-energies (must memorize):** $E_{\pm} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 \pm 1/\sqrt{N})$.
- **Energy gap (must memorize):** $\Delta E = 1/\sqrt{N}$.
- **Optimal runtime (must memorize):** $T = \pi\hbar\sqrt{N}/2$ (continuous) ↔ $k \approx \pi\sqrt{N}/4$ (discrete).
- **Success probability:** $P(t) = \sin^{2}(\Delta E\, t / 2\hbar)$ — pure Rabi-like oscillation in the 2D subspace.
- **Subspace geometry:** The dynamics live in $\mathcal{S} = \text{span}\{\vert w\rangle, \vert w^{\perp}\rangle\}$; outside this subspace, $H$ has a vast zero-energy eigenspace that is *invariant*.
- **Physical constants:** $\hbar$ (often set to 1), $N = 2^n$.
- **Modern relevance:** QAOA, quantum annealing, VQE all inherit this framework.
- **Lower-bound optimality:** $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N})$ is *provably optimal* — no quantum algorithm can do unstructured search faster.
- **Connection to analog hardware:** Rydberg atom arrays, trapped ions, and superconducting annealers can natively simulate $H$.
- **Trotter link:** $\Delta t = \pi\hbar$ recovers one Grover iteration from $e^{-iH\Delta t/\hbar}$.
- **Failure mode:** Restart on classical failure; the algorithm is probabilistic with periodic Rabi revival.
- **Mark loss prevention:** Always cite $\Delta E = 1/\sqrt{N}$ explicitly — it is the single most-tested equation in KTU exams for this topic.
